## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*List the major design considerations to be kept in mind while designing a farm implement. Mention any two BIS standards relevant to agricultural machinery.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The major design considerations are:
1. **Functional suitability** — match the geometry to the soil, crop, and power source.
2. **Strength and rigidity** — must withstand draft, impact, and fatigue loads with an appropriate Factor of Safety (typically 2–3 for static, 3–5 for dynamic).
3. **Material and manufacturing feasibility** — choose materials that are forgeable, weldable, and repairable in rural workshops.
4. **Economic viability** — cost of ownership, life, and resale value.
5. **Ergonomics, safety, and BIS compliance** — operator comfort, shielding, transport locks.

Relevant BIS standards: **IS 419 (ploughs), IS 4460 (seed drills), IS 6813 (sprayers)**. (Any two).

> *Award 1 mark for each correct point, full marks for 3 well-explained points.*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between ductile and brittle materials. Give one example of each used in agricultural machinery.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Ductile Material | Brittle Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Plastic deformation** | Significant before fracture | Negligible |
| **Fracture type** | Cup-and-cone (fibrous) | Cleavage (granular) |
| **Energy absorbed** | High (tough) | Low (notch-sensitive) |
| **Design criterion** | Yield strength $\sigma_y$ | Ultimate strength $\sigma_{\text{ult}}$ |
| **Example in farm machinery** | Mild steel plough beam, medium carbon steel tine | Cast iron plough wheel, ceramic coating on share |

> *Award 1 mark for the definition of each, 1 mark for the comparison, 1 mark for the example pair.*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A** — *(14 Marks, internal choice to Question B)*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Set A]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **design considerations** for the **mouldboard plough** under Indian field conditions. Your answer must cover functional, structural, material, and ergonomic aspects. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A **mouldboard plough share** is subjected to a draft force of $12 \text{ kN}$ acting at a height of $250 \text{ mm}$ above the share-mounting plane. The share is made of **high-carbon steel (0.7 % C) with $\sigma_y = 550 \text{ MPa}$ and $\sigma_{\text{ult}} = 820 \text{ MPa}$**. The cross-section at the critical (mounting) location is rectangular, $60 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$, with the 60 mm dimension along the bending direction. Take a **Factor of Safety = 3**.
Determine:
(i) The maximum bending moment at the critical section.
(ii) The maximum bending stress induced.
(iii) Whether the design is safe under static loading.
(iv) One practical recommendation if the design is not safe. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Design Considerations for a Mouldboard Plough (7 Marks):**

1. **Functional Considerations:** Plough type (MB, disc, chisel) must match the soil — black cotton soil needs higher curvature (helicoidal); sandy soil needs a flatter mouldboard. Working width (225–350 mm) must be matched to tractor hp (2–3 kW per cm of width).
2. **Structural Considerations:** The plough must withstand the draft force (typically 8–15 kN at 5 km/h) and the vertical soil reaction without bending. Standard sections such as the *BIS plough beam profile* (slotted flat with reinforcing ribs) are used.
3. **Material Considerations:** Share — high-carbon steel, hardened to 55–60 HRC for abrasion resistance; Mouldboard — medium carbon steel, 0.4–0.5 % C, hardened to 40–45 HRC for wear + slide; Frog (where share & mouldboard meet) — pearlitic malleable cast iron or forged steel.
4. **Ergonomic & Safety:** Three-point hitch geometry must match the tractor; transport lock to prevent swing on roads; skimmer attachment for burial of crop residue.
5. **Standards & Economics:** Conformance to IS 419; replaceable share tip to reduce cost; manufacturing by forging + welding for economy.

> *Award 1–2 marks for each aspect depending on depth.*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Bending moment at critical section:**

$$
M = F \times h = 12 \text{ kN} \times 250 \text{ mm} = 12{,}000 \text{ N} \times 250 \text{ mm} = 3.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Section modulus** (with $b = 20 \text{ mm}$, $h = 60 \text{ mm}$):

$$
Z = \dfrac{b\,h^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{20 \times 60^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{20 \times 3600}{6} = 12{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending stress:**

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^{6}}{12{,}000} = 250 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 250 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Allowable stress (using yield strength with FoS = 3):**

$$
\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\text{FoS}} = \dfrac{550}{3} = 183.33 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$
\sigma_{\max} = 250 \text{ MPa} \; > \; \sigma_{\text{allow}} = 183.33 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Conclusion:** The design is **NOT safe** under static loading. The bending stress exceeds the allowable by ≈ 36 %.

**Step 6 — Practical recommendation:**
Either **(a)** increase the section depth to $h_{\text{new}}$ such that $Z_{\text{new}} = M / \sigma_{\text{allow}}$:

$$
Z_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^{6}}{183.33} \approx 16{,}364 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

With $b = 20 \text{ mm}$: $h_{\text{new}} = \sqrt{6 \cdot Z_{\text{new}} / b} = \sqrt{6 \times 16{,}364 / 20} \approx 70 \text{ mm}$ — i.e., upgrade to $20 \text{ mm} \times 70 \text{ mm}$; **or (b)** use a higher-grade alloy steel with $\sigma_y \geq 750 \text{ MPa}$; **or (c)** reduce the height of pull by lowering the hitch point.

> *Award 1 mark per step. Bonus 1 mark for the practical recommendation if it shows engineering judgment.*

---

#### **QUESTION B** — *(14 Marks, internal alternative to Question A)*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Set B]**

**(a)** Classify the **types of loads** acting on a tractor-mounted **disc plough** during field operation. For each load category, explain the **type of stress** it induces in the disc and name the **mechanical property** the disc material must possess to resist it. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A **rotavator PTO shaft** transmits $22 \text{ kW}$ at a rotational speed of $540 \text{ rpm}$. The shaft is made of **medium carbon alloy steel (EN-19)** with an allowable shear stress of $55 \text{ MPa}$. Determine:
(i) The torque transmitted.
(ii) The required solid shaft diameter (using the torsion formula).
(iii) The maximum shear strain at the surface, if the shear modulus of the steel is $82 \text{ GPa}$.
(iv) The twist angle in degrees per metre of shaft length. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Load-Stress-Property Matrix for a Disc Plough (7 Marks):**

| Load Category | Mechanism in Field | Induced Stress | Required Material Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Static draft load** | Steady horizontal pull by the tractor | Tensile + bending stress at the disc-hub junction | High **yield strength** ($\sigma_y$) |
| **2. Vertical soil reaction** | Weight of soil lifted + gravity on the disc | Bending (cyclic) + compressive on concave face | **Fatigue strength** |
| **3. Impact load (stones/roots)** | Sudden shock when disc strikes an obstacle | Impact + bending stress spikes | High **toughness** (Charpy impact) |
| **4. Frictional / sliding load** | Disc slides against soil with relative velocity | Surface shear + thermal from friction | High **hardness** (≥ 45 HRC) + **wear resistance** |
| **5. Rotational / centrifugal load** | Disc rotates at 540–720 rpm | Radial (tensile) stress at the rim | **Tensile strength** + **balance** to avoid vibration |
| **6. Corrosive / chemical load** | Contact with fertilizers, moisture, soil acids | Long-term surface degradation | **Corrosion resistance** (coatings, alloying) |
| **7. Torsional load (PTO-driven variants)** | Power transmitted through the disc-hub shaft | Torsional shear stress in the shaft | **Shear strength** ($\tau$) |

> *Award 1 mark per well-explained row; full marks require at least 4 rows with one sentence each on the stress and the property.*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the torque from power and speed:**

$$
P = 2\pi\,N\,T / 60 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T = \dfrac{60\,P}{2\pi\,N}
$$

With $P = 22{,}000 \text{ W}$, $N = 540 \text{ rpm}$:

$$
T = \dfrac{60 \times 22{,}000}{2\pi \times 540} = \dfrac{1{,}320{,}000}{3392.92} \approx 389.05 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Required shaft diameter (using solid round shaft, $\tau_{\max} = 16T / \pi d^{3}$):**

$$
d^{3} = \dfrac{16\,T}{\pi\,\tau_{\text{allow}}} = \dfrac{16 \times 389.05 \times 10^{3} \text{ N·mm}}{\pi \times 55 \text{ N/mm}^{2}}
$$

$$
d^{3} = \dfrac{6{,}224{,}800}{172.788} \approx 36{,}026 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

$$
d = (36{,}026)^{1/3} \approx 33.0 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt the next standard size: **$d = 35 \text{ mm}$**.

**Step 3 — Maximum shear strain at the surface** ($\gamma = \tau / G$):

$$
\gamma_{\max} = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{allow}}}{G} = \dfrac{55 \text{ MPa}}{82{,}000 \text{ MPa}} = 6.71 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}
$$

(Equivalent to $0.0384°$.)

**Step 4 — Twist angle per metre of length:**

$$
\theta = \dfrac{T}{G\,J} \quad \text{per unit length}, \quad J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \times 35^{4}}{32} \approx 147{,}340 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

$$
\theta = \dfrac{389.05 \times 10^{3} \text{ N·mm}}{82{,}000 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \times 147{,}340 \text{ mm}^{4}} = \dfrac{389{,}050}{1.2082 \times 10^{10}} \approx 3.22 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad/mm}
$$

Per metre (i.e., 1000 mm):

$$
\theta_{1\text{m}} = 3.22 \times 10^{-2} \text{ rad} \approx 1.85° \text{ per metre}
$$

**Conclusion:** The shaft of $35 \text{ mm}$ diameter safely transmits $22 \text{ kW}$ at $540 \text{ rpm}$ with a manageable twist of ≈ 1.85°/m.

> *Award 1 mark per step. Common student error: using $d^{3}$ in the wrong unit (e.g., entering 36,026 in $d^{3}$ in metres, which would give $d \approx 33 \text{ m}$!). Always confirm units before pressing the cube-root key.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of bending stress:** Do *not* write $\sigma = -M y / I$ in the final answer unless the question specifically asks for the sign. The board usually wants the *magnitude* $|\sigma| = M y / I$.
> 2. **Unit conversions:** $1 \text{ kN·m} = 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$; $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$. Mixing these up is the #1 reason for losing full marks in numericals.
> 3. **Factor of safety choice:** For *ductile* materials use $\sigma_y / \text{FoS}$; for *brittle* materials use $\sigma_{\text{ult}} / \text{FoS}$. State the criterion explicitly.
> 4. **Stress vs. strength vocabulary:** Stress is the *induced* quantity; strength is the *material* property. Many students interchange them and lose a mark.
> 5. **Mermaid diagrams in answer sheets:** In the KTU 2024 written exam you cannot draw Mermaid. Translate the block diagrams into clean *labelled block diagrams with arrows* using a pen. The Mermaid here is for your *understanding*, not for the answer sheet.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Design = F-S-M-E-S** (Function, Strength, Material, Manufacturing, Ergonomics & Safety).
- [ ] **BIS standards** to quote: IS 419 (plough), IS 4460 (seed drill), IS 6813 (sprayer).
- [ ] **Plough share material** = high-carbon steel (0.6–0.8 % C), 55–60 HRC, with carburized tip.
- [ ] **Mouldboard material** = medium carbon steel, 40–45 HRC.
- [ ] **Cultivator tine material** = medium carbon spring steel (e.g., EN-45, 55Si7, SUP-9).
- [ ] **Rotavator blade material** = medium carbon steel with induction-hardened edge; boron steel variants exist.
- [ ] **PTO shaft material** = alloy steel (e.g., EN-19, EN-24) for combined torsion + bending.
- [ ] **Sprayer boom** = aluminium alloy or FRP for low weight + corrosion resistance.
- [ ] **Load types:** static, dynamic, impact, fluctuating/cyclic, thermal, eccentric/torsional, shock.
- [ ] **Stress types:** tensile, compressive, shear, bending, torsional, combined.
- [ ] **Master formula #1:** $\sigma = M / Z$ — for beam design (always state units).
- [ ] **Master formula #2:** $\tau = 16T / (\pi d^{3})$ — for solid round shaft design.
- [ ] **Master formula #3:** $\text{FoS} = \sigma_y / \sigma_{\text{work}}$ — for ductile material strength check.
- [ ] **Master formula #4:** $\delta = P L^{3} / (3 E I)$ — for cantilever tip deflection.
- [ ] **Master formula #5:** $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ — for converting between $E$ and $G$ (use $\nu = 0.3$ for steel).
- [ ] **Hardness tests:** Brinell (BHN) for cast/forge; Rockwell C (HRC) for hardened steel; Rockwell B (HRB) for soft steel; Vickers (HV) for surface treatments and thin sections.
- [ ] **Stress-strain curve landmarks:** Proportional limit → Elastic limit → Upper yield → Lower yield → UTS → Necking → Fracture.
- [ ] **Area under stress-strain curve = toughness.**
- [ ] **Slope of elastic region = Young's modulus $E$.**
- [ ] **For brittle materials:** design against UTS, not yield.
- [ ] **For cyclic loading:** design against fatigue strength, not static strength.
- [ ] **Always state the FoS explicitly in every numerical answer** (typical values: 2–3 for static, 3–5 for impact/cyclic).
- [ ] **Always convert units before substituting into formulas** — most-mark-loss culprit in numericals.
- [ ] **For corrosion-prone environments (fertilizers, wet paddy), prefer coated, galvanized, or stainless components.**
- [ ] **For weight-critical applications (sprayer booms, drone-mounted implements), prefer aluminium alloys or FRP composites.**
- [ ] **For high-impact applications (rotavator blades, cultivator tines), prefer tough spring steels over brittle cast irons.**
- [ ] **Final engineering verdict for any design problem:** Compare $\sigma_{\max}$ (or $\tau_{\max}$, $\delta_{\max}$) with $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$ (or $\tau_{\text{allow}}$, $\delta_{\text{limit}}$); verdict is "Safe" only if induced < allowable by a positive margin.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Theories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT604 - DESIGN OF AGRICULTURAL IMPLEMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Failure Theories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Theory** is a set of mathematical hypotheses or criteria that predict the conditions under which a material loaded under a complex multi-axial stress state will fail (yield or fracture). These theories allow the designer to replace a complex 3-D stress state with an **equivalent uniaxial stress** (or equivalent stress) that can be compared directly with the material property obtained from a simple tensile test.

In the context of **Design of Agricultural Implements & Machines**, failure theories are essential because almost every component — from a **plough share**, **disc blade**, **rotavator tine**, **combine harvester cutter bar**, to the **PTO shaft** of a tractor — experiences **combined loading** (bending + torsion + axial + shear + impact). The designer must guarantee that the component does not yield permanently, fracture catastrophically, or fail by fatigue under field-service loads.

### 1.2 The Six Classical Failure Theories

| No. | Theory Name | Proposed By | Type |
|-----|-------------|-------------|------|
| 1 | Maximum Normal Stress Theory | W. J. M. Rankine (1858) | Brittle / Rupture |
| 2 | Maximum Normal Strain Theory | Saint-Venant | Brittle |
| 3 | Maximum Shear Stress Theory | H. Tresca (1864) | Ductile |
| 4 | Maximum Distortion Energy Theory | R. von Mises (1913) | Ductile |
| 5 | Maximum Strain Energy Theory | E. Haigh (1924) | Ductile |
| 6 | Coulomb-Mohr / Internal Friction Theory | C. A. Coulomb (1773) | Brittle / Soils |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Squeeze Test"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (The Watermelon Test):** Imagine you press your thumbs into a watermelon. The melon fails not because of the normal (pushing) stress, but because the **internal shear** along oblique planes tears the flesh apart. Ductile metals (steel plough beams) fail in shear, while brittle materials (cast iron plough-point blanks, soil aggregates) fail by **splitting** along the maximum tensile plane. Failure theories are the formal mathematical translations of these two distinct physical observations.

### 1.4 Why Failure Theories Matter in Agricultural Engineering

Agricultural machinery operates in **highly stochastic, harsh, and corrosive environments** (soil abrasion, crop residue impact, moisture, dust). The principal design requirement per KTU Module 1 is:

$$n = \frac{\text{Strength (Failure Criterion)}}{\text{Applied Stress}} \geq n_{designed}$$

where the **numerator** is supplied by the failure theory, and $n$ is the **factor of safety** — typically **1.5 to 3.0** for tillage tools, **2.0 to 4.0** for high-speed rotary components.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Stress Tensor

For any 3-D stress state characterized by principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ (with $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$), the total stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$ can be split into two physically meaningful parts:

**a) Hydrostatic (Volumetric) Component** — causes volume change only:

$$\sigma_{ij}^{H} = \sigma_m \, \delta_{ij} \quad \text{where} \quad \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3}{3}$$

**b) Deviatoric (Distortion) Component** — causes shape change only:

$$\sigma_{ij}^{D} = \sigma_{ij} - \sigma_m \delta_{ij}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept — Ductile vs. Brittle Distinction:**
> - **Ductile materials** (mild steel, aluminium alloys used in tractor chassis, plough beams) yield by **shape distortion** (slip on 45° planes). Theories (3), (4) and (5) predict this.
> - **Brittle materials** (cast iron, hardened tillage tips, soil/rock) fail by **fracture** due to **maximum tensile normal stress**. Theories (1), (2) and (6) apply.

### 2.2 The Six Failure Criteria — Derivation Logic

**Theory 1: Maximum Normal Stress (Rankine)**

Failure occurs when the **largest principal stress** reaches the tensile strength $\sigma_t$ (or the smallest reaches $-\sigma_c$ for compression):

$$\sigma_1 \leq \frac{\sigma_t}{n} \quad \text{and} \quad \vert \sigma_3 \vert \leq \frac{\sigma_c}{n}$$

- **How:** Compares the principal stresses to uniaxial test results.
- **Why it works for brittle:** Brittle fracture initiates at the plane of maximum tension, normal to that plane.
- **Limitation:** Ignores the influence of intermediate principal stress $\sigma_2$ and shear stresses entirely.

**Theory 2: Maximum Normal Strain**

$$\varepsilon_1 \leq \frac{\varepsilon_t}{n} \quad \text{where} \quad \varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2+\sigma_3)\right]$$

- Includes Poisson effect, but **over-predicts failure** in some ductile cases.

**Theory 3: Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca)**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{2n} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$

- **How:** Failure occurs when the **maximum shear** in the stressed element reaches the shear yield stress of the material in a simple tension test.
- **Why for ductile:** Yielding in ductile metals initiates on planes of maximum shear (≈ 45° to the load axis in tension).
- **KTU favorite** — simplest, most conservative ductile criterion.

**Theory 4: Maximum Distortion Energy (von Mises)**

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}} \leq \frac{S_y}{n}$$

- **How:** Failure occurs when the **distortion (shape-change) energy per unit volume** equals that at yield in a simple tension test.
- **Most accurate** ductile criterion; matches experimental data within 10–15 % for most engineering metals.
- Used in virtually all FEA packages (ANSYS, ABAQUS) as the default yield model.

**Theory 5: Maximum Strain Energy (Haigh)**

$$U \leq \frac{U_y}{n} \quad \text{where} \quad U = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2-2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

- Sets **total strain energy density** (volumetric + distortional) equal to that at uniaxial yield.
- **Less accurate** than von Mises for ductile materials because it includes volumetric energy, which does not cause yielding in metals.

**Theory 6: Coulomb-Mohr Theory (for Soils and Brittle Materials)**

$$\frac{\sigma_1}{S_{ut}} - \frac{\sigma_3}{S_{uc}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

where $S_{ut}$ = ultimate tensile strength and $S_{uc}$ = ultimate compressive strength.

- **Highly relevant** to soil-mechanics and tillage-tool design (the **earth-pressure** analysis uses this).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD — Memorize this consolidated table. ALL row separators use the standard pipe. For absolute-value bars in equations, use $\vert x \vert$, NOT the literal `|` character, to preserve table syntax.**

| # | Failure Theory | Governing Equation | Equivalent Stress $\sigma_{eq}$ | Material Class | Safety Factor Eq. |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rankine (Max Normal Stress) | $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_t$ | $\sigma_{eq}=\sigma_1$ | Brittle | $n=\dfrac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_1}$ |
| 2 | Saint-Venant (Max Normal Strain) | $\varepsilon_1 \leq \varepsilon_y$ | $\sigma_{eq}=\sigma_1-\nu(\sigma_2+\sigma_3)$ | Brittle | $n=\dfrac{S_y}{\sigma_1-\nu(\sigma_2+\sigma_3)}$ |
| 3 | Tresca (Max Shear Stress) | $\sigma_1-\sigma_3 \leq S_y$ | $\sigma_{eq}=\sigma_1-\sigma_3$ | Ductile | $n=\dfrac{S_y}{\sigma_1-\sigma_3}$ |
| 4 | von Mises (Distortion Energy) | $\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]} \leq S_y$ | $\sigma_{VM}$ as above | Ductile | $n=\dfrac{S_y}{\sigma_{VM}}$ |
| 5 | Haigh (Max Strain Energy) | $\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2-2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1) \leq S_y^2$ | $\sigma_{eq}=\sqrt{\text{LHS}}$ | Ductile (less accurate) | $n=\dfrac{S_y}{\sigma_{eq}}$ |
| 6 | Coulomb-Mohr | $\dfrac{\sigma_1}{S_{ut}}-\dfrac{\sigma_3}{S_{uc}} \leq \dfrac{1}{n}$ | — | Brittle / Soils | Direct as given |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Agricultural Implement Design

| Component | Dominant Loading | Recommended Theory |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|
| Mould-board plough share | Bending + Impact + Soil Abrasion | Tresca + Coulomb-Mohr (soil side) |
| Rotavator blade (L-shaped) | Centrifugal + Impact + Soil reaction | von Mises (metal) + Mohr-Coulomb (interface) |
| Disc harrow (concave disc) | Bending + Torsion | von Mises |
| Combine harvester cutter bar | High-frequency impact + Shear | Tresca (shear) + Fatigue (Goodman/GER) |
| PTO shaft | Torsion + Bending | von Mises (combined shaft) |
| Cultivator tine (rigid) | Bending + Soil draft force | Tresca (conservative ductile check) |
| Soil aggregates / Furrow slice | Compression + Shear | Mohr-Coulomb |
| Cast-iron plough point blank | Impact + Bending | Rankine (brittle check) |

### 2.5 Comparative Insights for KTU Viva

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of Conservativeness (Most → Least Conservative for Ductile Materials):**
> $$\text{Tresca} \; > \; \text{von Mises} \; > \; \text{Haigh (strain energy)} \; > \; \text{Saint-Venant}$$
> Tresca and von Mises coincide when $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3$ or $\sigma_2 = \tfrac{\sigma_1+\sigma_3}{2}$. The **deviation between Tresca and von Mises is at most 13.4 %** (occurs at $\sigma_2 = 0$, pure shear-equivalent condition).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the von Mises Criterion from Distortion Energy

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 1 and is expected in a 14-mark question. We derive it from first principles using strain energy decomposition.

**Step 1 — Start with the Generalised Hooke's Law in 3-D**

For an isotropic, linearly elastic material under principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2+\sigma_3)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1+\sigma_3)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_3 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_3 - \nu(\sigma_1+\sigma_2)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Total Strain Energy Density $U$**

The work done per unit volume (strain energy density) for a linear-elastic material is:

$$U = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\sigma_1 \varepsilon_1 + \sigma_2 \varepsilon_2 + \sigma_3 \varepsilon_3\right)$$

Substituting the strain expressions:

$$U = \frac{1}{2E}\left\{\sigma_1\left[\sigma_1-\nu(\sigma_2+\sigma_3)\right] + \sigma_2\left[\sigma_2-\nu(\sigma_1+\sigma_3)\right] + \sigma_3\left[\sigma_3-\nu(\sigma_1+\sigma_2)\right]\right\}$$

Expanding the bracket term by term:

$$U = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

This is the **Total Strain Energy Density** (Haigh's criterion uses this directly).

**Step 3 — Hydrostatic (Volumetric) Strain Energy Density $U_v$**

The hydrostatic stress is $\sigma_m = \tfrac{\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3}{3}$. Under pure hydrostatic stress, the volumetric strain is:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}\cdot 3\sigma_m = \frac{3(1-2\nu)\sigma_m}{E}$$

Therefore the volumetric strain energy is:

$$U_v = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\sigma_m \cdot \varepsilon_v = \frac{3(1-2\nu)\sigma_m^2}{2E} = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{6E}(\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3)^2$$

Expanding $(\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3)^2$:

$$U_v = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{6E}\left[\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2 + 2(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Distortion Strain Energy Density $U_d$ (subtraction)**

$$U_d = U - U_v$$

$$U_d = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)\right] - \frac{(1-2\nu)}{6E}\left[\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2 + 2(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic Simplification**

To simplify, group the coefficients of $\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2$ and $(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)$:

Coefficient of $(\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2)$:

$$a = \frac{1}{2E} - \frac{(1-2\nu)}{6E} = \frac{3 - (1-2\nu)}{6E} = \frac{2+2\nu}{6E} = \frac{1+\nu}{3E}$$

Coefficient of $(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)$:

$$b = -\frac{\nu}{E} - \frac{2(1-2\nu)}{6E} = -\frac{\nu}{E} - \frac{(1-2\nu)}{3E} = \frac{-3\nu - (1-2\nu)}{3E} = \frac{-(1+\nu)}{3E}$$

Therefore:

$$U_d = \frac{1+\nu}{3E}\left[(\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2) - (\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

**Step 6 — Recognise the Identity**

Using the algebraic identity:

$$(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2\left[(\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2) - (\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

Hence the final compact form:

$$\boxed{U_d = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]}$$

**Step 7 — Calibrate Against a Simple Tension Test**

In a simple tension test ($\sigma_1 = S_y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$):

$$U_{d,\text{yield}} = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(S_y-0)^2+(0-0)^2+(0-S_y)^2\right] = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[2S_y^2\right] = \frac{(1+\nu)S_y^2}{3E}$$

**Step 8 — Equate $U_d$ at Failure to $U_{d,\text{yield}}$ (Failure Hypothesis)**

By the **Distortion Energy Hypothesis**, failure is predicted when $U_d = U_{d,\text{yield}}$:

$$\frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] = \frac{(1+\nu)S_y^2}{3E}$$

Cancel $\frac{1+\nu}{E}$ from both sides:

$$(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2S_y^2$$

Dividing by 2 and taking the square root:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}} \leq S_y}$$

This is the **von Mises equivalent stress** — the most widely used ductile yield criterion in modern design.

### 3.2 Derivation of Octahedral Shear Stress Form

A second elegant form of the von Mises criterion uses the **octahedral plane** (the plane whose normal makes equal angles with the three principal axes). The octahedral shear stress is defined as:

$$\tau_{oct} = \frac{1}{3}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}$$

The von Mises criterion in terms of $\tau_{oct}$ becomes:

$$\tau_{oct} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{3}\,S_y$$

**Proof using direction cosines:** The unit normal to the octahedral plane is $\hat{n} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,1,1)$. The shear-stress transformation is:

$$\tau_{oct}^2 = \tfrac{1}{3}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]$$

Substituting into the von Mises yield condition $(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2S_y^2$:

$$3\tau_{oct}^2 = 2S_y^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \tau_{oct} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}}\,S_y = 0.8165\,S_y$$

This proves the result.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Tractor PTO Shaft Under Combined Loading

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A tractor PTO shaft is subjected to a bending moment $M = 800$ N·m, a torque $T = 600$ N·m, and an axial tensile force $F = 20$ kN. The shaft diameter $d = 40$ mm. The material is AISI 1045 steel with $S_y = 380$ MPa. Calculate the factor of safety using **(a) Tresca** and **(b) von Mises** criteria.

**Solution — Step 1: Stresses on the Outer Surface**

Section modulus (bending): $Z = \tfrac{\pi d^3}{32} = \tfrac{\pi (40)^3}{32} = 62\,832 \text{ mm}^3$

Polar section modulus (torsion): $Z_p = \tfrac{\pi d^3}{16} = 125\,664 \text{ mm}^3$

Cross-sectional area: $A = \tfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = 1257 \text{ mm}^2$

Bending stress: $\sigma_b = \tfrac{M}{Z} = \tfrac{800 \times 10^3}{62\,832} = 12.73$ MPa

Torsional shear stress: $\tau = \tfrac{T}{Z_p} = \tfrac{600 \times 10^3}{125\,664} = 4.77$ MPa

Axial stress: $\sigma_a = \tfrac{F}{A} = \tfrac{20\,000}{1257} = 15.92$ MPa

**Step 2: Principal Stresses (using $\sigma_x = \sigma_a + \sigma_b$, $\sigma_y = 0$, $\tau_{xy} = \tau$)**

$$\sigma_x = 12.73 + 15.92 = 28.65 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_y = 0, \quad \tau_{xy} = 4.77 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 14.325 \pm \sqrt{(14.325)^2 + (4.77)^2} = 14.325 \pm \sqrt{205.20 + 22.75} = 14.325 \pm 15.11$$

$$\sigma_1 = 29.43 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 0 \text{ (plane stress)}, \quad \sigma_3 = -0.79 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 (a): Tresca Factor of Safety**

$$n_{Tresca} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{380}{29.43 - (-0.79)} = \frac{380}{30.22} = 12.57$$

**Step 3 (b): von Mises Factor of Safety**

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(29.43)^2+(0.79)^2+(30.22)^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{865.82 + 0.62 + 913.25}{2}} = \sqrt{889.85} = 29.83 \text{ MPa}$$

$$n_{VM} = \frac{380}{29.83} = 12.74$$

**Conclusion:** Both criteria indicate the shaft is heavily over-designed for steady-state loads — but the KTU **expected design** answer would account for fatigue using a torque-transmission cycle, which would reduce $n$ significantly.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Theory Selection Logic

| Loading Type | Material | Hydrostatic Sensitive? | Recommended Theory |
|--------------|----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Pure tension/compression | Brittle | Yes (asymmetric) | Rankine |
| Pure shear | Ductile | No | Tresca / von Mises |
| Torsion of shaft | Ductile | No | von Mises |
| Pressure vessel (thin) | Ductile | Yes (mild) | von Mises |
| Soil aggregate | Granular | Yes | Mohr-Coulomb |
| Cast iron gear tooth | Brittle | Yes | Rankine / Coulomb-Mohr |
| Welded joint in plough frame | Ductile | No | von Mises (with fatigue) |
| Static 3-D hydrostatic | Ductile | N/A | No failure (metals) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Failure Theory Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Known stress state sigma1, sigma2, sigma3] --> nodeB{Material Type?}
    nodeB -- Ductile --> nodeC{Loading Type?}
    nodeB -- Brittle --> nodeD{Mohr Circle Available?}
    nodeB -- Granular Soil --> nodeE[Use Mohr-Coulomb Theory]
    nodeC -- Static --> nodeF{Accuracy Required?}
    nodeC -- Cyclic Fatigue --> nodeG[Use Goodman or Soderberg on von Mises]
    nodeF -- Quick Conservative --> nodeH[Tresca: sigma1 - sigma3 = Sy / n]
    nodeF -- FEA / Design Code --> nodeI[von Mises Distortion Energy]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeJ[Coulomb-Mohr or Modified Mohr]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeK[Rankine: max principal stress]
    nodeH --> nodeL[Compute Factor of Safety n]
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeG --> nodeL
    nodeE --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM{Compare n with n_design}
    nodeM -- n greater than n_design --> nodeN[Safe Design]
    nodeM -- n less than n_design --> nodeO[REDESIGN: increase section or change material]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Decomposition of Stress State into Hydrostatic and Deviatoric Parts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Applied 3D Stress State sigma_ij] --> nodeB[Hydrostatic Component sigma_m delta_ij]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Deviatoric Component s_ij]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Volume Change only]
    nodeB --> nodeE[No yielding in metals]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Shape Distortion]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Causes Yielding in Ductile Materials]
    nodeF --> nodeH[von Mises Criterion uses s_ij magnitude]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Yield Check: sigma_VM greater than S_y]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Hydrostatic = Safe in ductile metals]
    nodeE --> nodeJ
```

### 4.3 Comparative Envelope Diagram (2-D Representation in sigma1-sigma3 Plane)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Yield Envelope in sigma1 - sigma3 Plane with sigma2 = 0] --> nodeB[Tresca Hexagon: Vertices at Sy, 0, -Sy]
    nodeA --> nodeC[von Mises Ellipse: Inscribed in Tresca hexagon]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Rankine Square: Sides at sigma1 = Sy and sigma3 = -Sy]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Coulomb-Mohr: Asymmetric hexagon in tension vs compression]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Most Conservative Ductile]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Best-fit Ductile]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Brittle: Square Envelope]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Soils and Brittle with different S_ut and S_uc]
```

### 4.4 Functional Flow — Application of Tresca vs von Mises in Design Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step 1: Compute Principal Stresses from FEA or analytical formula] --> nodeB[Step 2: Identify Material: Sy, Sut, Suc]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3A: Tresca Equivalent sigma_eq = sigma1 - sigma3]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Step 3B: von Mises Equivalent sigma_VM = sqrt 0.5 sum of squared differences]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Step 4: Compute n = Sy / sigma_eq]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Step 4: Compute n = Sy / sigma_VM]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Step 5: Compare n with n_design]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step 6: Document Result and Select Material / Geometry]
```

### 4.5 Mohr Circle Representation of the Three Principal Stresses

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Mohrs Circle for 3D Stress] --> nodeB[Outer Circle: sigma1 to sigma3]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Inner Circle: sigma1 to sigma2]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Inner Circle 2: sigma2 to sigma3]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Maximum Shear = sigma1 - sigma3 divided by 2]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Radius tau1 = sigma1 - sigma2 divided by 2]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Radius tau2 = sigma2 - sigma3 divided by 2]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Tresca uses this radius]
    nodeF --> nodeI[von Mises uses weighted combination of all three]
    nodeG --> nodeI
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**State and explain the Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca) for failure of ductile materials.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> According to Tresca's theory, failure (yielding) occurs when the **maximum shear stress** in a complex stress state equals the maximum shear stress at yield in a simple tension test on the same material. The criterion is:
> $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{S_y}{n}$$
> where $\sigma_1, \sigma_3$ are the algebraically largest and smallest principal stresses, $S_y$ is the uniaxial yield strength, and $n$ is the factor of safety. It assumes that yielding in ductile metals initiates on planes of maximum shear, typically at 45° to the load axis in uniaxial tension. The theory is **independent of the intermediate principal stress $\sigma_2$** and is therefore **conservative** — its predictions are slightly more conservative (by up to 13.4 %) than von Mises, but it is simpler to apply. It is most suitable for **quick, hand-calculation ductile checks** in agricultural components like plough beams and cultivator tines.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Distinguish clearly between the Maximum Normal Stress Theory and the Maximum Distortion Energy Theory with reference to their applicability.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Aspect | Max Normal Stress (Rankine) | Max Distortion Energy (von Mises) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Governing quantity | Largest principal stress $\sigma_1$ | Distortion strain energy $U_d$ |
> | Material type | **Brittle** (cast iron, soil) | **Ductile** (mild steel, aluminium) |
> | Failure mode predicted | Tensile fracture | Yielding by shape change |
> | Considers $\sigma_2$? | **No** | **Yes** |
> | Equivalent stress | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1$ | $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2]}$ |
> | Application | Cast plough point, soil aggregates | Tractor frame, rotavator shaft, tillage tines |
> Rankine ignores the shearing and intermediate stress effects, making it unsafe for ductile materials. Von Mises accounts for the full energy associated with shape change and is the most accurate ductile criterion in modern codes (FEA default).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the **von Mises yield criterion** for ductile materials starting from the total strain energy density expression. Clearly show the decomposition into volumetric and distortional parts, and the calibration against a simple tension test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **rotavator blade** made of medium-carbon steel ($S_y = 420$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$) is subjected, at a critical point, to a stress state: $\sigma_x = 180$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 60$ MPa, $\sigma_z = 0$ MPa, and shear stresses $\tau_{xy} = 75$ MPa, $\tau_{yz} = 30$ MPa, $\tau_{zx} = 0$ MPa. Determine the **factor of safety** using **(i) Tresca** and **(ii) von Mises** criteria. State which theory predicts a more conservative result. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Solution to (a) — Derivation of von Mises Criterion (7 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Allocation Guide (Examiner's Key):**
> - Step 1: Hooke's Law in principal coordinates — 1 mark
> - Step 2: Total strain energy density $U$ — 1 mark
> - Step 3: Volumetric component $U_v$ — 1 mark
> - Step 4: Deviatoric component $U_d = U - U_v$ — 1 mark
> - Step 5: Algebraic simplification to compact form — 1 mark
> - Step 6: Calibration with simple tension and final $\sigma_{VM}$ — 1 mark
> - Step 7: Equivalent stress statement and factor-of-safety form — 1 mark

**Step 1 — Hooke's Law:**

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2+\sigma_3)\right]$$

(and cyclic permutations for $\varepsilon_2, \varepsilon_3$).

**Step 2 — Total strain energy density:**

$$U = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{3}\sigma_i\varepsilon_i = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2-2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric (hydrostatic) energy density** with $\sigma_m = \tfrac{\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3}{3}$:

$$U_v = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{6E}(\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3)^2$$

**Step 4 — Deviatoric (distortion) energy density** $U_d = U - U_v$:

After expanding and simplifying (as shown exhaustively in Section 3.1):

$$U_d = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]$$

**Step 5 — Calibration** against simple tension at yield ($\sigma_1 = S_y, \sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$):

$$U_{d,y} = \frac{(1+\nu)S_y^2}{3E}$$

**Step 6 — Equating** $U_d = U_{d,y}$ and simplifying:

$$(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2S_y^2$$

**Step 7 — Final von Mises equivalent stress:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}}}$$

Factor of safety: $n_{VM} = \dfrac{S_y}{\sigma_{VM}}$.

---

**Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Form the stress tensor and find principal stresses:**

$$\sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 180 & 75 & 0 \\ 75 & 60 & 30 \\ 0 & 30 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MPa}$$

The principal stresses are the eigenvalues of this matrix. Using the **characteristic equation**:

$$\det(\sigma - \lambda I) = 0$$

Expanding the determinant (using cofactor expansion or a numerical solver) yields the principal stresses:

$$\sigma_1 = 219.42 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 39.74 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 = -19.16 \text{ MPa}$$

(Verification: $\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3 = 180+60+0 = 240$ MPa; sum of eigenvalues = trace = 240 MPa. ✓)

**Step 2 (i) — Tresca Factor of Safety:**

$$n_{Tresca} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{420}{219.42 - (-19.16)} = \frac{420}{238.58} = 1.76$$

**Step 2 (ii) — von Mises Factor of Safety:**

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(219.42-39.74)^2+(39.74-(-19.16))^2+((-19.16)-219.42)^2}{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(179.68)^2+(58.90)^2+(238.58)^2}{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{32\,284.9+3\,469.2+56\,920.4}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{92\,674.5}{2}} = \sqrt{46\,337.3} = 215.26 \text{ MPa}$$

$$n_{VM} = \frac{420}{215.26} = 1.95$$

**Conclusion:** **Tresca is more conservative** ($n_{Tresca} = 1.76 < n_{VM} = 1.95$). If the design requires $n_{design} = 1.8$, the rotavator blade **fails the Tresca check** but **passes the von Mises check**, illustrating the practical engineering trade-off between conservatism and accuracy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students compute only $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ without first finding the **three principal stresses correctly** from the full 3×3 stress matrix. The 3-D problem is **not a plane-stress problem** here because $\tau_{yz} = 30$ MPa is non-zero — you MUST solve the cubic characteristic equation or use software. Partial credit is given for correct setup of the stress matrix (1 mark) and the cubic equation (1 mark) even if the roots are miscalculated.

---

#### **Question B — (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** and the **Coulomb-Mohr Theory** for failure of materials. Compare their applicability to **ductile metals** and **brittle/granular materials**, citing two agricultural-engineering examples for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **mould-board plough share** is made of high-carbon steel ($S_y = 540$ MPa, $S_{ut} = 820$ MPa) and is subjected, at a critical section, to the principal stresses $\sigma_1 = 320$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = -80$ MPa, $\sigma_3 = -180$ MPa. Compute the **factor of safety** using **(i) Maximum Shear Stress Theory**, **(ii) Maximum Normal Stress Theory**, and **(iii) Distortion Energy Theory**. Comment on which theory is most appropriate for this ductile steel share. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Solution to (a) — Theory Comparison (7 marks):**

> **Mark Allocation Guide:**
> - Tresca explanation, formula, basis — 1.5 marks
> - Coulomb-Mohr explanation, formula, basis — 1.5 marks
> - Comparison table — 1.5 marks
> - Two examples each from agricultural engineering — 1 mark each set — 2.5 marks

**Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca, 1864):**

Yielding occurs when the maximum shear stress in the loaded element reaches the maximum shear stress at yield in a simple tension test. Mathematically:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{S_y}{n} \quad \text{or} \quad \tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1-\sigma_3}{2} = \frac{S_y}{2n}$$

**Basis:** Slip along 45° planes is the microscopic mechanism of yielding in ductile metals (movement of dislocations on close-packed planes). The theory is simple, conservative, and independent of $\sigma_2$.

**Coulomb-Mohr Theory (1773 / 1900):**

A two-parameter theory that explicitly distinguishes tensile and compressive strength. The Mohr's circle at failure touches a straight envelope line:

$$\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$$

For combined loading, the failure criterion is:

$$\frac{\sigma_1}{S_{ut}} - \frac{\sigma_3}{S_{uc}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Basis:** Suitable for materials with **different tensile and compressive strengths** — granular soils, rock, concrete, cast iron.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Tresca | Coulomb-Mohr |
|---|---|---|
| Number of strength parameters | 1 ($S_y$) | 2 ($S_{ut}, S_{uc}$) |
| Material type | Ductile metals | Brittle / Soils / Rock |
| Tensile–compressive symmetry | Assumed equal | Distinguished |
| Geometric shape of envelope | Regular hexagon in $\pi$-plane | Asymmetric hexagon |
| Conservatism | Conservative for ductile | Accurate for frictional materials |
| Hydrostatic sensitivity | Insensitive | Sensitive (frictional term) |

**Agricultural Examples:**

- **Tresca (Ductile):** Tractor **PTO shaft** under combined torsion and bending; **rotavator tine** under bending impact.
- **Coulomb-Mohr (Brittle/Granular):** **Soil aggregate** failure under tillage tool; **cast iron gear tooth** of an obsolete thresher.

---

**Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $\sigma_1 = 320$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = -80$ MPa, $\sigma_3 = -180$ MPa, $S_y = 540$ MPa, $S_{ut} = 820$ MPa.

**Method (i) — Maximum Shear Stress Theory:**

$$n_{Tresca} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{540}{320 - (-180)} = \frac{540}{500} = 1.08$$

**Method (ii) — Maximum Normal Stress Theory:**

$$n_{Rankine} = \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{820}{320} = 2.5625 \approx 2.56$$

(Since the material is steel and ductile, this theory is **physically inappropriate**, but the question demands its computation.)

**Method (iii) — Distortion Energy Theory:**

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(320-(-80))^2+((-80)-(-180))^2+((-180)-320)^2}{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(400)^2+(100)^2+(-500)^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{160\,000+10\,000+250\,000}{2}} = \sqrt{210\,000} = 458.26 \text{ MPa}$$

$$n_{VM} = \frac{540}{458.26} = 1.178$$

**Comment on Most Appropriate Theory:**

Since the mould-board plough share is made of **high-carbon steel** (a **ductile** material that yields before fracture), the **Distortion Energy (von Mises) Theory** is most appropriate. It provides the most accurate factor of safety for ductile metals under multi-axial loading. The **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine)** is **inappropriate** because it greatly over-predicts safety (n = 2.56) for a ductile material where shear-based yielding is the actual failure mode. The **Tresca value (n = 1.08)** is conservative and acceptable for **quick design checks**, but the **von Mises value (n = 1.18)** is closer to experimental reality.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. In Tresca's criterion, **always subtract the smallest principal stress from the largest**, including sign. Students often write $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ — this is WRONG unless $\sigma_3 = \sigma_2$. (1 mark penalty for each wrong application.)
> 2. In the von Mises calculation, the **difference terms inside the square** must use **consistent sign** (numerical subtraction including negative signs). Skipping the $\sigma_2$ term entirely costs **3 marks**.
> 3. For the comparison part (a), students often confuse **Coulomb-Mohr** with **Mohr's circle**. Mohr's circle is a *graphical representation* of stress, NOT a failure theory. Coulomb-Mohr is a failure envelope. Conflating them loses **2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LAST-MINUTE REVISION BULLETS — Pin these to your memory.**

- **Ductile materials → use von Mises (or Tresca for conservative quick check).**
- **Brittle materials → use Rankine (Max Normal Stress) or Coulomb-Mohr.**
- **Granular soils → use Mohr-Coulomb with cohesion $c$ and friction angle $\phi$.**
- **Hydrostatic stress alone cannot cause yielding in ductile metals** (no shape change → no slip).
- **Volumetric (hydrostatic) energy** does NOT contribute to yielding; only **distortional energy** does.
- **Tresca is the most conservative** ductile theory; **deviation from von Mises ≤ 13.4 %**.
- **Tresca coincides with von Mises** when $\sigma_2 = 0$ (pure shear-equivalent) or $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2$ or $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3$.
- **Octahedral plane** is the plane that makes equal angles with all three principal axes; the **octahedral shear stress** is the basis of von Mises' alternative form: $\tau_{oct} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}}\,S_y$ at yield.
- **Safety factor formula is universal:** $n = \dfrac{\text{Strength parameter}}{\sigma_{eq}}$.
- **Cast iron plough points → brittle → Rankine / Coulomb-Mohr.**
- **Steel plough beams, tractor chassis, rotavator blades, PTO shafts → ductile → von Mises.**
- **Soil failure in tillage → Mohr-Coulomb (cohesion + internal friction).**
- **Tractor PTO shafts typically fail in torsion + bending** — use the **combined-shaft formula** based on von Mises.
- **High-cycle fatigue combines with failure theory** via Goodman/Soderberg/Gerber lines on the equivalent alternating stress.
- **For pure shear loading:** $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{3}\,\tau$ (very useful for **rotavator shear bolts** and **cutter-bar drives**).
- **For pure torsion of a circular shaft:** $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{3}\,\dfrac{T \cdot c}{J}$, while $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T \cdot c}{J}$ — so **von Mises gives $\sqrt{3}$ times the Tresca value** under pure torsion.
- **Order of conservatism (most → least):** Tresca → von Mises → Haigh (Strain Energy) → Saint-Venant.
- **The 13.4 % difference** between Tresca and von Mises occurs at the **biaxial stress state** $\sigma_1 = -\sigma_3$ with $\sigma_2 = 0$.
- **Maximum Strain Energy (Haigh)** is rarely used because it incorrectly includes hydrostatic energy as a contributor to yielding.

**End of Module 1 — Failure Theory Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT604 - DESIGN OF AGRICULTURAL IMPLEMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factor of Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Factor of Safety (FoS / N):** The ratio of the **maximum stress** (or **load**) that a machine component can withstand (without failure) to the **maximum allowable stress** (or **working/design stress/load**) that is actually imposed on it during service.

Mathematically, the Factor of Safety is expressed as:

$$N = \frac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}} = \frac{\sigma_{max\,allowable}}{\sigma_{applied}} = \frac{W_{failure}}{W_{service}}$$

In the design of agricultural implements, the **Factor of Safety** is a *design margin* intentionally built into every load-bearing member (e.g., plough beams, tynes, rotary shafts, harrow discs, tool bars) to account for **uncertainties** in:
- Magnitude and nature of field loads (impact, vibration, soil resistance)
- Material property scatter (cast iron, mild steel, high-carbon steel)
- Manufacturing defects and wear
- Environmental degradation (corrosion, dust, moisture)
- Service life and fatigue cycling

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the use of **Allowable Stress Design (ASD)** methodology, where the calculated working stress (induced stress) must always remain **below the material's allowable stress limit**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Bridge & the Elephant" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are an engineer asked to design a rope to lift an **elephant** weighing 5,000 kg. You are not 100% sure how heavy the elephant is, the rope material is slightly variable, the elephant jerks suddenly, and rain weakens the rope over time.
>
> If the rope's breaking strength is 10,000 kg, you would not use the rope at its limit (i.e., FoS = 1). You would say: *"I will only ever load this rope up to 2,500 kg."* This gives a Factor of Safety of **N = 10,000 / 2,500 = 4**.
>
> **In farm machinery:** The "elephant" is the **draft force** of a plough, the **torque** on a rotary tiller shaft, or the **bending load** on a cultivator tyne. The "rope" is the steel section, the spring, or the weld. We design it to never operate near its breaking point.

## 1.3 Standard Range in Agricultural Implement Design

| Component Type | Typical FoS (N) | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural frames & beams** | **2.0 – 3.0** | Static + impact loads from soil |
| **Rotating shafts (PTO, rotor)** | **1.5 – 2.5** | Fatigue and dynamic loading |
| **Springs (seat, suspension)** | **1.2 – 1.5** | Material uniformity is high |
| **Chains, belts, ropes** | **3.0 – 5.0** | Wear, ageing, shock loads |
| **Bolts, rivets, welds** | **2.5 – 4.0** | Stress concentration zones |
| **Cast iron components** | **4.0 – 6.0** | Brittle, casting defects |
| **Brittle materials (CI, stone)** | **6.0 – 10.0** | No yield region |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always justify the FoS chosen. Mention the **type of load** (static/dynamic), **material ductility**, **failure mode** (yield/fatigue/brittle), and **consequences of failure** in your answer.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Concept Decomposition — "Why" and "How"

The Factor of Safety is **not a single number** — it is a *design philosophy* with multiple formulations depending on the dominant **failure mode**. The KTU 2024 Module-1 syllabus specifically asks students to distinguish between:

### A. Factor of Safety Based on Yielding
Used when the material is **ductile** and failure is defined as **onset of plastic deformation** (e.g., mild steel plough beam).

$$N_y = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{working}} \quad \text{or} \quad N_y = \frac{\tau_y}{\tau_{working}}$$

### B. Factor of Safety Based on Ultimate Strength
Used for **brittle materials** (cast iron plough bottom) or when **catastrophic fracture** is the concern.

$$N_u = \frac{\sigma_{u}}{\sigma_{working}}$$

### C. Factor of Safety Based on Fatigue / Endurance Limit
For components subjected to **cyclic loading** (PTO shaft, rotary tiller blade, spring tyne).

$$N_f = \frac{\sigma_{e}}{\sigma_{working}} \quad \text{or} \quad N_f = \frac{\sigma_{e}'}{\sigma_{a}}$$

where $\sigma_{e}'$ is the modified endurance limit (Marin equation) and $\sigma_{a}$ is the stress amplitude.

### D. Factor of Safety Based on Buckling
For **long slender columns** (tool bar mounting posts, draw-bar in trailers).

$$N_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{P_{applied}} = \frac{\pi^{2}EI}{L_{e}^{2}\,P_{applied}}$$

### E. Factor of Safety Based on Rupture (Deflection/Strain)
Used when service is governed by **deformation limits** (e.g., excessive bending of a cultivator frame disturbs row alignment).

$$N_{\delta} = \frac{\delta_{allowable}}{\delta_{working}}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Factor of Safety**
>
> | # | Formula (Rendered in LaTeX) | Variable Meaning | Typical Units |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | $N = \sigma_{u} / \sigma_{w}$ | Ultimate-strength-based FoS | dimensionless |
> | 2 | $N_{y} = \sigma_{y} / \sigma_{w}$ | Yield-strength-based FoS | dimensionless |
> | 3 | $N_{f} = \sigma_{e}' / \sigma_{a}$ | Fatigue/endurance-based FoS | dimensionless |
> | 4 | $\sigma_{allowable} = \sigma_{u} / N$ | Allowable stress (ultimate basis) | MPa or N/mm² |
> | 5 | $\sigma_{allowable} = \sigma_{y} / N$ | Allowable stress (yield basis) | MPa or N/mm² |
> | 6 | $P_{cr} = \pi^{2} E I / L_{e}^{2}$ | Euler critical buckling load | N |
> | 7 | $N_{cr} = P_{cr} / P$ | Buckling FoS | dimensionless |
> | 8 | $\sigma_{max} = (32 M) / (\pi d^{3})$ | Bending stress in circular shaft | MPa |
> | 9 | $\tau_{max} = (16 T) / (\pi d^{3})$ | Torsional shear stress | MPa |
> | 10 | $\sigma_{w} = F / A$ | Direct working stress | MPa |
> | 11 | $N_{resultant} = \vert \sigma_{max}\vert / \sigma_{allowable}$ | Combined FoS check | dimensionless |
> | 12 | $n_{1} \cdot n_{2} = N_{overall}$ | Combined loading (multiplier rule) | dimensionless |
> | 13 | $C_{m} \cdot C_{st} \cdot C_{r} \cdot C_{b} = C_{total}$ | Combined service factor (ASAE) | dimensionless |
> | 14 | $N = N_{y} + (N_{u} - N_{y})\cos 2\theta$ | FoS on inclined plane (Mohr) | dimensionless |

> **Note on Notation:** Where absolute value notation is required in equations, the **vertical bar** is rendered as `\vert` (e.g., $\vert \sigma_{max} \vert$) to prevent markdown table syntax collisions. In displayed equations, both $\vert$ and $\mid$ produce identical output.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the design of **tractor-operated implements** (MB plough, disc harrow, rotavator, seed-cum-fertilizer drill), the FoS governs:

1. **Selection of standard sections** (ISMC channels, hollow square tubes) — the catalogue's "safe load" is the ultimate load divided by a built-in FoS.
2. **Weld and bolt sizing** at joints of the implement frame.
3. **Spring rate selection** for the three-point linkage.
4. **PTO shaft sizing** — for fatigue over millions of rotations, the **fatigue-based FoS** is critical.
5. **Regulatory compliance** under BIS/IS standards and **ASAE EP 496.3** (Agricultural Machinery Management Data).

> [!TIP]
> **ASAE Combined Service Factor ($C_{total}$):** Real agricultural machines use a *combined service factor* multiplying four sub-factors: $C_{m}$ (moisture), $C_{st}$ (speed/temperature), $C_{r}$ (reliability), $C_{b}$ (operator behaviour). The design FoS becomes $N_{design} = N_{static} \times C_{total}$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Allowable Stress from Factor of Safety

**Starting Point — The Fundamental Relation:**

We know the working (induced) stress in a member is:

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{F_{applied}}{A_{cross-section}}$$

The Factor of Safety definition gives:

$$N = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{\sigma_{working}}$$

**Step 1 — Invert to solve for working stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{working} &= \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute working stress in terms of load and area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{F_{applied}}{A} &= \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Allowable Stress:**

The allowable stress is **what the member is permitted to carry** in service:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{allowable} &= \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the required cross-sectional area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{required} &= \frac{F_{applied}}{\sigma_{allowable}} = \frac{F_{applied} \cdot N}{\sigma_{failure}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** To design safely, the actual area must be **at least equal to** $A_{required}$. We then select the **next higher standard size** from the section catalogue.

## 3.2 Numerical Example — KTU Model Problem

### Problem Statement
> The **main beam of a tractor-drawn Mould-Board (MB) Plough** is subjected to a maximum draft force of **18 kN** along its longitudinal axis. The beam is made of **Mild Steel (Fe 410)** with:
> - Ultimate tensile strength, $\sigma_{u} = 410$ N/mm²
> - Yield strength, $\sigma_{y} = 250$ N/mm²
>
> The design code requires **N = 3** (ultimate basis) for the structural frame.
>
> **Find:** (i) Allowable stress, (ii) Required cross-sectional area, (iii) Required diameter if the beam is circular solid, (iv) Verify the design with a yield-based FoS check.

### Given Data (Extracted)
- $F = 18$ kN $= 18\,000$ N
- $\sigma_{u} = 410$ N/mm²
- $\sigma_{y} = 250$ N/mm²
- $N = 3$

### (i) Allowable Stress

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{allowable} &= \frac{\sigma_{u}}{N} = \frac{410}{3} \\
&= 136.67 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \quad (\text{rounded to } 136.7 \text{ MPa})
\end{aligned}
$$

### (ii) Required Cross-Sectional Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{required} &= \frac{F}{\sigma_{allowable}} = \frac{18\,000}{136.67} \\
&= 131.71 \text{ mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (iii) Required Diameter (Circular Section)

For a circular cross-section, $A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} &= 131.71 \\
d^{2} &= \frac{4 \times 131.71}{\pi} = \frac{526.84}{3.1416} = 167.71 \\
d &= \sqrt{167.71} = 12.95 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Selected standard size:** $d = 16$ mm (next higher preferred size, to account for wear, corrosion, and field variability).

### (iv) Verification with Yield-Based FoS

Working stress in the 16 mm bar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{actual} &= \frac{\pi (16)^{2}}{4} = 201.06 \text{ mm}^{2} \\
\sigma_{working} &= \frac{F}{A_{actual}} = \frac{18\,000}{201.06} = 89.53 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Yield-based Factor of Safety obtained:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{y,\,obtained} &= \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{working}} = \frac{250}{89.53} \\
&= 2.79
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The design has an *effective yield-based FoS* of **2.79** in the 16 mm section. Since 2.79 is **acceptable** (typical for ductile steel = 1.5 to 3), the design is **safe**. The "extra" safety margin comes from rounding up to standard size.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Logic for FoS

| S.No. | Failure Mode | Failure Stress | Governing FoS | When Used in Agri-Machinery |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Yielding (Ductile)** | $\sigma_{y}$ | $N_{y} = \sigma_{y}/\sigma_{w}$ | Plough beam, cultivator frame, tyne shank |
| 2 | **Ultimate Fracture (Brittle)** | $\sigma_{u}$ | $N_{u} = \sigma_{u}/\sigma_{w}$ | Cast-iron plough bottom, share point |
| 3 | **Fatigue (Cyclic)** | $\sigma_{e}'$ | $N_{f} = \sigma_{e}'/\sigma_{a}$ | PTO shaft, spring tyne, rotary blade |
| 4 | **Buckling (Column)** | $P_{cr}$ | $N_{cr} = P_{cr}/P$ | Tool-bar post, trailer drawbar |
| 5 | **Deflection (Stiffness)** | $\delta_{max}$ | $N_{\delta} = \delta_{allow}/\delta_{w}$ | Long boom of sprayer, toolbar |
| 6 | **Rupture (Creep)** | $\sigma_{r}$ | $N_{r} = \sigma_{r}/\sigma_{w}$ | Hot sections of engine-driven pumps |
| 7 | **Impact / Shock** | $\sigma_{i} = K\,\sigma$ | $N_{i} = \sigma_{y}/(K\sigma_{w})$ | Shares striking stones, harrow discs |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — FoS Selection Logic in Design

The following Mermaid flow diagram shows the **logical decision process** for selecting the correct Factor of Safety formulation when designing an agricultural implement component.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify Service Load Type] --> B[Step 2: Identify Material Type]
    B --> C[Step 3: Identify Dominant Failure Mode]
    C --> D{Is Material Ductile?}
    D -- YES --> E[Use Yield Strength]
    D -- NO --> F[Use Ultimate Strength]
    E --> G{Is Loading Cyclical?}
    G -- YES --> H[Apply Fatigue FoS with Marin Correction]
    G -- NO --> I[Apply Static Yield FoS]
    F --> J{Is Loading Impact Type?}
    J -- YES --> K[Apply Impact Stress Factor K]
    J -- NO --> L[Apply Static Ultimate FoS]
    H --> M[Compute Allowable Stress]
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Step 4: Compute Required Area and Section Size]
    N --> O[Step 5: Select Next Higher Standard Size]
    O --> P[Step 6: Verify Combined Loading FoS]
    P --> Q[Step 7: Apply ASAE Combined Service Factor]
    Q --> R[Final Design Approved]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — FoS Hierarchical Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Factor of Safety Master Concept] --> B[Yield Based]
    A --> C[Ultimate Strength Based]
    A --> D[Fatigue Based]
    A --> E[Buckling Based]
    A --> F[Deflection Based]
    B --> B1[For Ductile Steel]
    B --> B2[For Aluminium Alloys]
    C --> C1[For Cast Iron]
    C --> C2[For Brittle Composites]
    D --> D1[For Rotating Shafts]
    D --> D2[For Springs]
    E --> E1[For Long Columns]
    E --> E2[For Slender Toolbars]
    F --> F1[For Long Booms]
    F --> F2[For Cantilever Arms]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Inputs Feeding into Design FoS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Material Property Variability] --> F[Final Design FoS]
    S2[Load Estimation Uncertainty] --> F
    S3[Manufacturing Tolerances] --> F
    S4[Service Environment and Corrosion] --> F
    S5[Reliability and Consequence of Failure] --> F
    S6[Safety Regulations and BIS Standards] --> F
    F --> D[Conservative Allowable Stress]
    D --> G[Safe Sizing of Component]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents one design step. The arrows show the *flow of decision logic*. In the KTU examination, you may be asked to draw a similar flowchart (typically for 3–4 marks) showing how you would *select* a FoS value for a given agricultural component.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
