## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. Define Factor of Safety. Why is it necessary in the design of agricultural implements? **[3 Marks]** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocation shown in brackets):**

> **Definition [1 mark]:** The Factor of Safety (N) is defined as the ratio of the maximum stress (load) a material can withstand to the maximum allowable (working) stress (load) it is actually subjected to in service.
>
> $$N = \frac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}}$$
>
> **Necessity in Agricultural Implements [2 marks]:**
> 1. Agricultural field loads are **highly variable** (soil type, depth, speed, moisture) and difficult to predict precisely.
> 2. Implements are exposed to **impact, vibration, dust, and corrosion** which degrade material strength over time.
> 3. Material properties of **cast iron, steel, springs** show batch-to-batch variability.
> 4. FoS provides a **design margin** to ensure operator safety, prevent crop loss, and comply with **BIS / ASAE standards**.

---

### Q2. List any four factors on which the choice of Factor of Safety depends. **[3 Marks]** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per factor + 1 mark for explanation):**

The choice of Factor of Safety depends on:

1. **Type of loading** (static, dynamic, impact, cyclic fatigue). *(½ m)*
2. **Material properties** (ductile, brittle, homogeneity). *(½ m)*
3. **Mode of failure** (yielding, fracture, buckling, fatigue). *(½ m)*
4. **Consequence of failure** (operator safety, repair cost, machine criticality). *(½ m)*
5. **Service environment** (corrosive, abrasive, temperature exposure). *(½ m)*
6. **Reliability and quality control of manufacturing**. *(½ m)*

*(Any four with one-line explanation — full 3 marks)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**
*Internal Choice: Either this OR Question B*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

**(a)** Define Factor of Safety. With a neat sketch, explain the **stress–strain behaviour of a ductile material** and show the **position of working stress, yield stress, and ultimate stress** on the curve. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A **rotavator rotor blade** is subjected to a maximum bending moment of **420 N·m** due to soil impact. The blade cross-section is rectangular with width $b = 40$ mm and depth $h = 12$ mm. The blade is made of **Spring Steel (50CrV4)** with yield strength $\sigma_{y} = 1200$ N/mm². Determine the **Factor of Safety against yielding**. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Definition + Stress–Strain Sketch [7 Marks]

**Definition [1 mark]:**

> Factor of Safety (N) is the ratio of the failure stress (yield or ultimate, depending on material) to the working/induced stress in the component.
> $$N = \sigma_{failure} / \sigma_{working}$$

**Description of the Stress–Strain Diagram [4 marks]:**

For a ductile mild steel under tensile loading, the curve has the following key points (to be drawn in the answer script):

| Region | Description | Stress Range |
|---|---|---|
| **O – A** | Linear elastic region (Hooke's law) | $0 \to \sigma_{p}$ (proportional limit) |
| **A – B** | Elastic region (no permanent deformation) | $\sigma_{p} \to \sigma_{y}$ (yield point) |
| **B – C** | Perfectly plastic / yield plateau | $\sigma_{y}$ (constant) |
| **C – D** | Strain-hardening region | $\sigma_{y} \to \sigma_{u}$ (ultimate) |
| **D – E** | Necking region | $\sigma_{u} \to \sigma_{f}$ (fracture) |

**Position of Working Stress [2 marks]:**

The designer chooses $\sigma_{working}$ such that it lies in the **linear-elastic region**, well below $\sigma_{y}$. The Factor of Safety is the ratio of $\sigma_{y}$ to $\sigma_{working}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{working} &\ll \sigma_{y} \\
N_{y} &= \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{working}} \;\; \text{(typically 1.5 to 3 for ductile steel)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**

- $M = 420$ N·m $= 420 \times 10^{3}$ N·mm
- $b = 40$ mm, $h = 12$ mm
- $\sigma_{y} = 1200$ N/mm²

**Step 1 — Section Modulus of the Rectangular Section [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{b h^{2}}{6} = \frac{40 \times (12)^{2}}{6} = \frac{40 \times 144}{6} = 960 \text{ mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Bending Stress [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{420 \times 10^{3}}{960} \\
&= 437.5 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \;(\text{MPa})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor of Safety against Yielding [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{y} &= \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{1200}{437.5} \\
&= 2.74
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion [1 mark]:**

> Since $N_{y} = 2.74$ falls within the **recommended range of 1.5 to 3** for ductile spring steel, the rotavator blade is **SAFE** under the given bending load.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative to Question A)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Factor of Safety based on Yielding** and **Factor of Safety based on Ultimate Strength**. State the conditions under which each is used in the design of agricultural machinery. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The **drawbar of a tractor-trailer combination** is a hollow circular tube of outer diameter 60 mm and inner diameter 50 mm. It is subjected to an axial tensile pull of **50 kN** during transport. The material is **Structural Steel (Fe 410)** with $\sigma_{u} = 410$ N/mm² and $\sigma_{y} = 250$ N/mm². Find the **Factor of Safety based on ultimate strength** and the **Factor of Safety based on yielding**. Comment on the safety of the design. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Differentiation Table + Conditions [7 Marks]

| Basis | FoS based on Yielding | FoS based on Ultimate Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $N_{y} = \sigma_{y} / \sigma_{w}$ | $N_{u} = \sigma_{u} / \sigma_{w}$ |
| **Failure criterion** | Onset of plastic deformation | Complete fracture / rupture |
| **Material type** | **Ductile** (mild steel, aluminium) | **Brittle** (cast iron, CI plough bottom) |
| **Allowable stress** | $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_{y}/N_{y}$ | $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_{u}/N_{u}$ |
| **Typical FoS range** | **1.5 – 3.0** | **3.0 – 6.0** (higher for brittle) |
| **Agri-machinery example** | Spring tyne, plough beam, cultivator frame | Share point, mould board, CI hub |
| **Loading type** | Static or slowly varying | Static, brittle failure |
| **Design philosophy** | Prevent plastic deformation | Prevent sudden fracture |

**Conditions of Use [3 marks]:**
- **Yield-based FoS** is used when the material yields before fracture and we want to limit **permanent deformation** (ductile design philosophy).
- **Ultimate-based FoS** is used for **brittle materials** that fracture without yielding, or where **sudden catastrophic failure** must be prevented.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**

- Outer diameter, $D = 60$ mm → $D^{2} = 3600$
- Inner diameter, $d = 50$ mm → $d^{2} = 2500$
- Axial pull, $F = 50$ kN $= 50\,000$ N
- $\sigma_{u} = 410$ N/mm², $\sigma_{y} = 250$ N/mm²

**Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area of the Hollow Tube [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4}\,(D^{2} - d^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(3600 - 2500) \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1100 = 863.94 \text{ mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Working (Induced) Tensile Stress [1 mark]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{working} &= \frac{F}{A} = \frac{50\,000}{863.94} = 57.88 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor of Safety based on Ultimate Strength [1.5 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{u} &= \frac{\sigma_{u}}{\sigma_{working}} = \frac{410}{57.88} = 7.08
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Factor of Safety based on Yielding [1.5 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{y} &= \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{working}} = \frac{250}{57.88} = 4.32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Safety Comment [1 mark]:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment:** Both factors of safety are well above the minimum recommended values for ductile steel ($N_{y} \geq 1.5$ and $N_{u} \geq 3$). The drawbar is **CONSERVATIVELY SAFE**, indicating the design is **over-designed** (or the load is much lower than the material capacity). A designer may optimize (reduce wall thickness) if weight and cost are concerns.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (kN → N, m → mm) — leads to wrong answers worth **1–2 marks lost**.
> 2. **Using the wrong stress formula** (e.g., using torsional formula $\tau = 16T/\pi d^{3}$ for an axial load problem) — major error.
> 3. **Not stating the design philosophy** (yield-based vs. ultimate-based) before computing N — examiner deducts **1 mark for missing reasoning**.
> 4. **Choosing an unrealistic FoS** (e.g., N = 1.0 or N = 20) without justification — always quote the **code or standard** (BIS / ASAE).
> 5. **Skipping the verification step** — the examiner expects a *yield-based FoS check* after an *ultimate-based design* (and vice versa).
> 6. **In the sketch, confusing $\sigma_{y}$ and $\sigma_{u}$** on the stress–strain curve — leads to **2 marks lost** in descriptive questions.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Factor of Safety in Agricultural Implement Design**
>
> - **Definition:** $N = \sigma_{failure} / \sigma_{working}$ (always ratio, always dimensionless).
> - **Yield-based FoS** → ductile materials (steel, aluminium); prevents plastic deformation.
> - **Ultimate-based FoS** → brittle materials (cast iron, CI share); prevents fracture.
> - **Fatigue-based FoS** → rotating / cyclic parts (PTO shaft, rotavator blade); uses $\sigma_{e}'$ (modified endurance limit).
> - **Buckling-based FoS** → long slender columns (toolbar, drawbar) → Euler's $P_{cr} = \pi^{2}EI/L_{e}^{2}$.
> - **Allowable stress:** $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_{failure} / N$ — this is the **maximum permitted** working stress.
> - **Standard FoS ranges:** Steel = 1.5–3, Cast iron = 4–6, Brittle = 6–10, Springs = 1.2–1.5.
> - **Always use next-higher standard section size** after calculation.
> - **Verify** the design with an *alternative failure mode* FoS check (e.g., ultimate design → yield check).
> - **ASAE Combined Service Factor:** $C_{total} = C_{m} \cdot C_{st} \cdot C_{r} \cdot C_{b}$ — multiply with static FoS for field conditions.
> - **KTU-favourite units:** Stress in N/mm² (MPa), Length in mm, Force in N or kN.
> - **Common formula trap:** Bending stress = $M/Z$; Torsional stress = $T \cdot r / J = 16T/(\pi d^{3})$ for solid shaft.
> - **Combined loading:** Use $\sigma_{max}$ from principal stress theory (max-normal-stress) or $\sigma_{VM}$ (von Mises) and compare with $\sigma_{allowable}$.
> - **Always state the design code / standard** (e.g., BIS 7443, ASAE S304, IS 14200) wherever applicable — gives **bonus ½ mark** in KTU valuation.
> - **Justify the FoS value** with: (a) type of load, (b) material, (c) failure mode, (d) consequence of failure.
> - **Sign convention:** Tensile = positive, Compressive = negative — be consistent in Mohr's circle problems.
> - **Sketch tip:** Always mark $\sigma_{w}$ well *below* $\sigma_{y}$ on the stress–strain diagram to show *safe* design margin.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Allowable Stress, Stress Concentration, Elementary Fatigue and Creep Aspects, Cotter Joints
Knuckle Joint and Pinned Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT604 - DESIGN OF AGRICULTURAL IMPLEMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Knuckle Joint and Pinned Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Knuckle Joint — Formal Definition

A **knuckle joint** is a mechanical pin-type joint used to connect two rods that are subjected to tensile (pulling) loads. It permits a small angular movement (limited rotation) between the connected rods, similar to a human knuckle — which gives the joint its name. It is widely used in agricultural machinery for tie rods, control linkages, and connecting components in ploughs, seed drills, cultivators, and tractor-mounted implements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBAGT604 — Module 1):**
> A knuckle joint is a type of **pinned (hinged) joint** designed primarily for **tensile load transmission** with limited angular articulation. It is *not* a rigid coupling and is *not* intended for torque transmission.

## 1.2 Pinned Joint — Formal Definition

A **pinned joint** (also called a *hinge joint* or *cotter joint with pin*) is a general class of connection where a cylindrical pin passes through aligned holes in two or more members, locking them together while permitting relative rotation. Knuckle joints, cotter joints, and clevis joints are all specific subtypes of pinned joints. In agricultural machinery design, pinned joints are used wherever hinged articulation, ease of assembly/disassembly, and tensile load transfer are required.

## 1.3 Components of a Knuckle Joint

A standard knuckle joint consists of four principal components:

1. **Single Eye (Rod End 1)** — A rod forged with a circular eye at one end, through which the pin passes.
2. **Double Eye (Rod End 2 / Fork End)** — A rod end split into two parallel prongs (a fork) with coaxial holes, sandwiching the single eye.
3. **Pin** — A cylindrical hardened steel pin that passes through the aligned holes and is secured by a tapered split pin (cotter pin) at the open end.
4. **Tapered Split Pin / Collar** — Prevents the pin from sliding out under vibration and load reversal.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Finger Knuckle:** Press your index and middle fingers together to form a "V". Now insert a pencil (the **pin**) through a small ring on each finger (the **eyes**). The fingers can swing slightly (limited rotation) but cannot come apart unless the pencil is removed. The pencil carries all the pulling force. This is exactly how a knuckle joint works in a tractor's three-point linkage or in the steering tie-rod of a plough assembly.

## 1.5 Applications in Agricultural Machinery

| Application | Function of Joint |
|---|---|
| Tractor 3-point linkage rods | Articulated tensile connection between tractor and implement |
| Plough beam tie-rods | Maintains geometry under pull force |
| Seed drill / fertilizer drill drawbar | Allows alignment during transport & field operation |
| Cultivator frame connectors | Permits folding and articulation |
| Trailer hitches | Quick-disconnect tensile coupling |
| Sprayer boom supports | Allows folding for transport |

## 1.6 Standard Empirical Proportions (for KTU Design Problems)

The following proportions are universally accepted in machine design practice and are expected in KTU numericals:

| Parameter | Symbol | Empirical Relation |
|---|---|---|
| Pin diameter | $d_p$ | $= d$ |
| Outer diameter of eye | $d_e$ | $= 2.5\,d$ |
| Thickness of single eye | $t_1$ | $= 1.5\,d$ |
| Thickness of each fork prong | $t_2$ | $= 0.75\,d$ |
| Gap between fork prongs | $c$ | $= t_1 - 2t_2$ *(should be ≥ d)* |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle

The load $W$ applied to one rod is transmitted through the **eye → pin → fork** path to the other rod. Because the pin is the only shear-transferring element, **the pin is the most critical component** in the design. The design ensures that the joint does not fail by any of the standard modes under the working load $W$.

## 2.2 Modes of Failure of a Knuckle Joint

A knuckle joint may fail by any of the following six mechanisms. The design must satisfy **all** criteria:

1. **Tensile failure of the solid rod** (behind the eye).
2. **Shearing of the pin** (single or double shear).
3. **Crushing of the pin** (bearing stress on pin).
4. **Crushing of the eye** (bearing stress on eye).
5. **Tearing of the eye** (along the section through the pin hole).
6. **Shearing of the eye / fork** (along the side of the pin hole).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination), memorise these six equations. They are the foundation of *every* knuckle joint design problem.**

| Sl. | Failure Mode | Design Equation | Restriction |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tension in rod | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{4W}{\pi d^2} \le \sigma_{t,\text{perm}}$ | Defines $d$ |
| 2 | Shear in pin (double shear) | $\tau = \dfrac{2W}{\pi d_p^{\,2}} \le \tau_{\text{perm}}$ | Defines $d_p$ |
| 3 | Crushing on pin | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{W}{d_p \cdot t_1} \le \sigma_{c,\text{perm}}$ | Defines $t_1$ |
| 4 | Crushing on eye | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{W}{d_e \cdot t_1} \le \sigma_{c,\text{perm}}$ | Defines $d_e$ |
| 5 | Tearing of eye | $W_{\text{tear}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\bigl(d_e^{\,2} - d_p^{\,2}\bigr)\sigma_t \ge W$ | Verification |
| 6 | Shearing of eye (side) | $W_{\text{sh,eye}} = 2\,(d_e - d_p)\,t_1\,\tau \ge W$ | Verification |

**Notation:**
- $W$ = axial tensile load on the rod (N)
- $d$ = diameter of solid rod (mm)
- $d_p$ = diameter of pin (mm)
- $d_e$ = outer diameter of eye (mm)
- $t_1$ = thickness of single eye (mm)
- $\sigma_{t,\text{perm}}, \tau_{\text{perm}}, \sigma_{c,\text{perm}}$ = permissible stresses (MPa) for the chosen material

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Design Logic (How to Solve a KTU Numerical)

The design is **sequential** — each step's result becomes the input to the next. Follow this exact order to obtain full marks:

1. **Step 1 — Rod diameter ($d$):** From tensile failure of the rod.
   $$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4W}{\pi\,\sigma_{t,\text{perm}}}}$$
2. **Step 2 — Pin diameter ($d_p$):** Standard design choice $d_p = d$ (for equal strength against shear and tension). If computed independently from pin shear:
   $$d_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\pi\,\tau_{\text{perm}}}}$$
3. **Step 3 — Eye outer diameter ($d_e$):** From crushing of eye OR from empirical rule:
   $$d_e = \max\!\left(\,2.5\,d,\ \sqrt{\dfrac{4W}{\pi\,\sigma_{c,\text{perm}}}} + d_p\right)$$
4. **Step 4 — Single-eye thickness ($t_1$):** From crushing of pin:
   $$t_1 = \dfrac{W}{d_p \cdot \sigma_{c,\text{perm}}}$$
5. **Step 5 — Fork thickness each side ($t_2$):**
   $$t_2 = 0.75\,d$$
6. **Step 6 — Pin length ($l$):** $l = 2\,t_2 + t_1$ (for double-eye arrangement, with head). Add collar/split-pin allowance.
7. **Step 7 — Verify** tearing and shearing resistance of eye (≥ W).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in agricultural engineering:** In tractor-implement systems, the knuckle joint must withstand shock loads (e.g., plough hitting a stone) that may reach **2–3× the steady-state draft force**. KTU design practice assumes a **factor of safety** of **3 to 5** on ultimate strength to absorb these dynamic spikes, which is why permissible stresses in the syllabus are roughly 1/3 to 1/5 of ultimate values.

## 2.6 Material Properties Commonly Used in KTU Problems

| Material | $\sigma_{t,\text{ult}}$ (MPa) | $\tau_{\text{ult}}$ (MPa) | $\sigma_{c,\text{ult}}$ (MPa) | Typical FoS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 400 | 320 | 640 | 3–5 |
| Medium Carbon Steel (pin) | 600 | 480 | 960 | 3–4 |
| Wrought Iron | 350 | 280 | 560 | 4–5 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Design Equations

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Rod Diameter ($d$) from Tensile Failure

The rod carries the full axial load $W$ over its cross-section. Tensile stress:
$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{W}{A_{\text{rod}}} = \dfrac{W}{\dfrac{\pi}{4}\,d^2} = \dfrac{4W}{\pi d^2}$$

For safe design, set $\sigma_t \le \sigma_{t,\text{perm}}$:
$$\dfrac{4W}{\pi d^2} \le \sigma_{t,\text{perm}}$$

Rearranging for $d$:
$$d \ge \sqrt{\dfrac{4W}{\pi\,\sigma_{t,\text{perm}}}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This is the *smallest* cross-section that will not yield in tension. The eye must be forged from a rod of at least this diameter.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Pin Diameter ($d_p$) from Shear Failure (Double Shear)

In a knuckle joint, the pin is supported by the single eye on one side and by the two prongs of the fork on the other. Hence the pin tends to fail along **two parallel shear planes** — this is *double shear*.

If the shear load on each plane is $W/2$, and the area of each plane is $\dfrac{\pi}{4}d_p^{\,2}$:
$$\tau = \dfrac{W/2}{\dfrac{\pi}{4}d_p^{\,2}} = \dfrac{2W}{\pi d_p^{\,2}}$$

Setting $\tau \le \tau_{\text{perm}}$:
$$d_p \ge \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\pi\,\tau_{\text{perm}}}}$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Eye Thickness ($t_1$) from Crushing of Pin

The pin presses on the inner surface of the eye over a *projected bearing area* of $d_p \times t_1$. The compressive (crushing) stress is:
$$\sigma_c = \dfrac{W}{d_p \cdot t_1}$$

For safety:
$$t_1 \ge \dfrac{W}{d_p \cdot \sigma_{c,\text{perm}}}$$

### 3.1.4 Derivation of Tearing Resistance of the Eye

The eye could tear along the *tangential section* behind the pin hole. The resisting area is an annulus of outer diameter $d_e$ and inner diameter $d_p$:
$$A_{\text{tear}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\bigl(d_e^{\,2} - d_p^{\,2}\bigr)$$

The tearing load is therefore:
$$W_{\text{tear}} = A_{\text{tear}} \cdot \sigma_t = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\bigl(d_e^{\,2} - d_p^{\,2}\bigr)\sigma_t$$

For safety: $W_{\text{tear}} \ge W$.

### 3.1.5 Derivation of Shearing Resistance of the Eye (Side Shear)

The eye could shear off on the two side planes parallel to the applied load, each of area $(d_e - d_p) \cdot t_1$. Total resisting force:
$$W_{\text{sh,eye}} = 2\,(d_e - d_p)\,t_1\,\tau$$

For safety: $W_{\text{sh,eye}} \ge W$.

---

## 3.2 Complete Worked Example (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Design a knuckle joint to connect two mild-steel rods subjected to an axial tensile load of **$W = 50\,\text{kN}$**. Permissible stresses: $\sigma_t = 60\,\text{MPa}$, $\tau = 40\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_c = 80\,\text{MPa}$. The joint must allow limited angular movement.

### Given Data
- $W = 50\,\text{kN} = 50\,000\,\text{N}$
- $\sigma_{t,\text{perm}} = 60\,\text{MPa} = 60\,\text{N/mm}^2$
- $\tau_{\text{perm}} = 40\,\text{MPa} = 40\,\text{N/mm}^2$
- $\sigma_{c,\text{perm}} = 80\,\text{MPa} = 80\,\text{N/mm}^2$

### Step 1 — Diameter of Solid Rod ($d$)

$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4W}{\pi\,\sigma_{t,\text{perm}}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 50\,000}{\pi \times 60}}$$

$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{200\,000}{188.496}} = \sqrt{1061.03} = 32.57\,\text{mm}$$

**Round up:** $d = 33\,\text{mm}$ (standard practice in KTU: round to next higher integer or standard size).

### Step 2 — Diameter of Pin ($d_p$)

Standard design rule: $d_p = d = 33\,\text{mm}$.

**Verification by direct shear calculation:**
$$d_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\pi\,\tau_{\text{perm}}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 50\,000}{\pi \times 40}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{100\,000}{125.66}} = \sqrt{795.77} = 28.21\,\text{mm}$$

Since direct shear gives $d_p = 28.21\,\text{mm}$ and the equal-strength rule gives $d_p = 33\,\text{mm}$, **the pin is governed by the equal-strength criterion**: $d_p = 33\,\text{mm}$.

### Step 3 — Outer Diameter of Eye ($d_e$)

Empirical rule: $d_e = 2.5\,d = 2.5 \times 33 = 82.5\,\text{mm}$.

**Verification by crushing of eye:**
$$d_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{4W}{\pi\,\sigma_{c,\text{perm}}} + d_p^{\,2}}$$

$$d_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 50\,000}{\pi \times 80} + 33^2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{200\,000}{251.33} + 1089} = \sqrt{795.77 + 1089} = \sqrt{1884.77} = 43.42\,\text{mm}$$

Take the **larger** of the two: $d_e = 82.5\,\text{mm}$ (empirical rule governs).

### Step 4 — Thickness of Single Eye ($t_1$)

$$t_1 = \dfrac{W}{d_p \cdot \sigma_{c,\text{perm}}} = \dfrac{50\,000}{33 \times 80} = \dfrac{50\,000}{2640} = 18.94\,\text{mm}$$

**Adopt standard rule** (which gives a more robust design): $t_1 = 1.5\,d = 1.5 \times 33 = 49.5\,\text{mm}$.

We use the **empirical value** $t_1 = 49.5\,\text{mm}$ as it is the industry standard.

### Step 5 — Thickness of Each Fork Prong ($t_2$)

$$t_2 = 0.75\,d = 0.75 \times 33 = 24.75\,\text{mm}$$

**Check gap:**
$$c = t_1 - 2\,t_2 = 49.5 - 2(24.75) = 49.5 - 49.5 = 0$$

The empirical proportions give $c = 0$, which is theoretically fine but allows no clearance. Practically, set $t_2 = 24\,\text{mm}$ so that $c = 1.5\,\text{mm} > 0$ (provides assembly clearance).

### Step 6 — Length of Pin ($l$)

For a knuckle joint with a head on one side and a split-pin on the other:
$$l = t_1 + 2\,t_2 + \text{(head allowance)} + \text{(split-pin allowance)}$$
$$l = 49.5 + 2(24) + 6 + 6 = 109.5\,\text{mm} \approx 110\,\text{mm}$$

### Step 7 — Verification of Tearing Resistance of Eye

$$W_{\text{tear}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\bigl(d_e^{\,2} - d_p^{\,2}\bigr)\,\sigma_t$$

$$W_{\text{tear}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(82.5^2 - 33^2)(60) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(6806.25 - 1089)(60)$$

$$W_{\text{tear}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(5717.25)(60) = 0.7854 \times 5717.25 \times 60 = 269\,427\,\text{N} = 269.4\,\text{kN}$$

**Factor of safety against tearing:** $\text{FoS} = 269.4 / 50 = 5.39$ ✓ (Safe)

### Step 8 — Verification of Shearing of Eye (Side Planes)

$$W_{\text{sh,eye}} = 2\,(d_e - d_p)\,t_1\,\tau = 2(82.5 - 33)(49.5)(40)$$

$$W_{\text{sh,eye}} = 2 \times 49.5 \times 49.5 \times 40 = 196\,020\,\text{N} = 196.02\,\text{kN}$$

**Factor of safety against side shear:** $\text{FoS} = 196.02 / 50 = 3.92$ ✓ (Safe)

### Step 9 — Verification of Crushing on Pin

$$\sigma_c = \dfrac{W}{d_p \cdot t_1} = \dfrac{50\,000}{33 \times 49.5} = \dfrac{50\,000}{1633.5} = 30.61\,\text{MPa} \le 80\,\text{MPa} \checkmark$$

### Final Design Summary

| Parameter | Symbol | Designed Value |
|---|---|---|
| Rod diameter | $d$ | **33 mm** |
| Pin diameter | $d_p$ | **33 mm** |
| Outer eye diameter | $d_e$ | **82.5 mm** |
| Single eye thickness | $t_1$ | **49.5 mm** |
| Fork prong thickness (each) | $t_2$ | **24 mm** |
| Pin length | $l$ | **110 mm** |
| Load capacity (tearing) | $W_{\text{tear}}$ | 269.4 kN |
| Load capacity (side shear) | $W_{\text{sh,eye}}$ | 196.0 kN |
| Safety factor (overall) | — | **≥ 3.9** |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always show *both* the empirical-rule value *and* the verification value. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for the verification step alone.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Knuckle Joint

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional load-transfer architecture** of a knuckle joint assembly. It shows how the applied axial load $W$ is distributed through the components and the failure-mode checks that govern the design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Axial Tensile Load W] --> B[Rod 1: Single Eye End]
    B --> C[Pin: Cylindrical Shear Transfer Element]
    C --> D[Rod 2: Double Eye / Fork End]
    D --> E[Output: Load Transmitted to Second Rod]
    F[Material Selection: Mild Steel or Medium Carbon] --> C
    F --> B
    F --> D
    G[Design Check 1: Tension in Rod] --> B
    H[Design Check 2: Shear in Pin Double Shear] --> C
    I[Design Check 3: Crushing on Pin] --> C
    J[Design Check 4: Crushing on Eye] --> B
    K[Design Check 5: Tearing of Eye] --> B
    L[Design Check 6: Side Shear of Eye] --> B
    M[Output: Geometric Dimensions d dp de t1 t2 l] --> N[Final Manufacturable Joint]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic (ASCII Representation)

```
                 Pin
       ┌──────────┤├──────────┐
       │          ││          │
       │   ┌──────┘└──────┐   │
       │   │   FORK END   │   │   t1 = 49.5 mm
       │   │  (t2 + t2)   │   │   Single-eye thickness
       │   ├──────┐┌──────┤   │
       │   │      ││      │   │   d_p = 33 mm
       │   │ EYE  ││ EYE  │   │   Pin diameter
       │   │ END  ││ END  │   │   d_e = 82.5 mm
       │   │      ││      │   │   Eye outer diameter
       │   └──────┘└──────┘   │
       │          ││          │
       └──────────┘└──────────┘
                 Pin
                 │
              Split-Pin
            (Secures Pin)
```

## 4.3 Failure-Mode Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Failure 1: Tension in Rod] --> FM1[Check: d from sigma_t]
    R2[Failure 2: Pin Shear Double] --> FM2[Check: d_p from tau]
    R3[Failure 3: Pin Crushing] --> FM3[Check: t1 from sigma_c]
    R4[Failure 4: Eye Crushing] --> FM4[Check: d_e from sigma_c]
    R5[Failure 5: Eye Tearing] --> FM5[Check: W_tear ge W]
    R6[Failure 6: Eye Side Shear] --> FM6[Check: W_sh_eye ge W]
    FM1 --> SAFE[All Checks Passed Equals SAFE Design]
    FM2 --> SAFE
    FM3 --> SAFE
    FM4 --> SAFE
    FM5 --> SAFE
    FM6 --> SAFE
```

## 4.4 Component Identification Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Knuckle Joint Assembly] --> B1[1 Single Eye Component]
    A1 --> B2[2 Double Eye or Fork Component]
    A1 --> B3[3 Pin Component]
    A1 --> B4[4 Split Pin or Tapered Cotter]
    B1 --> C1[Forged from Mild Steel Rod]
    B2 --> C2[Forged from Mild Steel Rod]
    B3 --> C3[Hardened Medium Carbon Steel]
    B4 --> C4[Spring Steel or MS Wire]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"List any six modes of failure of a knuckle joint."**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. Tensile failure of the solid rod.
2. Shearing of the pin (single/double).
3. Crushing of the pin.
4. Crushing of the eye.
5. Tearing of the eye (tangential rupture behind the pin hole).
6. Shearing of the eye along the side planes of the pin hole.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"State the standard empirical proportions used in the design of a knuckle joint, defining all symbols."**

**Model Answer:**

For a knuckle joint, the following empirical relations (based on rod diameter $d$) are used:

- $d_p = d$ *(pin diameter equals rod diameter for equal strength)*
- $d_e = 2.5\,d$ *(outer diameter of the eye)*
- $t_1 = 1.5\,d$ *(thickness of the single eye)*
- $t_2 = 0.75\,d$ *(thickness of each fork prong)*

These proportions have been validated experimentally and are accepted in design codes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Design Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Using the wrong shear condition. In a knuckle joint, the pin is in **double shear** (not single shear) because the single eye is sandwiched between the two fork prongs. Many students incorrectly use the single-shear formula.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to round up the final dimensions to the next standard size. A 32.57 mm rod must be reported as 33 mm, not 32 mm or 32.57 mm.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–3 marks):** Skipping the verification of tearing and side-shear modes. The examiner expects at least one verification step.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Not mentioning the factor of safety in the final design statement.
> - **Common Mistake 5 (–2 marks):** Mixing up the empirical values with calculated values. Clearly label which value is empirical and which is from the design check.

---

### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set A]`

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]:** Design a knuckle joint to connect two mild-steel rods for a tensile load of $W = 40\,\text{kN}$. The permissible stresses are: $\sigma_t = 55\,\text{MPa}$, $\tau = 45\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_c = 90\,\text{MPa}$. Determine all principal dimensions and verify the design against tearing and side-shear modes.

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a):**

**Given:** $W = 40 \times 10^3\,\text{N}$, $\sigma_t = 55\,\text{MPa}$, $\tau = 45\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_c = 90\,\text{MPa}$.

**Step 1 — Rod diameter ($d$):**
$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4W}{\pi\,\sigma_t}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 40\,000}{\pi \times 55}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{160\,000}{172.79}} = \sqrt{926.04} = 30.43\,\text{mm}$$

Adopt $d = 31\,\text{mm}$ (rounded up).

**Step 2 — Pin diameter ($d_p$):**
By equal-strength rule: $d_p = d = 31\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 3 — Eye outer diameter ($d_e$):**
By empirical rule: $d_e = 2.5\,d = 2.5 \times 31 = 77.5\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 4 — Single-eye thickness ($t_1$):**
By empirical rule: $t_1 = 1.5\,d = 1.5 \times 31 = 46.5\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 5 — Fork thickness each side ($t_2$):**
$t_2 = 0.75\,d = 0.75 \times 31 = 23.25\,\text{mm}$. Adopt $t_2 = 23\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 6 — Verification against tearing of eye:**
$$W_{\text{tear}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\bigl(d_e^{\,2} - d_p^{\,2}\bigr)\sigma_t = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(77.5^2 - 31^2)(55)$$
$$= \dfrac{\pi}{4}(6006.25 - 961)(55) = 0.7854 \times 5045.25 \times 55 = 217\,932\,\text{N} = 217.93\,\text{kN}$$

Since $217.93\,\text{kN} \gg 40\,\text{kN}$, **the eye is safe against tearing**. Factor of safety = 217.93 / 40 = **5.45**. ✓

**Step 7 — Verification against side shear of eye:**
$$W_{\text{sh,eye}} = 2(d_e - d_p)\,t_1\,\tau = 2(77.5 - 31)(46.5)(45)$$
$$= 2 \times 46.5 \times 46.5 \times 45 = 194\,602.5\,\text{N} = 194.6\,\text{kN}$$

Factor of safety = 194.6 / 40 = **4.87**. ✓

**Final Answer (a):** $d = 31\,\text{mm}$, $d_p = 31\,\text{mm}$, $d_e = 77.5\,\text{mm}$, $t_1 = 46.5\,\text{mm}$, $t_2 = 23\,\text{mm}$. Design is safe.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]:** A knuckle joint has a rod diameter of 36 mm, pin diameter of 36 mm, and eye outer diameter of 90 mm. If the single-eye thickness is 54 mm, determine: (i) the safe axial tensile load the joint can carry, (ii) the factor of safety against tearing, given $\sigma_t = 60\,\text{MPa}$, $\tau = 42\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_c = 90\,\text{MPa}$. State the governing failure mode.

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b):**

**Given:** $d = 36\,\text{mm}$, $d_p = 36\,\text{mm}$, $d_e = 90\,\text{mm}$, $t_1 = 54\,\text{mm}$.

**Check 1 — Rod in tension:**
$$W_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,d^2\,\sigma_t = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(36^2)(60) = 0.7854 \times 1296 \times 60 = 61\,072\,\text{N} = 61.07\,\text{kN}$$

**Check 2 — Pin in double shear:**
$$W_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,d_p^{\,2}\,\dfrac{\tau}{0.5} = 0.5\,\pi\,d_p^{\,2}\,\tau = 0.5 \times \pi \times 36^2 \times 42$$
$$= 0.5 \times 3.1416 \times 1296 \times 42 = 85\,502\,\text{N} = 85.50\,\text{kN}$$

**Check 3 — Crushing on pin:**
$$W_3 = d_p \cdot t_1 \cdot \sigma_c = 36 \times 54 \times 90 = 174\,960\,\text{N} = 174.96\,\text{kN}$$

**Check 4 — Crushing on eye:**
$$W_4 = d_e \cdot t_1 \cdot \sigma_c = 90 \times 54 \times 90 = 437\,400\,\text{N} = 437.4\,\text{kN}$$

**Check 5 — Tearing of eye:**
$$W_5 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\bigl(d_e^{\,2} - d_p^{\,2}\bigr)\sigma_t = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(90^2 - 36^2)(60)$$
$$= 0.7854 \times (8100 - 1296) \times 60 = 0.7854 \times 6804 \times 60 = 320\,547\,\text{N} = 320.55\,\text{kN}$$

**Check 6 — Side shear of eye:**
$$W_6 = 2(d_e - d_p)\,t_1\,\tau = 2(90 - 36)(54)(42) = 2 \times 54 \times 54 \times 42 = 244\,944\,\text{N} = 244.94\,\text{kN}$$

**Safe load** = minimum of all = $\min(61.07, 85.50, 174.96, 437.4, 320.55, 244.94) = 61.07\,\text{kN}$.

**Governing failure mode:** Tension failure of the rod.

**Factor of safety** depends on the *applied* load; if the applied load is 61.07 kN, FoS = 1. For a typical working load of, say, $W_{\text{app}} = 30\,\text{kN}$:
$$\text{FoS} = 61.07 / 30 = 2.04$$

---

### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Set B]`

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]:** A knuckle joint is to be designed for a tractor-mounted cultivator tie-rod subjected to a fluctuating tensile load with mean value $W = 30\,\text{kN}$. Using the Soderberg or simple factor-of-safety approach, design the rod diameter, pin diameter, eye diameter, and eye thickness. Take $\sigma_t = 50\,\text{MPa}$, $\tau = 35\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_c = 75\,\text{MPa}$. Use a factor of safety $N = 4$.

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a):**

**Given:** $W = 30 \times 10^3\,\text{N}$, $N = 4$, $\sigma_t = 50\,\text{MPa}$, $\tau = 35\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_c = 75\,\text{MPa}$.

**Permissible (allowable) stresses for design:**
$$\sigma_{t,\text{perm}} = \sigma_t / N = 50/4 = 12.5\,\text{MPa}$$
$$\tau_{\text{perm}} = \tau / N = 35/4 = 8.75\,\text{MPa}$$
$$\sigma_{c,\text{perm}} = \sigma_c / N = 75/4 = 18.75\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 1 — Rod diameter ($d$):**
$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4W}{\pi\,\sigma_{t,\text{perm}}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 30\,000}{\pi \times 12.5}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{120\,000}{39.27}} = \sqrt{3055.77} = 55.28\,\text{mm}$$

Adopt $d = 56\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 2 — Pin diameter ($d_p$):**
By equal-strength rule: $d_p = d = 56\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 3 — Eye outer diameter ($d_e$):**
$d_e = 2.5\,d = 2.5 \times 56 = 140\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 4 — Single-eye thickness ($t_1$):**
$t_1 = 1.5\,d = 1.5 \times 56 = 84\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 5 — Fork thickness each side ($t_2$):**
$t_2 = 0.75\,d = 0.75 \times 56 = 42\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 6 — Pin length:**
$l = t_1 + 2\,t_2 + \text{allowance} = 84 + 84 + 12 = 180\,\text{mm}$.

**Final dimensions:** $d = 56\,\text{mm}$, $d_p = 56\,\text{mm}$, $d_e = 140\,\text{mm}$, $t_1 = 84\,\text{mm}$, $t_2 = 42\,\text{mm}$, $l = 180\,\text{mm}$.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare knuckle joints with cotter joints. State four advantages and two limitations of knuckle joints in agricultural machinery.

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (b):**

| Parameter | Knuckle Joint | Cotter Joint |
|---|---|---|
| Load type | Pure tension | Tension or compression |
| Articulation | Permits rotation | Rigid, no rotation |
| Component count | 4 (single eye, fork, pin, split pin) | 5 (socket, spigot, cotter, gib, collar) |
| Typical applications | Tractor linkages, tie-rods | Piston rod–cross head, valve mechanisms |
| Disassembly | Easy (pin removal) | Easy (cotter slide-out) |
| Strength under shock | Moderate | Higher (larger bearing area) |

**Four advantages of knuckle joint in agricultural machinery:**

1. **Simple and economical to manufacture** — can be forged from a single rod.
2. **Allows limited angular movement**, ideal for tractor-implement geometry.
3. **Easy assembly and disassembly** — pin can be removed for field repairs.
4. **Compact and lightweight** — suited to mobile agricultural equipment where weight minimisation matters.

**Two limitations:**

1. **Not suitable for compressive loads** — the pin would buckle/bend, not shear.
2. **Lower load capacity** than a cotter joint of similar size — limited by the pin's shear strength.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist (read 5 minutes before exam):**

- ☐ Knuckle joint is a **pinned joint for tensile load** with limited angular movement.
- ☐ **Six failure modes** to check: rod tension, pin shear (double!), pin crushing, eye crushing, eye tearing, eye side-shear.
- ☐ **Empirical proportions (memorise!):** $d_p = d$, $d_e = 2.5d$, $t_1 = 1.5d$, $t_2 = 0.75d$.
- ☐ **Pin is in double shear** — use $2W/\pi d_p^2$ not $W/\pi d_p^2$ (the #1 mistake).
- ☐ **Tearing area** is an annulus: $A = (\pi/4)(d_e^2 - d_p^2)$.
- ☐ **Round up** final dimensions to next standard size.
- ☐ **Verify** tearing and side-shear even after using empirical rules (carries 2–3 marks).
- ☐ **Factor of safety** typically 3–5 for agricultural machinery (shock loads).
- ☐ **Pin material** is usually harder (medium carbon) than the eye material (mild steel).
- ☐ **Split pin (cotter pin)** prevents axial sliding of the main pin under vibration.
- ☐ Agricultural applications: 3-point linkage, plough tie-rod, seed-drill drawbar, trailer hitch, sprayer boom, cultivator frame.
- ☐ Knuckle joint is **NOT** for compression, torsion, or large rotations — use universal joint or cotter joint instead.
- ☐ Always **state the governing failure mode** in your final answer (the mode giving the lowest safe load).
- ☐ Pin length formula: $l = 2 t_2 + t_1 + \text{head} + \text{split-pin allowance}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turnbuckle, Design of Welded Joints Subjected to Static Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT604 - DESIGN OF AGRICULTURAL IMPLEMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Turnbuckle, Design of Welded Joints Subjected to Static Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Turnbuckle — Formal Definition

A **turnbuckle** is a mechanical coupling device consisting of a metallic central frame (body) with two internally threaded, opposite-handed bores, into which two externally threaded rods (or eye-bolts, hooks, or jaws) are screwed. One rod carries a **right-hand thread** and the other carries a **left-hand thread**, so that rotating the central frame simultaneously advances or retracts both rods, thereby adjusting the tension or length of the assembly without rotating the end fittings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBAGT604, Module 1):**
> *A turnbuckle is a tension-adjusting device used in agricultural machinery for tightening cables, chains, tie-rods, and bracing members. It is treated as a threaded coupling whose design must resist tensile and shear loads transmitted through its body and threads.*

**Standard Terminology (as per IS 1367 & Machine Design texts):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Body / Frame** | Central hexagonal or oval piece with two opposite-handed threaded holes |
| **Eye Bolt / Rod End** | Externally threaded terminal fitting (eye, hook, or jaw) |
| **Lock Nut** | Prevents accidental loosening under vibration |
| **Take-Up Length** | Total adjustability provided by the device |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Handed Screw Jack"

Imagine you are pulling a rope tight between two posts. If you simply tie a knot, you cannot tighten it further once tied. Now imagine you insert a small metal sleeve in the middle of the rope that has **two screws going in opposite directions** — one screwing inward (right-hand) and one outward (left-hand). When you **rotate the sleeve like a wing nut**, both ends of the rope are pulled inward simultaneously, just like pulling your hands together to tighten a string. That sleeve is a **turnbuckle**.

In agricultural machinery, the same principle tightens the **bracing wires of a tractor-mounted plough**, the **check chains of a cultivator**, the **tension rods of a disc harrow gang**, and the **stay wires of a greenhouse structure**.

### 1.3 Welded Joints Subjected to Static Loads — Formal Definition

A **welded joint** is a permanent joint formed by localized fusion of the base metals (with or without filler material) at the interface. When the joint is loaded by forces that do **not fluctuate with time** (i.e., their magnitude and direction remain constant), it is classified as being **subjected to static loads**. The design of such joints involves calculating the **throat area, shear stress, and combined stress** at the weld interface to ensure the joint can safely transmit the applied load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Classification Used by KTU Examiners:**
> 1. **Fillet Weld** — Triangular cross-section joining two surfaces at right angles (most common).
> 2. **Butt Weld** — End-to-end fusion of two plates in the same plane.
> 3. **Plug / Slot Weld** — Used to supplement fillet welds or for lap joints.
>
> For Module 1 (Static Loads), **fillet welds under direct shear, transverse loading, and bending** form the core analytical content.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Iron Tailor's Stitch"

Think of a tailor stitching two pieces of cloth. The thread (filler metal) runs along the seam (weld line) and holds the cloth together by friction and interlocking. If the cloth pieces are pulled sideways (parallel loading), the thread experiences **shear**. If they are pulled apart perpendicular to the seam (transverse loading), the thread still experiences shear but across the full throat. A weld works identically — every load, no matter the direction, ultimately produces **shear stresses on the weld throat**.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Permissible shear stress for mild-steel fillet weld (static): **$100 \text{ N/mm}^2$**
> - Permissible tensile stress for butt weld (static): **$140 \text{ N/mm}^2$**
> - Permissible shear stress for structural-steel weld: **$110 \text{ N/mm}^2$**
> - Throat thickness for equal-leg fillet weld of leg size $s$: **$t = 0.707\,s$**
> - Standard mild-steel ultimate tensile strength: **$400 \text{ N/mm}^2$**
> - Factor of Safety for welded agricultural structures: **$3$ to $5$**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Turnbuckle — Operating Principle and Design Logic

A turnbuckle operates on the **principle of differential screw motion**. Because the two internal threads in the body are of opposite hand, rotating the body by one full turn causes **both rods to move inward (or outward) by exactly one pitch length** simultaneously. This is what makes the device an efficient tensioner.

#### Design Steps for a Turnbuckle

1. **Determine the maximum working load** $W$ acting along the axis (tension in the cable/rod).
2. **Select the material** for the body and the end rods (commonly Mild Steel or Medium Carbon Steel, IS 2062 grade).
3. **Calculate the core (root) diameter** of the threaded rod using the permissible tensile stress $\sigma_t$:

$$d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4\,W}{\pi\,\sigma_t}}$$

4. **Choose the nominal diameter** $d$ from the standard thread table such that $d > d_c$.
5. **Verify thread shear stress** at the root of the threads using:

$$\tau = \dfrac{W}{\pi\,d_c\,n\,p}$$

   where $n$ is the number of engaged threads and $p$ is the pitch.
6. **Design the body (frame)** for tensile and shear load transfer. The body experiences:
   - **Tensile stress** along its longitudinal axis.
   - **Shear stress** on the threaded bore wall.
7. **Check for buckling** of the end rods if they are long and slender.
8. **Specify lock nuts** at both ends to prevent rotation-induced loosening under field vibration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in Agriculture:**
> - Tightening **draft control linkages** on tractors.
> - Adjusting **check chains** on mould-board ploughs to control side draft.
> - Tensioning **wire bracing** on paddy transplanters and greenhouses.
> - Adjusting **lift rod length** on three-point hitches.
> - Cable stay adjustment on **trailer-mounted combine harvesters**.

#### Failure Modes of a Turnbuckle

| Failure Mode | Location | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| Thread stripping | Internal threads of body | Insufficient engaged length |
| Tensile fracture | Rod shank | Under-design of root diameter |
| Body cracking | Central frame between bores | Stress concentration at thread run-out |
| Bending fatigue | Eye-bolt shank | Misalignment with load line |
| Loosening under vibration | Threaded interface | Absence of lock nut |

---

### 2.2 Welded Joints Under Static Loads — Theory and Assumptions

#### 2.2.1 Fillet Weld Geometry

A fillet weld of equal leg size $s$ forms an approximately **isosceles right triangle** at its cross-section.

- **Leg size** $s$ — the length of each side of the triangle along the parent metal surfaces.
- **Throat thickness** $t$ — the shortest distance from the weld root to the weld face.

For an equal-leg fillet weld:

$$t = s \cos 45^\circ = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\,s$$

- **Throat area** for a weld of length $l$:

$$A_t = 0.707\,s\,l$$

#### 2.2.2 Loading Categories on Fillet Welds

| Load Type | Description | Failure Plane |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallel / Longitudinal** | Load acts along the weld length | Shear on throat |
| **Transverse** | Load acts perpendicular to weld length | Shear on throat (but higher strength) |
| **Combined** | Direct shear + bending moment | Principal stress at throat |

#### 2.2.3 Strength of a Fillet Weld Under Direct Shear

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{0.707\,s\,l}$$

For safe design:

$$\tau \leq \tau_{\text{perm}}$$

The **required weld length** is therefore:

$$l = \dfrac{F}{0.707\,s\,\tau_{\text{perm}}}$$

This length must be provided **on each side** if the joint is a lap joint with welds on both edges.

#### 2.2.4 Welded Joint Subjected to Bending Moment

For a welded joint loaded by a bending moment $M$, the throat stress at the extreme fibre is:

$$\tau = \dfrac{M\,y}{I_w}$$

where the **moment of inertia of the weld group** $I_w$ is computed by treating each weld as a line of thickness $t = 0.707\,s$.

For a single transverse fillet weld (length $l$, throat $t$) bending about its centroid:

$$I_w = \dfrac{t\,l^3}{12}$$

Hence:

$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{6\,M}{t\,l^2}$$

#### 2.2.5 Welded Joint Subjected to Torsion

For a circular weld group (e.g., welded pipe flange) of radius $r$ and throat $t$:

$$I_p = 2\pi\,r^3\,t$$

$$\tau = \dfrac{T\,r}{I_p} = \dfrac{T}{2\pi\,r^2\,t}$$

#### 2.2.6 Eccentrically Loaded Welded Joint

When the load $P$ does not pass through the centroid of the weld group at an eccentricity $e$, the resultant throat stress at any point is the vector sum of:
- **Direct shear:** $\tau_d = P/A_w$
- **Shear due to moment $M = P \cdot e$:** $\tau_m = M \cdot r / J_w$

The **maximum shear stress** at the farthest point from the centroid:

$$\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \tau_m^2 + 2\,\tau_d\,\tau_m\,\cos\theta}$$

For most KTU problems, this simplifies to:

$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{P}{A_w} + \dfrac{P\,e\,r_{\max}}{J_w}$$

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you will need for Module 1 problems on Turnbuckles and Welded Joints (Static Loads).

| S.No. | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Turnbuckle rod core diameter | $d_c = \sqrt{4W/(\pi\sigma_t)}$ | $W$ in N, $\sigma_t$ in N/mm², $d_c$ in mm |
| 2 | Thread shear stress | $\tau = W/(\pi d_c n p)$ | $n$ = no. of engaged threads, $p$ = pitch (mm) |
| 3 | Fillet weld throat thickness | $t = 0.707\,s$ | $s$ = leg size (mm) |
| 4 | Throat area | $A_t = 0.707\,s\,l$ | $l$ = weld length (mm) |
| 5 | Direct shear stress in fillet weld | $\tau = F/(0.707\,s\,l)$ | $F$ in N, result in N/mm² |
| 6 | Required weld length | $l = F/(0.707\,s\,\tau_{\text{perm}})$ | — |
| 7 | Bending stress in weld (transverse) | $\tau_{\max} = 6M/(t\,l^2)$ | $M$ in N·mm |
| 8 | Torsional shear in circular weld | $\tau = T/(2\pi r^2 t)$ | $r$ = radius of weld group (mm) |
| 9 | Eccentric loading resultant shear | $\tau = P/A_w + P e r_{\max}/J_w$ | $A_w = 0.707 s \sum l_i$ |
| 10 | Polar moment of weld group | $J_w = I_x + I_y$ | For symmetric groups |

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Agricultural Engineering

Welded joints are the **backbone of every agricultural implement frame** — from the **tool bar of a cultivator** to the **main frame of a tractor-mounted combine harvester**. Turnbuckles appear in nearly every **adjustable bracing system** on tillage, sowing, and harvesting equipment. A student who masters these two topics can directly design and validate field-ready machinery for the Kerala agricultural sector (paddy transplanters, coconut climbers, spice dryers, etc.).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Fillet Weld Throat Thickness

**Given:** A fillet weld with equal leg size $s$ forms an isosceles right triangle when viewed in cross-section.

**To Prove:** $t = 0.707\,s$

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
The weld cross-section has two legs of length $s$ meeting at a $90^\circ$ angle. The throat is the perpendicular distance from the root (where the two parent-metal surfaces meet) to the hypotenuse (the weld face).

**Step 2 — Apply trigonometry.**
The angle between the leg and the throat is $45^\circ$ (since the triangle is right-angled and isosceles).

$$\cos 45^\circ = \dfrac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \dfrac{t}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$\cos 45^\circ = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{1}{1.414} \approx 0.707$$

Therefore:

$$t = s \cos 45^\circ = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707\,s \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Direct Shear Stress in a Parallel Fillet Weld

**Given:** A lap joint with two parallel fillet welds (one on each side of the overlap), each of length $l$, leg size $s$, carrying an axial tensile load $F$.

**To Derive:** Expression for the average shear stress on the weld throat.

**Step 1 — Identify the load path.**
The applied load $F$ is transmitted entirely through the **two weld throats** (assuming the parent metal is stronger than the weld). Each weld carries an equal share, so:

$$F_1 = F_2 = \dfrac{F}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Express the throat area of one weld.**

$$A_{t1} = 0.707\,s\,l$$

**Step 3 — Apply the shear stress definition.**

$$\tau = \dfrac{F_1}{A_{t1}} = \dfrac{F/2}{0.707\,s\,l} = \dfrac{F}{1.414\,s\,l}$$

**Step 4 — Generalise for $n$ parallel welds.**

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{n \cdot 0.707\,s\,l} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Interpretation:** Doubling the number of welds (e.g., placing welds on both edges of a lap joint) halves the shear stress — this is the designer's most direct lever for strength enhancement.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Bending Stress in a Transverse Fillet Weld

**Given:** Two plates connected by a transverse fillet weld of length $l$ (along the line of action of the moment) and leg size $s$, subjected to a pure bending moment $M$ about an axis perpendicular to the weld length.

**Step 1 — Treat the weld as a line of thickness $t = 0.707\,s$ at the joint plane.**

The cross-sectional "second moment of area" of the weld line is:

$$I_w = \dfrac{t\,l^3}{12}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the flexure formula at the extreme fibre ($y = l/2$).**

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{M\,y}{I_w} = \dfrac{M\,(l/2)}{t\,l^3 / 12} = \dfrac{6\,M}{t\,l^2}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to throat shear stress (since welds fail in shear).**

$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{6\,M}{t\,l^2} = \dfrac{6\,M}{0.707\,s\,l^2} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 1 — Turnbuckle Design

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):**
A turnbuckle is used to tension a wire rope of a tractor plough bracing system. The maximum tension in the rope is **$15 \text{ kN}$**. Design the turnbuckle end rods using Mild Steel of permissible tensile stress **$\sigma_t = 60 \text{ N/mm}^2$**. Assume the thread pitch is **$4 \text{ mm}$** and verify the thread shear stress.

**Step 1 — Compute the core diameter of the rod.**

$$d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4\,W}{\pi\,\sigma_t}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 15\,000}{\pi \times 60}}$$

$$d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{60\,000}{188.496}} = \sqrt{318.31}$$

$$d_c \approx 17.84 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Select standard nominal diameter.**
Adopt **$d = 20 \text{ mm}$** (standard ISO metric coarse thread).

**Step 3 — Determine engaged thread count.**
Standard thread engagement length is taken as the **thickness of the turnbuckle body**, typically $1.5\,d$ for steel-on-steel. Hence engaged length $L_e = 1.5 \times 20 = 30 \text{ mm}$.

$$n = \dfrac{L_e}{p} = \dfrac{30}{4} = 7.5 \approx 7 \text{ threads}$$

**Step 4 — Verify thread shear stress.**

$$\tau = \dfrac{W}{\pi\,d_c\,n\,p} = \dfrac{15\,000}{\pi \times 17.84 \times 7 \times 4}$$

$$\tau = \dfrac{15\,000}{1568.5} \approx 9.56 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compare with permissible value.**
Permissible thread shear for mild steel (per IS 1367 / standard Machine Design practice) is approximately **$30 \text{ to } 40 \text{ N/mm}^2$**.

Since $9.56 \ll 30$, the design is **safe**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Welded Lap Joint Under Static Load

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
Two mild-steel plates of thickness **$10 \text{ mm}$** are joined by parallel fillet welds on both edges of a lap joint. The joint carries a static tensile load of **$80 \text{ kN}$**. Permissible shear stress in the weld is **$100 \text{ N/mm}^2$**. Determine the **size and length of the weld**.

**Step 1 — Choose weld leg size.**
The leg size should not exceed the **thickness of the thinner plate minus 1.5 mm** (rule of thumb to avoid burning through).

$$s_{\max} = 10 - 1.5 = 8.5 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt standard size: **$s = 8 \text{ mm}$**.

**Step 2 — Compute required total throat area.**

$$A_t = \dfrac{F}{\tau_{\text{perm}}} = \dfrac{80\,000}{100} = 800 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute total weld length.**

$$l_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{A_t}{0.707\,s} = \dfrac{800}{0.707 \times 8} = \dfrac{800}{5.656}$$

$$l_{\text{total}} \approx 141.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Split between two welds.**
Provide one weld on each side of the lap:

$$l = \dfrac{l_{\text{total}}}{2} = \dfrac{141.4}{2} = 70.7 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt standard length: **$l = 75 \text{ mm}$ on each side**.

**Step 5 — Verification.**

$$\tau_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{F}{2 \times 0.707 \times s \times l} = \dfrac{80\,000}{2 \times 0.707 \times 8 \times 75}$$

$$\tau_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{80\,000}{848.4} \approx 94.3 \text{ N/mm}^2 < 100 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{SAFE} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.6 Worked Example 3 — Eccentrically Loaded Welded Bracket

**Problem Statement:**
A cantilever bracket of thickness **$12 \text{ mm}$** is welded to a column by **two vertical fillet welds of length $100 \text{ mm}$ each**, spaced **$150 \text{ mm}$ apart** centre-to-centre. A vertical load of **$20 \text{ kN}$** is applied at a horizontal eccentricity of **$200 \text{ mm}$** from the weld centroid. The weld leg size is **$8 \text{ mm}$**. Permissible shear stress is **$110 \text{ N/mm}^2$**. Check the safety of the weld.

**Step 1 — Compute throat thickness and weld area.**

$$t = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.656 \text{ mm}$$

$$A_w = 2 \times t \times l = 2 \times 5.656 \times 100 = 1131.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute direct shear stress.**

$$\tau_d = \dfrac{P}{A_w} = \dfrac{20\,000}{1131.2} \approx 17.68 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute bending moment on weld group.**

$$M = P \times e = 20\,000 \times 200 = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute polar moment of weld group.**
For two parallel lines spaced $b = 150$ mm apart, each of length $l = 100$ mm:

$$I_x = 2 \times \dfrac{t\,l^3}{12} = 2 \times \dfrac{5.656 \times 100^3}{12} = 9.43 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$I_y = 2 \times t\,l \times \left(\dfrac{b}{2}\right)^2 = 2 \times 5.656 \times 100 \times 75^2 = 6.36 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$J_w = I_x + I_y = 9.43 \times 10^5 + 6.36 \times 10^6 = 7.30 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 5 — Compute moment-induced shear at the farthest point.**
Maximum distance from centroid: $r_{\max} = \sqrt{(l/2)^2 + (b/2)^2} = \sqrt{50^2 + 75^2} = \sqrt{2500 + 5625} = \sqrt{8125} \approx 90.14$ mm.

$$\tau_m = \dfrac{M\,r_{\max}}{J_w} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^6 \times 90.14}{7.30 \times 10^6} \approx 49.4 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Combine (worst-case linear summation).**

$$\tau_{\max} = \tau_d + \tau_m = 17.68 + 49.4 = 67.08 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Since $67.08 < 110$ N/mm², the weld is **SAFE**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Parameter | Turnbuckle | Welded Joint |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Tension adjustment | Permanent load transfer |
| **Standard strength equation** | $d_c = \sqrt{4W/(\pi\sigma_t)}$ | $\tau = F/(0.707\,s\,l)$ |
| **Critical stress type** | Tensile (rod), shear (thread) | Shear (throat) |
| **Common failure** | Thread stripping, rod fracture | Throat shear, crater cracking |
| **Inspection method** | Visual + thread gauge | Visual + dye-penetrant |
| **Field replaceability** | Yes (loosen, rotate, re-tighten) | No (must cut and re-weld) |
| **Cost-effectiveness for agri-implements** | High for adjustable members | High for rigid frame joints |

---

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Turnbuckle — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Eye Bolt Left Hand Thread"] --> B["Central Frame Body"]
    B --> C["Eye Bolt Right Hand Thread"]
    A --> D["Cable or Rod End 1"]
    C --> E["Cable or Rod End 2"]
    F["Rotational Input"] --> B
    G["Lock Nut Left"] --> A
    H["Lock Nut Right"] --> C
    I["Tensioned Member Tractor Plough"] --> D
    J["Tensioned Member Implement Frame"] --> E
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram shows how rotation of the central frame simultaneously moves both eye-bolts axially, thereby increasing or decreasing the overall length and tightening the connected cables/rods.

---

### 4.2 Welded Lap Joint — Load Path Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Applied Tensile Load P"] --> L1["Upper Plate Thickness 10 mm"]
    L1 --> W1["Fillet Weld Left Side Throat 0.707s"]
    W1 --> L2["Lower Plate Thickness 10 mm"]
    L2 --> W2["Fillet Weld Right Side Throat 0.707s"]
    W2 --> P2["Reaction Force at Support"]
    L1 -.-> S1["Shear Stress tau equals P over 1.414 s l"]
    W1 -.-> S2["Failure Plane 45 degrees through throat"]
```

---

### 4.3 Weld Group Under Eccentric Load — Stress Combination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Eccentric Load P at distance e"] --> B["Direct Shear tau d equals P over Aw"]
    A --> C["Moment M equals P times e"]
    C --> D["Moment Induced Shear tau m equals M r over Jw"]
    B --> E["Vector Combination at Farthest Point"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Maximum Shear Stress tau max"]
    F --> G{"Compare with tau perm"}
    G -- Less than --> H["DESIGN SAFE"]
    G -- Greater than --> I["INCREASE WELD SIZE OR LENGTH"]
```

---

### 4.4 Stress Distribution in a Transverse Fillet Weld Under Bending

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M["Bending Moment M Applied"] --> F["Extreme Fibre Stress sigma b equals 6M over t l squared"]
    F --> N["Neutral Axis at Weld Centroid"]
    F --> P["Maximum Tension at Top Fibre"]
    F --> Q["Maximum Compression at Bottom Fibre"]
    P --> R["Convert to Throat Shear tau max"]
    Q --> R
    R --> S["Compare with Permissible Shear tau perm equals 100 N per mm squared"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C["Engaged Length L sub e"]
    C --> D["Internal Threads in Turnbuckle Body"]
    D --> E["Number of Engaged Threads n equals L sub e over p"]
    E --> F["Thread Shear Stress tau equals W over pi d sub c n p"]
    F --> G["Compare with tau perm"]
    G -- OK --> H["DESIGN ACCEPTED"]
    G -- Not OK --> I["INCREASE BODY THICKNESS OR USE HIGHER GRADE STEEL"]
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Define a turnbuckle. List any four applications of turnbuckles in agricultural machinery.*

**Model Answer:**
A turnbuckle is a mechanical tension-adjusting device consisting of a central frame with two internally threaded bores of opposite hand (one right-hand and one left-hand), into which two externally threaded rods or eye-bolts are screwed. Rotation of the frame moves both rods axially in opposite directions, thereby adjusting tension.

**Four applications in agricultural machinery:**
1. Tensioning draft control linkages of tractors.
2. Adjusting check chains on mould-board ploughs.
3. Tightening bracing cables on combine harvesters.
4. Take-up adjustment on three-point hitch lift rods.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for applications)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*What is meant by "throat thickness" of a fillet weld? Why is it considered the critical dimension in design?*

**Model Answer:**
The **throat thickness** $t$ of a fillet weld is the perpendicular distance from the weld root (intersection of the two parent-metal surfaces) to the weld face (hypotenuse of the triangular weld cross-section). For an equal-leg fillet weld of leg size $s$, $t = 0.707\,s$.

It is the **critical dimension** because failure of the weld under static loading occurs by **shear along the throat plane**, and the area resisting this shear is directly proportional to $t$. All KTU design equations for fillet welds use $t$ as the effective thickness.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for definition with formula, 1.5 for justification)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, PBAGT604]**

**(a)** A turnbuckle is used in the bracing system of a tractor-mounted disc plough. The maximum tension in the bracing cable is **$25 \text{ kN}$**. Design the end rods of the turnbuckle using Mild Steel with permissible tensile stress **$\sigma_t = 56 \text{ N/mm}^2$** and permissible shear stress **$\tau = 32 \text{ N/mm}^2$**. Assume the thread pitch is **$5 \text{ mm}$** and engaged length equals **$1.5\,d$**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two mild-steel plates of thickness **$12 \text{ mm}$** are lap-jointed using parallel fillet welds on both edges. The joint transmits a static load of **$90 \text{ kN}$**. Permissible shear stress in the weld is **$100 \text{ N/mm}^2$**. Determine the **weld leg size and length**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Core diameter from tensile strength.**

$$d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 25\,000}{\pi \times 56}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{100\,000}{175.93}} = \sqrt{568.5} \approx 23.84 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Standard nominal diameter.**
Adopt **$d = 27 \text{ mm}$** (ISO metric M27 coarse thread, root diameter ≈ 23.75 mm, satisfying requirement).

**Step 3 — Engaged length and thread count.**

$$L_e = 1.5 \times 27 = 40.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$n = \dfrac{L_e}{p} = \dfrac{40.5}{5} = 8.1 \approx 8 \text{ threads}$$

**Step 4 — Thread shear stress check.**

$$\tau = \dfrac{W}{\pi d_c n p} = \dfrac{25\,000}{\pi \times 23.84 \times 8 \times 5} = \dfrac{25\,000}{2996.4} \approx 8.34 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Safety verification.**
Since $8.34 \ll 32$ N/mm², design is safe. **Design accepted.**

*(7 marks — 2 for core diameter, 2 for nominal selection, 2 for shear check, 1 for final statement)*

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Maximum permissible leg size.**
$s_{\max} = t_{\text{plate}} - 1.5 = 12 - 1.5 = 10.5$ mm. Adopt **$s = 10 \text{ mm}$**.

**Step 2 — Total throat area required.**

$$A_t = \dfrac{90\,000}{100} = 900 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total weld length.**

$$l_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{900}{0.707 \times 10} = \dfrac{900}{7.07} \approx 127.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Length per weld (two welds).**

$$l = \dfrac{127.3}{2} \approx 63.7 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt **$l = 70 \text{ mm}$ on each side** for safety and round-off.

**Step 5 — Verification.**

$$\tau_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{90\,000}{2 \times 0.707 \times 10 \times 70} = \dfrac{90\,000}{989.8} \approx 90.93 \text{ N/mm}^2 < 100 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{SAFE}$$

*(7 marks — 1 for leg size selection, 2 for area/length derivation, 2 for split between welds, 2 for verification)*

---

#### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**(a)** A welded cantilever bracket is attached to a column by **two vertical fillet welds of length $120 \text{ mm}$ each, spaced $180 \text{ mm}$ centre-to-centre**. A vertical load of **$30 \text{ kN}$** acts at a horizontal eccentricity of **$250 \text{ mm}$**. Weld leg size is **$10 \text{ mm}$**. Using permissible shear stress **$110 \text{ N/mm}^2$**, check the safety of the joint. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a turnbuckle. List the **standard materials** used for its body and end rods. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Throat and weld area.**

$$t = 0.707 \times 10 = 7.07 \text{ mm}$$

$$A_w = 2 \times t \times l = 2 \times 7.07 \times 120 = 1696.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Direct shear.**

$$\tau_d = \dfrac{30\,000}{1696.8} \approx 17.68 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Moment.**

$$M = 30\,000 \times 250 = 7.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4 — Polar moment of weld group.**

$$I_x = 2 \times \dfrac{t\,l^3}{12} = 2 \times \dfrac{7.07 \times 120^3}{12} = 2.04 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$I_y = 2 \times t\,l \times (b/2)^2 = 2 \times 7.07 \times 120 \times 90^2 = 13.74 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$J_w = 15.78 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 5 — Maximum radius.**

$$r_{\max} = \sqrt{(l/2)^2 + (b/2)^2} = \sqrt{60^2 + 90^2} = \sqrt{11\,700} \approx 108.17 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Moment-induced shear.**

$$\tau_m = \dfrac{7.5 \times 10^6 \times 108.17}{15.78 \times 10^6} \approx 51.4 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 7 — Combined.**

$$\tau_{\max} = 17.68 + 51.4 \approx 69.08 \text{ N/mm}^2 < 110 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{SAFE}$$

*(7 marks — distributed across steps 1–7)*

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Construction:**
A turnbuckle consists of:
1. A **central body (frame)** — usually hexagonal or oval-shaped — with two **internally threaded bores**, one with right-hand thread and the other with left-hand thread.
2. **Two end fittings** — eye-bolts, hooks, or jaws — each with an external thread matching the corresponding bore.
3. **Two lock nuts** to prevent loosening under vibration.

**Working:**
When the body is rotated about its own axis, the two opposite-handed threads cause the end fittings to **move simultaneously inward or outward** by exactly one pitch per revolution. This shortens or lengthens the overall assembly, thereby **increasing or decreasing tension** in the cable/rod connected between the two end fittings.

**Materials:**
- **Body:** Mild Steel (IS 2062 Grade A), Forged Carbon Steel, or Drop-Forged Alloy Steel.
- **End rods/eye-bolts:** Medium Carbon Steel (EN8 / EN9), drop-forged and heat-treated.
- **Surface finish:** Hot-dip galvanizing or zinc plating for corrosion resistance in field conditions.

*(7 marks — 2 for sketch, 2 for construction, 2 for working, 1 for materials)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 0.707** — Many students compute weld area as $s \cdot l$ instead of $0.707\,s\,l$. This error underestimates the throat area and overestimates shear stress, leading to unnecessarily large welds or, worse, unsafe designs.
> 2. **Ignoring the load split between two welds** — In lap joints with welds on both edges, students often use $F$ instead of $F/2$ in shear calculation.
> 3. **Forgetting to compute polar moment $J_w$ correctly** — For non-symmetric weld groups, students must compute both $I_x$ and $I_y$ and add them. Skipping $I_y$ is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Turnbuckle thread count error** — Using $L_e = d$ instead of $L_e = 1.5\,d$ underestimates thread engagement and overstates thread shear stress.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing kN with N/mm² without proper conversion. Always write full units in every step to avoid the 0.5–1 mark deduction that KTU examiners routinely apply.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**
>
> - A **turnbuckle** is a differential-screw device that uses **opposite-handed threads** to adjust tension.
> - Design the **core diameter** of end rods from tensile stress: $d_c = \sqrt{4W/(\pi\sigma_t)}$.
> - Always verify **thread shear stress** using $\tau = W/(\pi d_c n p)$.
> - Provide **lock nuts** at both ends in all agricultural field applications to counter vibration.
> - For a **fillet weld**, the **throat thickness** $t = 0.707\,s$ is the failure-critical dimension.
> - Direct shear stress in fillet weld: $\tau = F/(0.707\,s\,l)$.
> - Always choose **$s \leq t_{\text{plate}} - 1.5 \text{ mm}$** to avoid burning through the parent metal.
> - For **bending on transverse fillet weld**: $\tau_{\max} = 6M/(0.707\,s\,l^2)$.
> - For **eccentric loading**, compute **direct shear + moment-induced shear** and sum them (worst-case).
> - Standard permissible stresses (mild steel welds, static): $\tau = 100$ N/mm², $\sigma_t = 140$ N/mm².
> - In KTU answers, **always state the chosen standard size** (e.g., M20, 8 mm leg, etc.) after design calculation.
> - Convert all loads to **Newtons (N)** and all stresses to **N/mm²** before substitution.
> - Draw a **neat dimensioned sketch** in every 7-mark answer — it alone can fetch 1–2 marks even if calculations have minor errors.
> - For weld groups, treat each weld as a **line of thickness $t = 0.707\,s$** and apply standard flexure/torsion formulas.

---
