> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT604 - DESIGN OF AGRICULTURAL IMPLEMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Threaded Fasteners, Bolted Joints (Shear & Eccentric), Shafts (Torsion & Combined), Keys, Levers, Couplings (Jaw/Sleeve/Flange), Springs (Helical & Leaf), Belt Drives (Flat & V-Belt) and Pulleys

---

# MODULE 2 — DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Design of Threaded Fasteners Subjected to Direct Static Loads

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
A *threaded fastener* is a mechanical device (bolt, screw, stud) having external or internal helical ridges (threads) used to clamp two or more components together by converting an applied axial tensile force into a clamping (preload) force. Under *direct static loading*, the fastener axis aligns with the line of action of the external tensile load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Static loading implies negligible fatigue, impact, or vibration. The dominant failure modes considered are **(i) tensile failure of the bolt shank**, and **(ii) failure of the threads (shear at root)**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine pulling two wooden planks apart with your hands, but they are held together by a strong metal wire wrapped in a helical coil around a rod. As you tighten the rod, the wire squeezes the planks together. The pulling force tries to stretch and break the rod — this is the *tensile load* on the fastener. The "squeezing" force is the *preload*.

**Key Physical Constants (IS 1367 / ISO 898):**
- Proof strength of steel bolt ($R_p$): **$\mathbf{600 \text{ MPa}}$** (for property class 8.8)
- Ultimate tensile strength ($R_m$): **$\mathbf{800 \text{ MPa}}$** (for 8.8)
- Factor of safety against yielding ($n$): typically **1.5 to 3**
- Standard metric coarse thread pitch (M10): **$\mathbf{1.5 \text{ mm}}$**

---

### 1.2 Bolted Joints Loaded in Shear

**Formal Definition:**
A bolted joint loaded in *shear* is one in which the external load acts perpendicular to the bolt axis, attempting to slide the clamped members across each other. The bolt shank resists this by transverse shear at the interface plane.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of two steel plates overlapping, joined by a single bolt at the centre. If you push one plate sideways relative to the other, the bolt acts like the hinge pin of a pair of scissors — its body tries to be cut across. This is *shear loading*.

> [!NOTE]
> In *tractor plough-bolt connections* and *harvester frame joints*, shear loading is the most common loading mode.

---

### 1.3 Bolted Joints Subjected to Eccentric Loading

**Formal Definition:**
A bolted joint is subjected to *eccentric loading* when the line of action of the applied load does not pass through the centroid of the bolt group. This creates **two simultaneous effects**: (i) a direct shear force and (ii) a *torsional moment* that tends to rotate the joint about the centroid of the bolt pattern.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Picture trying to unscrew a tight bottle cap. Your hand grips far from the centre. This creates a *twisting moment*. Similarly, in a bolted joint, an eccentric pull both translates AND rotates the connection — every bolt shares the burden.

---

### 1.4 Design of Shafts under Torsion and Combined Bending and Torsion

**Formal Definition:**
A *shaft* is a rotating machine element, usually of circular cross-section, used to transmit power (torque) between a driving and a driven member. Under combined loading, the shaft experiences *bending moment* $(M_b)$ due to transverse loads (gears, pulleys) AND *torsional moment* $(M_t)$ simultaneously.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A tractor PTO (Power Take-Off) shaft: one end receives torque from the engine, while the middle has a pulley with belt tension pulling it sideways. The shaft is being twisted AND bent — like wringing a towel while simultaneously bending it.

**Key Standard (IS 1161):**
- Allowable shear stress for mild steel shafts: **$\mathbf{35 \text{ to } 45 \text{ MPa}}$**

---

### 1.5 Design of Keys

**Formal Definition:**
A *key* is a small, usually rectangular, metal piece inserted between a shaft and a hub (gear, pulley) to prevent relative rotational motion by positive mechanical engagement.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a key inserted in a lock. The key fits in a slot (keyway) cut into the shaft, and a matching slot is cut into the hub. When the shaft rotates, the key pushes the hub to rotate with it — preventing slip.

---

### 1.6 Design of Lever

**Formal Definition:**
A *lever* is a rigid bar pivoted about a fulcrum, used to multiply the applied effort force to overcome a larger load. In machine design, levers are *hand levers, foot levers, brake levers, clutch levers* — ubiquitous in agricultural machinery (tractor brake pedal, seed-cum-fertilizer drill lever).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A *see-saw* on a playground. A small child sits on the long side, an adult on the short side — and they balance. The mechanical advantage comes from the *ratio of distances* from the fulcrum.

---

### 1.7 Design of Couplings — Jaw, Sleeve, Rigid Flange

**Formal Definition:**
A *coupling* is a device used to connect two shafts end-to-end for the purpose of transmitting power, while permitting (in some designs) misalignment, shock absorption, or disconnection.

| Type | Function | Application |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Muff/Sleeve Coupling** | Simple, rigid, in-line | Light-duty pumps, small fans |
| **Jaw (Claw) Coupling** | Flexible, transmits torque through jaws | Agricultural PTO, conveyors |
| **Rigid Flange Coupling** | Heavy-duty, permanently bolted | Compressors, rolling mills |

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A sleeve coupling is like two pieces of a broom handle joined by a tight-fitting sleeve. A jaw coupling is like two interlocking hand-clasps. A flange coupling is like two plates bolted face-to-face.

---

### 1.8 Design of Helical and Leaf Springs

**Formal Definition:**
A *spring* is an elastic machine element that stores mechanical energy when loaded and releases it upon unloading. Two major types relevant here:

- **Helical Spring**: A wire wound in a helix. Loaded axially in *torsion* of the wire (which is *bending* of the helix). Used in suspension, valves, clutches.
- **Leaf Spring**: A stack of flat, tapered or semi-elliptical strips (leaves) clamped together. Used in vehicle suspensions (tractor-trailers, bullock carts).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A *pencil spring* (helical) — push it, it compresses; release, it springs back. A *car leaf spring* — the metal bends when weight is applied and flattens again when unloaded.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Helical springs store energy via *torsion of the wire* (resisting bending of the coil). Leaf springs store energy via *bending stress*.

---

### 1.9 Design of Flat Belt and V-Belt Drives and Pulleys

**Formal Definition:**
A *belt drive* transmits power between two parallel shafts via friction between the belt and the pulleys. A *pulley* is the grooved wheel mounted on the shaft.

- **Flat Belt**: Rectangular cross-section. Used where large centre distances and high speeds are needed.
- **V-Belt**: Trapezoidal cross-section, wedged into a V-groove pulley. Used where compact, high-torque, slip-resistant drives are needed (combine harvesters, threshers, tractor accessories).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A V-belt is like a wedge driven into a groove — the harder it pulls, the tighter it grips. A flat belt is like a strap wrapped around two drums — relying purely on friction.

**Standard V-Belt Designations (IS 2494):**
- A, B, C, D, E sections — top width ranges from **13 mm to 38 mm**.
- V-angle of groove: **$\mathbf{38^{\circ} \text{ to } 40^{\circ}}$**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Design of Bolts under Direct Static (Tensile) Load

#### Operating Principle

When a bolt is tightened, it develops an **internal tensile preload** $F_i$. When an external tensile load $W$ is applied, the *additional* bolt tension is **less than $W$** because part of $W$ is relieved through reduction of the joint stiffness (preload drops slightly). The bolt experiences:

$$F_b = F_i + C \cdot W$$

where $C$ is the *joint stiffness constant*:

$$C = \frac{k_b}{k_b + k_c}$$

with $k_b$ = bolt stiffness, $k_c$ = joint (members) stiffness.

#### Failure Modes Considered
1. **Tensile failure of shank** at root of thread → governed by **core diameter $d_c$**.
2. **Thread shearing** → usually secondary, since nut is stronger.
3. **Yielding of bolt material** → design for **proof load**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** Always design the bolt for the **maximum** of (a) preload alone, or (b) preload + external load. Use the *nominal/root diameter* $d_c$, NOT the major diameter $d$.

#### Initial Design Equation (No Preload — Conservative)

$$d_c = \sqrt{\frac{4 W}{\pi \cdot \sigma_t / n}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 W \cdot n}{\pi \cdot \sigma_t}}$$

#### Standard Design Equation (With Preload $F_i = 0.75 \cdot R_p \cdot A_s$)

$$d_c = \sqrt{\frac{4 F_{b,\max}}{\pi \cdot R_p / n}}$$

---

### 2.2 Bolted Joints in Shear

The bolt is subjected to **double shear** if it passes through a *middle* plate sandwiched between two outer plates, or **single shear** if only two plates overlap.

$$\tau = \frac{F}{n \cdot A} = \frac{F}{n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2} \leq \tau_{\text{all}}$$

| Case | Shear Planes | Resisting Area |
|------|--------------|----------------|
| Single shear | 1 | $\frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ |
| Double shear | 2 | $\frac{2 \pi d^2}{4}$ |

**Also check bearing/crushing stress** on the plate:
$$\sigma_b = \frac{F}{d \cdot t} \leq \sigma_{b,\text{all}}$$

---

### 2.3 Eccentrically Loaded Bolted Joints

For a bolt group (say 4 bolts in a rectangle) loaded by an eccentric force $P$ at eccentricity $e$ from the bolt-group centroid $G$:

**Primary shear force (direct):**
$$F_1 = \frac{P}{n}$$

**Secondary shear force (due to moment $M = P \cdot e$):**
$$F_2 = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot r_{\max}}{\sum r_i^2}$$

where $r_i$ is the radial distance of the *i-th* bolt from the centroid $G$, and $r_{\max}$ is the farthest bolt's distance.

**Resultant shear force on the critical (farthest) bolt:**
$$R = \sqrt{F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2 F_1 F_2 \cos\theta}$$

For bolts on the same side (assumed), the maximum bolt:
$$R_{\max} = F_1 + F_2$$

**Bolt diameter from shear:**
$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 R_{\max}}{\pi \cdot \tau_{\text{all}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always compute **$\sum r_i^2$** for the *entire* bolt group, not just one quadrant.

---

### 2.4 Shaft Design under Torsion and Combined Bending-Torsion

#### (a) Pure Torsion

$$\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \leq \tau_{\text{all}}$$

$$d = \left( \frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{\text{all}}} \right)^{1/3}$$

#### (b) Combined Bending and Torsion — Equivalent Twisting Moment

Using the **maximum shear stress theory (MSS)**:

$$T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + M_t^2}$$

Using the **distortion energy theory (DET)**:

$$T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + 0.75 M_t^2}$$

**Shaft diameter:**

$$d = \left( \frac{16 T_e}{\pi \tau_{\text{all}}} \right)^{1/3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Most problems use **DET** (more accurate for ductile materials). However, MSS gives a more conservative (larger) diameter. Always check which theory the question demands.

#### Allowable Stress Values (for mild steel shaft, no keyway):

| Condition | $\tau_{\text{all}}$ (MPa) |
|-----------|---------------------------|
| Steady load, no shock | 55–65 |
| Light shock | 40–50 |
| Heavy shock | 28–35 |

---

### 2.5 Design of Keys

#### Standard Square Key (Most Common)

For shaft diameter $d$:

| Shaft $d$ (mm) | Key width $w$ (mm) | Key height $h$ (mm) |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 20–30 | 6 | 6 |
| 30–40 | 8 | 7 |
| 40–50 | 10 | 8 |
| 50–65 | 12 | 8 |
| 65–80 | 16 | 10 |
| 80–100 | 18 | 11 |

**Length of key** $L \approx 1.5 d$ to $2 d$ (must be less than hub length).

#### Shear Failure of Key

$$F = L \cdot w \cdot \tau_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad L = \frac{2 T}{w \cdot d \cdot \tau_s}$$

#### Crushing Failure of Key

$$F = \frac{L \cdot h}{2} \cdot \sigma_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad L = \frac{4 T}{h \cdot d \cdot \sigma_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design the key length as the larger of the two** $L$ values above. Typically crushing governs.

---

### 2.6 Design of Lever

#### Three Classes of Lever

| Class | Fulcrum location | Application |
|-------|------------------|-------------|
| **Class I** | Between effort and load | See-saw, hand-brake |
| **Class II** | Load between fulcrum and effort | Wheelbarrow, nutcracker |
| **Class III** | Effort between fulcrum and load | Trowel, fishing rod |

#### Design Equations (Class I Lever)

**Mechanical Advantage:**
$$\text{MA} = \frac{L_{\text{load}}}{L_{\text{effort}}} = \frac{F_{\text{effort}}}{F_{\text{load}}}$$

**Bending moment at fulcrum:**
$$M_{\max} = F_{\text{effort}} \cdot L_{\text{effort}} = F_{\text{load}} \cdot L_{\text{load}}$$

**Required section modulus:**
$$Z = \frac{M_{\max}}{\sigma_b}$$

**Lever cross-section (rectangular, with $b = 2t$):**
$$b \cdot t^2 / 6 = Z \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \left( \frac{6 Z}{2} \right)^{1/3} = (3Z)^{1/3}$$

---

### 2.7 Coupling Design

#### 2.7.1 Sleeve (Muff) Coupling

The coupling transmits torque via **shear of the coupling bolts OR the sleeve itself**.

For a sleeve of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$ (shaft):

$$\tau_{\text{sleeve}} = \frac{16 T}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)} \cdot D \leq \tau_{\text{all}}$$

**Sleeve length:**
$$L = 2 \cdot d + 15 \text{ mm (clearance)}$$

#### 2.7.2 Flange Coupling (Rigid)

For $n$ bolts of diameter $d_b$ on a circle of diameter $D_c$:

**Shear stress in bolts:**
$$\tau_b = \frac{2 T}{n \cdot d_b^2 \cdot D_c} \leq \tau_b^{\text{all}}$$

**Crushing stress on bolt:**
$$\sigma_c = \frac{4 T}{n \cdot d_b \cdot t_1 \cdot D_c} \leq \sigma_c^{\text{all}}$$

**Shear stress in flange (hub):**
$$\tau_h = \frac{16 T}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)} \cdot D \leq \tau_{\text{all}}$$

#### 2.7.3 Jaw (Claw) Coupling

For a jaw coupling with $n$ jaws and friction angle $\phi$:

**Axial force** (to prevent slipping):
$$F_a = \frac{T}{r \cdot n \cdot \mu} \quad (\text{with $\mu$ = friction coefficient})$$

**Jaw dimensions (typical):**
- Jaw length $L_j \approx 1.5 d$ to $2 d$
- Jaw width $b \approx 1.5 d_b$ to $2 d_b$

---

### 2.8 Spring Design

#### 2.8.1 Helical Spring (Compression)

**Wire diameter** $d$, mean coil diameter $D$, total coils $N_t$, active coils $N_a$.

**Spring index:**
$$C = \frac{D}{d} \quad (\text{typically 6 to 12})$$

**Shear stress in wire** (Wahl's correction, with curvature factor $K_w$):
$$\tau = K_w \cdot \frac{8 F D}{\pi d^3}, \quad K_w = \frac{4 C - 1}{4 C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C}$$

**Spring rate (stiffness):**
$$k = \frac{G \cdot d^4}{8 D^3 N_a}$$

**Deflection under load $F$:**
$$\delta = \frac{8 F D^3 N_a}{G d^4} = \frac{F}{k}$$

**Solid length (fully compressed):**
$$L_s = N_t \cdot d$$

**Free length:**
$$L_f = L_s + \delta_{\max} + 0.15 \cdot \delta_{\max} \quad (\text{with clearance})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear modulus $G$ for spring steel:** $\mathbf{81 \text{ GPa}}$ (approx.) **Modulus of rigidity for hard-drawn spring wire:** $\mathbf{80 \text{ kN/mm}^2}$.

#### 2.8.2 Leaf Spring

For a full-elliptic or semi-elliptic leaf spring with $n$ graduated leaves:

**Bending stress in master (longest) leaf:**
$$\sigma = \frac{6 W L}{n \cdot b \cdot t^2}$$

where $L$ is half-span, $b$ is leaf width, $t$ is leaf thickness.

**Deflection:**
$$\delta = \frac{6 W L^3}{n \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^3}$$

**Number of leaves** (from load):
$$n = \frac{6 W L}{b \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_b}$$

---

### 2.9 Belt Drive Design

#### 2.9.1 Flat Belt Drive

**Belt velocity:**
$$v = \frac{\pi d_1 n_1}{60} = \frac{\pi d_2 n_2}{60} \quad (\text{m/s})$$

**Belt length** (open belt, centre distance $C$):
$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + \frac{(d_2 - d_1)^2}{4 C}$$

**Angle of contact on smaller pulley:**
$$\alpha = 180^{\circ} - 2 \sin^{-1}\left( \frac{d_2 - d_2}{2C} \right)$$

**Centrifugal tension:**
$$T_c = m \cdot v^2 \quad (m = \text{belt mass per unit length})$$

**Maximum tension (Crowning/creep considered):**
$$T_1 = T_c + \sigma \cdot A$$

**Power transmitted:**
$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = (e^{\mu \alpha} - 1) \cdot T_2 \cdot v$$

where $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $\alpha$ in radians.

#### 2.9.2 V-Belt Drive

**Effective diameter:** $d = d_p$ (pitch diameter).

**Angle of wrap on smaller pulley:**
$$\alpha = 180^{\circ} - 2 \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{2C}\right)$$

**Belt velocity:**
$$v = \frac{\pi d_1 n_1}{60}$$

**Centrifugal tension:**
$$T_c = m \cdot v^2$$

**Power rating per belt** (from manufacturer tables — depends on section, speed, diameter):
$$P_{\text{rated}} = f(d_1, n_1, \alpha)$$

**Number of belts:**
$$N = \frac{P_{\text{design}}}{P_{\text{rated}}}$$

**Belt length (V-belt):**
$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + \frac{(d_2 - d_1)^2}{4C}$$

---

### 2.10 Master KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| Component | Critical Equation | Common Failure Mode |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Bolt (tension) | $d_c = \sqrt{4 F / (\pi \sigma_t)}$ | Shank yielding |
| Bolt (shear) | $d = \sqrt{4 F / (\pi n \tau)}$ | Pin shear |
| Eccentric bolt | $R_{\max} = F_1 + F_2$ | Combined shear |
| Shaft (combined) | $d = (16 T_e / \pi \tau)^{1/3}$ | Fatigue/twist |
| Key (crushing) | $L = 4 T / (h d \sigma_c)$ | Crushing |
| Lever | $t = (3 Z)^{1/3}$ | Bending |
| Sleeve coupling | $\tau = 16 T D / [\pi(D^4 - d^4)]$ | Shear of sleeve |
| Flange bolts | $\tau_b = 2 T / (n d_b^2 D_c)$ | Shear of bolts |
| Helical spring | $\tau = 8 F D K_w / (\pi d^3)$ | Shear of wire |
| Leaf spring | $\sigma = 6 W L / (n b t^2)$ | Bending of leaves |
| Belt (flat) | $P = (e^{\mu\alpha}-1) T_2 v$ | Slip / fatigue |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Always state assumptions, units, and choice of failure theory (MSS vs DET) explicitly. Examiners award full marks only when these are written.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bolted Joint under Eccentric Load — Equivalent Bolt Force

**Problem Setup:** Four bolts at the corners of a rectangle of sides $a$ and $b$, centred at $G$. A force $P$ acts at eccentricity $e$ (from $G$). We seek the maximum resultant force on the most-loaded bolt.

**Step 1 — Direct Shear (translation):**
The force $P$ is shared equally by all $n$ bolts (assuming rigid plate):

$$F_1 = \frac{P}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Torsional Moment:**
The eccentricity generates a moment about $G$:

$$M = P \cdot e$$

**Step 3 — Secondary Shear due to Rotation:**
Each bolt resists the rotation by a force proportional to its distance from $G$. For equilibrium of moment:

$$\sum F_{2,i} \cdot r_i = M$$

Assuming $F_{2,i} = k \cdot r_i$ (bolt stiffness $\times$ displacement proportional to radial distance):

$$k \sum r_i^2 = M \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{M}{\sum r_i^2}$$

Therefore, the force on the $i$-th bolt:

$$F_{2,i} = \frac{M \cdot r_i}{\sum r_i^2}$$

**Step 4 — For the Farthest Bolt (Maximum):**

$$F_{2,\max} = \frac{M \cdot r_{\max}}{\sum r_i^2} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot r_{\max}}{\sum r_i^2}$$

**Step 5 — Resultant Force (worst case — bolt on the same side as force direction):**

$$\boxed{R_{\max} = F_1 + F_{2,\max} = \frac{P}{n} + \frac{P \cdot e \cdot r_{\max}}{\sum r_i^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Bolt Diameter from Shear:**

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 R_{\max}}{\pi \tau_{\text{all}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The summation $\sum r_i^2$ for 4 bolts at the corners of a rectangle of sides $a$ and $b$ becomes: $\sum r_i^2 = 4 \cdot \left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + 4 \cdot \left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^2 = a^2 + b^2$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Shaft Diameter under Combined Bending and Torsion (DET)

**Step 1 — Maximum Shear Stress at Critical Point:**

For a shaft subjected to bending moment $M_b$ and torsional moment $M_t$, the principal stresses at the surface are:

$$\sigma_1, \sigma_2 = \frac{32 M_b}{\pi d^3} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{32 M_b}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16 M_t}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Shear Stress (half the principal stress difference):**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{16 M_b}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16 M_t}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSS):**

$$\tau_{\max} \leq \tau_{\text{all}}$$

$$\sqrt{\left(\frac{16 M_b}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16 M_t}{\pi d^3}\right)^2} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M_b^2 + M_t^2} \leq \tau_{\text{all}}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Twisting Moment (MSS):**

$$\boxed{T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + M_t^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Distortion Energy Theory (DET):**

For DET, the equivalent von Mises shear stress:

$$\tau_{eq} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M_b^2 + 0.75 M_t^2}$$

$$\boxed{T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + 0.75 M_t^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Diameter:**

$$d = \left(\frac{16 T_e}{\pi \tau_{\text{all}}}\right)^{1/3}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Helical Spring — Wahl's Correction Factor

A helical spring wire experiences *combined torsion AND direct shear* (because the helical geometry translates an axial load $F$ into a torque $T = F \cdot D/2$ and a direct shear $F$ on the wire cross-section).

**Step 1 — Pure Torsion Stress:**

$$\tau_t = \frac{16 \cdot T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \cdot F \cdot D/2}{\pi d^3} = \frac{8 F D}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 2 — Direct Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_s = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{4 F}{\pi d^2}$$

**Step 3 — Curvature Correction (stress concentration due to coil curvature):**
For curved members, the inner fibre carries higher stress. Bergsträsser factor:

$$K_B = \frac{4 C^2 - C - 1}{4 C(C-1)}, \quad C = D/d$$

**Step 4 — Total Shear Stress at Inner Fibre:**

$$\tau = K_B \cdot \tau_t + \tau_s$$

**Step 5 — Combine and simplify (Wahl's formula):**

$$\boxed{\tau = K_w \cdot \frac{8 F D}{\pi d^3}, \quad K_w = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Leaf Spring — Bending Stress

**Setup:** Semi-elliptic leaf spring, span $2L$, central load $W$, $n$ graduated leaves of width $b$ and thickness $t$.

**Step 1 — Reactions at each end:**
$$R = W/2$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Bending Moment (at centre):**

$$M_{\max} = R \cdot L = \frac{W L}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Section Modulus of $n$ leaves:**

$$Z = n \cdot \frac{b t^2}{6}$$

**Step 4 — Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{W L / 2}{n \cdot b t^2 / 6} = \frac{6 W L}{n \cdot b \cdot t^2}$$

**Step 5 — For design, given $\sigma_b^{\text{all}}$:**

$$\boxed{n = \frac{6 W L}{b \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_b^{\text{all}}}}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation: V-Belt — Power Transmission

**Step 1 — Centrifugal Tension (acts uniformly around the belt):**

$$T_c = m \cdot v^2 \quad (\text{N, with } m \text{ in kg/m and } v \text{ in m/s})$$

**Step 2 — Effective Tension Ratio (Capstan Equation with V-groove wedge angle $\beta$):**

The V-belt wedging into the groove changes the effective friction coefficient to:

$$\mu_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\mu}{\sin(\beta/2)}$$

The ratio of tensions:

$$\frac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu_{\text{eff}} \cdot \alpha}$$

**Step 3 — Net Effective Tension:**

$$T_e = T_1 - T_2 = (T_1 - T_c) - (T_2 - T_c)$$

**Step 4 — Power Transmitted:**

$$\boxed{P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v \quad (\text{Watts})}$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Design Equation

| Topic | Given | Find | Primary Equation |
|-------|-------|------|-----------------|
| Bolt tension | Force, allowable stress | Core diameter | $d_c = \sqrt{4F/(\pi \sigma_t)}$ |
| Eccentric joint | $P$, $e$, geometry | Bolt diameter | $R_{\max} = P/n + P e r_{\max}/\sum r_i^2$ |
| Shaft | $M_b$, $M_t$, $\tau$ | Diameter | $d = (16 T_e/\pi \tau)^{1/3}$ |
| Key | Torque, $d$, key size | Key length | $L = 4T/(h d \sigma_c)$ |
| Lever | $F$, $L$, $\sigma$ | Thickness | $t = (6 M/\sigma_b \cdot b)^{1/3}$ |
| Sleeve coupling | $T$, $D$, $d$ | Verify $\tau$ | $\tau = 16 T D / [\pi(D^4 - d^4)]$ |
| Helical spring | $F$, $\delta$, $G$ | $d$, $D$, $N$ | $\tau = K_w \cdot 8FD/(\pi d^3)$ |
| Leaf spring | $W$, $L$, $\sigma$ | $n$, $t$ | $\sigma = 6 W L/(n b t^2)$ |
| V-belt | $P$, $v$, $\mu$, $\alpha$ | $T_1$, $T_2$ | $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\alpha}$ |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]

**Define the term 'preload' in a bolted joint. Why is preloading beneficial?**

**Model Answer:**
Preload $F_i$ is the initial tension induced in a bolt when it is tightened before any external load is applied. It clamps the joint members together with a compressive force equal in magnitude to the bolt tension. **Benefits:**
1. Prevents loosening under vibration.
2. Reduces the *fluctuation* in bolt tension, improving fatigue life.
3. Increases joint stiffness and load-carrying capacity.
4. Prevents separation of joint members.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Typical preload is **75% of proof load** for permanent joints, **90% for non-permanent**.

---

#### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between flat belt drive and V-belt drive.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Flat Belt | V-Belt |
|---------|-----------|--------|
| Cross-section | Rectangular | Trapezoidal |
| Pulley groove | Flat crowned | V-shaped |
| Friction | $\mu$ (low) | $\mu / \sin(\beta/2)$ (high — wedging) |
| Slip | More prone | Slip-resistant |
| Centre distance | Large | Short |
| Speed ratio | Up to 1:10 | Up to 1:8 |
| Compactness | Less | More |
| Life | Longer | Shorter (due to flexing) |
| Agricultural use | Tractor belt to thresher | Combine harvester drives |

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** A bracket carrying a load of $W = 20 \text{ kN}$ is to be attached to a column using 4 bolts at the corners of a rectangle of size **120 mm × 80 mm**. The load acts at a distance of **200 mm** from the centroid of the bolt group. The bolts are made of steel with allowable shear stress $\tau = 60 \text{ MPa}$. Determine the **bolt diameter**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a **square key** for a shaft of diameter **60 mm** transmitting torque of **2.5 kN·m**. Permissible stresses: shear = 50 MPa, crushing = 110 MPa. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Direct Shear Force:**

$$F_1 = \frac{W}{n} = \frac{20 \times 10^3}{4} = 5000 \text{ N} = 5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Moment about Centroid:**

$$M = W \cdot e = 20 \times 10^3 \times 200 = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sum r_i^2$:**

For 4 bolts at corners of a rectangle ($a = 120$, $b = 80$):

$$\sum r_i^2 = 4 \left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + 4 \left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^2 = a^2 + b^2 = 120^2 + 80^2 = 14400 + 6400 = 20800 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Radial Distance:**

$$r_{\max} = \sqrt{(a/2)^2 + (b/2)^2} = \sqrt{60^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{5200} = 72.11 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Secondary Shear on Farthest Bolt:**

$$F_2 = \frac{M \cdot r_{\max}}{\sum r_i^2} = \frac{4 \times 10^6 \times 72.11}{20800} = \frac{2.884 \times 10^8}{20800} = 13866 \text{ N} = 13.87 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Resultant Force on Critical Bolt:**

$$R_{\max} = F_1 + F_2 = 5 + 13.87 = 18.87 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — Bolt Diameter:**

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 R_{\max}}{\pi \tau}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 18.87 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 60}} = \sqrt{\frac{75480}{188.5}} = \sqrt{400.4} = 20.01 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 20 \text{ mm (standard M20 bolt)}}$$

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Standard Square Key Size:**
For shaft $d = 60$ mm → from table: $w = 16$ mm, $h = 10$ mm.

**Step 2 — Length from Shear Failure:**

$$L_s = \frac{2 T}{w \cdot d \cdot \tau_s} = \frac{2 \times 2.5 \times 10^6}{16 \times 60 \times 50} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{48000} = 104.17 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Length from Crushing Failure:**

$$L_c = \frac{4 T}{h \cdot d \cdot \sigma_c} = \frac{4 \times 2.5 \times 10^6}{10 \times 60 \times 110} = \frac{1 \times 10^7}{66000} = 151.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Design Length** (larger of the two):

$$\boxed{L = 151.5 \text{ mm, standardise to 160 mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of Hub Length:**
Hub length should be ≥ key length. Use hub length = 160 mm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget that $\sum r_i^2$ must be calculated for **all** bolts in the group, NOT just one quadrant. Doing this incorrectly is the #1 reason students lose 2-3 marks in eccentric bolt problems.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** A transmission shaft is supported by two bearings **600 mm apart**. A pulley of diameter **400 mm** is mounted at the centre, with the belt tension ratio 3:1. The shaft transmits **7.5 kW at 300 rpm**. Using **Distortion Energy Theory** with allowable shear stress **45 MPa**, determine the **shaft diameter**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A helical compression spring is required to carry a maximum load of **800 N** with a deflection of **40 mm**. The spring index is **8** and allowable shear stress is **400 MPa**. Take $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$. Determine **(i) wire diameter, (ii) mean coil diameter, (iii) number of active coils**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Torque transmitted:**

$$T = \frac{60 P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 7500}{2 \pi \times 300} = \frac{450000}{1884.96} = 238.73 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Belt Tensions:**
Let $T_2 = t$, $T_1 = 3t$.
Net belt force on shaft:

$$F_{\text{belt}} = T_1 + T_2 = 3t + t = 4t$$

Torque from belt:

$$(T_1 - T_2) \times R = T \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2t \times 0.2 = 238.73 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = 596.83 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{\text{belt}} = 4 \times 596.83 = 2387.3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Bending Moment at Centre (between two bearings):**
Reactions: $R_A = R_B = F_{\text{belt}}/2 = 1193.65$ N.
Maximum bending moment (simply supported beam, central load):

$$M_b = \frac{F_{\text{belt}} \cdot L}{4} = \frac{2387.3 \times 600}{4} = 358095 \text{ N·mm} = 358.1 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Twisting Moment (DET):**

$$T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + 0.75 M_t^2} = \sqrt{358095^2 + 0.75 \times 238730^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{1.282 \times 10^{11} + 4.275 \times 10^{10}} = \sqrt{1.71 \times 10^{11}} = 413431 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 5 — Shaft Diameter:**

$$d = \left(\frac{16 T_e}{\pi \tau_{\text{all}}}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{16 \times 413431}{\pi \times 45}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{6.615 \times 10^6}{141.37}\right)^{1/3} = (46803)^{1/3}$$

$$d = 36.0 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{d = 36 \text{ mm (standard M36 or 35 mm})}$$

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Spring Index:**
Given $C = D/d = 8$, so $D = 8d$.

**Step 2 — Wahl's Factor:**

$$K_w = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C} = \frac{32 - 1}{32 - 4} + \frac{0.615}{8} = \frac{31}{28} + 0.0769 = 1.1071 + 0.0769 = 1.184$$

**Step 3 — Wire Diameter from Shear Stress Equation:**

$$\tau = K_w \cdot \frac{8 F D}{\pi d^3} = K_w \cdot \frac{8 F \cdot 8 d}{\pi d^3} = \frac{64 K_w F}{\pi d^2}$$

$$d^2 = \frac{64 K_w F}{\pi \tau} = \frac{64 \times 1.184 \times 800}{\pi \times 400} = \frac{60620.8}{1256.6} = 48.24$$

$$\boxed{d = 6.95 \text{ mm, standardise to } d = 7 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Mean Coil Diameter:**

$$D = C \cdot d = 8 \times 7 = 56 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Spring Stiffness:**

$$k = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{800}{40} = 20 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 6 — Number of Active Coils:**

$$N_a = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 k} = \frac{80 \times 10^3 \times 7^4}{8 \times 56^3 \times 20}$$

$$= \frac{80000 \times 2401}{8 \times 175616 \times 20} = \frac{1.921 \times 10^8}{2.81 \times 10^7} = 6.83$$

$$\boxed{N_a = 7 \text{ active coils (standard)}}$$

**Step 7 — Total Coils** (assuming squared and ground ends):
$$N_t = N_a + 2 = 9 \text{ coils}$$

**Step 8 — Solid Length:**
$$L_s = N_t \cdot d = 9 \times 7 = 63 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 9 — Free Length:**
$$L_f = L_s + \delta_{\max} + 0.15 \delta_{\max} = 63 + 40 + 6 = 109 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Common mistakes in spring design:
> 1. Forgetting Wahl's correction factor $K_w$ — this gives a NON-CONSERVATIVE answer.
> 2. Using the WRONG shear modulus ($E$ instead of $G$). For steel: $G = 80$ GPa, $E = 200$ GPa.
> 3. Confusing "active coils" vs "total coils" — design uses **active** for stiffness formula.
> 4. Forgetting the **15% additional deflection clearance** at free length.

---

### 5.3 KTU Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Topics for KTU University Exam (Module 2):**

**Threaded Fasteners:**
- ✅ Preload = **75% of proof load** (standard).
- ✅ Always design for **root/core diameter** $d_c$, not major diameter $d$.
- ✅ Combined loading: direct + torsional (for eccentric joints).
- ✅ Single shear vs double shear — clearly identify plate arrangement.

**Bolted Joints in Shear:**
- ✅ Bolt pitch, edge distance per **IS 800** code.
- ✅ Bearing stress on plate = $F / (d \cdot t)$.
- ✅ Slip-resistant joints require friction grip (HSFG bolts).

**Eccentric Loading:**
- ✅ Formula: $R_{\max} = P/n + P e r_{\max}/\sum r_i^2$.
- ✅ $\sum r_i^2$ for symmetric rectangular groups = $a^2 + b^2$.
- ✅ Always choose the **bolt farthest from centroid** AND on the side of force application.

**Shafts:**
- ✅ Combined bending-torsion: use **DET** ($T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + 0.75 M_t^2}$).
- ✅ MSS gives $T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + M_t^2}$ (more conservative).
- ✅ For stepped shafts, design each segment separately.
- ✅ Always consider **keyway weakening** (reduces $\tau_{\text{all}}$ by ~25%).

**Keys:**
- ✅ Square key is most common.
- ✅ Crushing usually governs → use $L = 4T/(h d \sigma_c)$.
- ✅ Key length should be ≤ hub length.

**Levers:**
- ✅ Most levers in agricultural machinery are **Class I** or **Class II**.
- ✅ Rectangular cross-section with $b = 2t$ is standard.
- ✅ Deflection check often neglected but mandatory for hand levers.

**Couplings:**
- ✅ Sleeve: light duty, axial alignment mandatory.
- ✅ Flange: heavy duty, bolt design is critical.
- ✅ Jaw coupling: PTO use, allows disconnection, but check jaw shear and bending.

**Springs:**
- ✅ Helical: shear stress with **Wahl's factor**; deflection uses $G$ (NOT $E$).
- ✅ Leaf: bending stress; $n$ leaves is integer (round up).
- ✅ Spring index $C = D/d$ should be **6 to 12**.

**Belt Drives:**
- ✅ Flat belt: use **plain friction formula** $\mu \alpha$.
- ✅ V-belt: use **augmented friction** $\mu/\sin(\beta/2)$ due to wedging.
- ✅ Always check **angle of wrap** on smaller pulley (>120° recommended).
- ✅ Belt length formula uses pitch diameters for V-belt.
- ✅ Number of belts is always a **whole number**, rounded up.

**Key Material Constants to Memorise:**
- $\mathbf{G_{\text{steel}}} = 80 \text{ GPa}$
- $\mathbf{E_{\text{steel}}} = 200 \text{ GPa}$
- $\mathbf{\tau_{\text{mild steel}}} = 35\text{–}65 \text{ MPa}$ (depending on load)
- $\mathbf{\sigma_{b,\text{steel}}} = 100\text{–}150 \text{ MPa}$
- $\mathbf{\mu_{\text{belt-rubber}}} = 0.25\text{–}0.4$
- $\mathbf{\rho_{\text{belt}}} = 1100 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (rubber)

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — PBAGT604 (KTU 2024 SCHEME)**
> *All formulas, derivations, and design procedures are aligned with IS standards and KTU Board Examiner valuation patterns.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Gears - Spur Gear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT604 - DESIGN OF AGRICULTURAL IMPLEMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Gears - Spur Gear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Spur Gear** is the simplest and most common type of gear, consisting of a cylindrical disk with straight teeth cut parallel to its axis of rotation. Spur gears transmit power between parallel shafts through positive (form-closed) engagement, maintaining a constant velocity ratio without any slip or belt tension losses.

In **Agricultural Machinery**, spur gears are extensively used in:
- Tractor **Power Take-Off (PTO)** shafts and transmission gearboxes
- **Combine harvester** threshing drums and cleaning shoe drives
- **Power tillers** and rotary cultivator gearboxes
- **Seed drills** and fertilizer spreaders (metering drive)
- **Sugarcane crushers**, **threshers**, and **chaff cutters**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The design of spur gears involves the calculation of **Beam Strength** (Lewis Equation), **Wear Strength** (Buckingham's Equation), and the selection of module based on **AGMA / IS 4460** standards. The KTU examiner frequently tests the application of Lewis form factor and dynamic load correction for agricultural drive systems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍯

Imagine you are holding two **honeycomb disks** and rotating them in mesh to transfer motion. Each hexagonal cell is a *tooth space*, and the material between two adjacent cells is a *tooth*. This is exactly how a spur gear works — but the teeth are involute-shaped, not hexagonal, because the involute curve provides **constant angular velocity ratio** during engagement.

A simpler everyday example: the **hand winch on a bullock cart** or the **gear system on a vintage farm mill**. When the operator turns one gear, the other follows predictably — if gear A has 20 teeth and gear B has 40 teeth, then one revolution of A produces half a revolution of B. This *speed reduction* is what gear design fundamentally aims to optimize.

## 1.3 Standard Spur Gear Terminology at a Glance

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Module** | $m$ | 1 to 10 mm (Agricultural) | Fundamental size unit |
| **Pressure Angle** | $\phi$ | **20°** (Modern Standard) | Force transmission angle |
| **Pitch Circle Diameter** | $D$ | $m \times T$ | Virtual rolling circle |
| **Circular Pitch** | $p_c$ | $\pi \times m$ | Arc length between teeth |
| **Addendum** | $a$ | $1.0\,m$ | Tooth height above pitch circle |
| **Dedendum** | $b$ | $1.25\,m$ | Tooth depth below pitch circle |
| **Whole Depth** | $h$ | $2.25\,m$ | Total tooth height |
| **Working Depth** | $h_w$ | $2.0\,m$ | Contact depth |
| **Clearance** | $c$ | $0.25\,m$ | Bottom clearance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant (Universal):** The mathematical constant $\pi = 3.14159...$ appears in nearly every gear geometry equation. Always retain **at least 4 decimal places** during design calculations to prevent compounding rounding errors in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Design Logic Flow

The design of a spur gear proceeds through a strict hierarchy of strength checks:

1. **Establish Service Requirements** → Power ($P$ in kW), Speed ($N$ in rpm), Speed Ratio ($i$)
2. **Compute Tangential Load** → $P_t = \dfrac{60 \times 10^6 \times P}{\pi \times D \times N}$ in Newtons
3. **Assume Module** → Based on empirical thumb rules for agricultural drives
4. **Compute Pitch Diameter** → $D = m \times T$ where $T$ is number of teeth
5. **Determine Lewis Form Factor** → $y = f(T, \phi)$
6. **Compute Beam Strength** ($S_b$) using Lewis Equation
7. **Compute Wear Strength** ($S_w$) using Buckingham's Equation
8. **Dynamic Load Check** → Compare $P_d$ with design load
9. **Finalize Material & Module**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Logic:** Beam strength governs the design when **wear strength is higher**, and vice versa. The **governing (lower) value** determines the safe design load.

## 2.2 Detailed Geometric Relationships

The **module ($m$)** is the most fundamental parameter of a spur gear and is defined as:

$$m = \frac{D}{T} \quad \text{(in mm)}$$

The **circular pitch** is the arc distance measured along the pitch circle between corresponding points of adjacent teeth:

$$p_c = \pi \cdot m = \frac{\pi \cdot D}{T} \quad \text{(in mm)}$$

The **tooth space** at the pitch circle equals the **tooth thickness** at the pitch circle, each being:

$$\text{Tooth thickness} = \frac{p_c}{2} = \frac{\pi \cdot m}{2}$$

## 2.3 Lewis Form Factor ($y$)

The Lewis form factor accounts for the **shape-dependent stress concentration** at the tooth root. It depends on the number of teeth and pressure angle:

$$y = 0.175 - \frac{0.841}{T} \quad \text{(for } \phi = 20° \text{ full-depth involute)}$$

$$y = 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{T} \quad \text{(for } \phi = 14.5° \text{ full-depth involute)}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value expressions below use `\vert` instead of `\vert` vertical pipes to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Module | $m = D \div T$ | mm | Basic size parameter |
| 2 | Pitch Diameter | $D = m \cdot T$ | mm | Geometry |
| 3 | Circular Pitch | $p_c = \pi \cdot m$ | mm | Tooth spacing |
| 4 | Pitch Line Velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60 \times 1000}$ | m/s | Dynamic load input |
| 5 | Tangential Tooth Load | $P_t = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | N | Static load |
| 6 | Beam Strength (Lewis) | $S_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ | N | Bending failure check |
| 7 | Wear Strength (Buckingham) | $S_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$ | N | Pitting failure check |
| 8 | Lewis Form Factor (20°) | $y = 0.175 - \dfrac{0.841}{T}$ | — | Tooth shape factor |
| 9 | Velocity Factor (Buckingham) | $C_v = \dfrac{A}{A + \sqrt{v}}$ | — | Dynamic factor |
| 10 | Dynamic Load | $P_d = \dfrac{P_t}{C_v}$ | N | Actual operating load |
| 11 | Module (Thumb Rule) | $m = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{P_t \cdot C}{k \cdot \psi \cdot y}}$ | mm | Initial module guess |
| 12 | Center Distance | $a = \dfrac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2}$ | mm | Shaft placement |

**Where the auxiliary symbols represent:**

- $\sigma_b$ — Allowable bending stress for gear material (N/mm²)
- $b$ — Face width of gear (mm), typically $b = 6m$ to $10m$
- $y$ — Lewis form factor (dimensionless)
- $Q$ — Ratio factor $= \dfrac{2 \cdot T_g}{T_g + T_p}$ for external gears
- $K$ — Load stress factor $= \dfrac{\sigma_{es}^2 \sin\phi}{1.4}\left(\dfrac{1}{E_p} + \dfrac{1}{E_g}\right)$ (N/mm²)
- $A$ — Buckingham constant (Table value: **50.4 for ordinary cut gears**, **52.8 for hobbed gears**)
- $\psi$ — Effective load coefficient, typically **1.5 to 2.0**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **tractor PTO drives** (typically **540 rpm** at the PTO, with the engine running at **2000 rpm**), spur gears are sized to transmit up to **45–60 kW** through compact gearboxes. The Lewis bending check ensures the teeth do not fracture under shock loads (e.g., when a plow hits a rock), while Buckingham's wear check prevents pitting fatigue that destroys gear flanks over thousands of hours of operation.

In **rice threshers**, the cylinder speed of **1200–1500 rpm** is achieved through spur gear reduction from the prime-mover shaft, requiring precise module selection to balance strength, cost, and noise.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lewis Beam Strength Equation

The Lewis equation is derived by modeling a gear tooth as a **cantilever beam** of varying cross-section, fixed at the root and loaded by the tangential force $P_t$ at the tip.

### Step 1: Cantilever Beam Model Setup

A single gear tooth is approximated as a tapered cantilever beam projecting from the pitch circle. The **bending moment at the critical root section** (the weakest point) is:

$$M = P_t \cdot h$$

where $h$ is the tooth height from pitch circle to tip (≈ addendum + half working depth ≈ **1.75m** for full-depth teeth).

### Step 2: Section Modulus of Parabolic Tooth Profile

Wilfred Lewis observed that the tooth profile closely resembles a **parabolic curve**, for which the section modulus is:

$$Z = \frac{b \cdot t^2}{6}$$

where:
- $b$ = face width (mm)
- $t$ = tooth thickness at the root (mm)

Using the geometric relationship for a parabolic tooth at the pitch circle:

$$t = \frac{\pi \cdot m}{2} \quad \text{(at pitch circle)}$$

### Step 3: Stress Equating to Bending Stress

The maximum bending stress at the root is:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P_t \cdot h}{\frac{b \cdot t^2}{6}} = \frac{6 \cdot P_t \cdot h}{b \cdot t^2}$$

### Step 4: Introduction of Lewis Form Factor ($y$)

By substituting the parabolic profile relations, Lewis showed that $\dfrac{t^2}{6h} = \pi \cdot m \cdot y$. Therefore:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P_t}{b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y}$$

### Step 5: Rearrangement for Beam Strength

The **beam strength** $S_b$ is defined as the maximum tangential load the tooth can transmit without exceeding the allowable bending stress $\sigma_b$:

$$\boxed{S_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y \quad \text{(Newtons)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Buckingham's Wear Strength Equation

Pitting (surface fatigue) failure occurs due to Hertzian contact stress between meshing tooth flanks. Buckingham derived an empirical formula based on contact mechanics.

### Step 1: Hertzian Contact Stress Foundation

For two cylinders in contact (analogous to meshing tooth flanks), the Hertz contact stress is:

$$\sigma_c \propto \sqrt{\frac{P_t}{b \cdot \rho_r}}$$

where $\rho_r$ is the **relative radius of curvature** at the contact point.

### Step 2: Relative Radius of Curvature

For involute gear flanks:

$$\frac{1}{\rho_r} = \frac{1}{r_1} + \frac{1}{r_2}$$

where $r_1$ and $r_2$ are the **radii of curvature** of the two contacting flanks at the pitch point.

### Step 3: Ratio Factor ($Q$)

Buckingham expressed the geometric term as a dimensionless **ratio factor**:

$$Q = \frac{2 \cdot T_g}{T_g + T_p}$$

where $T_g$ is the number of teeth on the gear (larger) and $T_p$ on the pinion (smaller).

### Step 4: Load Stress Factor ($K$)

The material property constant $K$ combines elasticity and surface fatigue strength:

$$K = \frac{\sigma_{es}^2 \cdot \sin\phi}{1.4} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{E_p} + \frac{1}{E_g}\right)$$

where $\sigma_{es}$ is the surface endurance limit, $\phi$ is the pressure angle, and $E$ is Young's modulus.

### Step 5: Final Wear Strength Equation

Combining the geometric factor, material factor, and face width:

$$\boxed{S_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K \quad \text{(Newtons)}}$$

where $d_p$ is the pinion pitch diameter.

## 3.3 Derivation of Buckingham's Dynamic Load Equation

The dynamic load accounts for **vibrations and impact** during tooth engagement.

### Step 1: Energy Approach

Buckingham equated the **strain energy stored in the tooth** to the **kinetic energy of the equivalent mass** at the pitch point.

### Step 2: Equivalent Mass

The dynamic effect is modeled as a mass $M_e$ moving at the pitch line velocity $v$:

$$\text{Kinetic Energy} = \frac{1}{2} M_e v^2$$

### Step 3: Tooth Spring Compliance

The tooth pair acts as a spring of stiffness $k$ per unit face width:

$$\text{Strain Energy} = \frac{P_d^2}{2k_b}$$

### Step 4: Equating Energies

By conservation of energy, Buckingham arrived at:

$$P_d = P_t + \frac{21 \cdot v \cdot (b \cdot C \cdot \cos\phi + P_{d,\max})}{21 \cdot v + \sqrt{b \cdot C \cdot \cos\phi + P_{d,\max}}}$$

This simplifies to the practical form:

$$\boxed{P_d = \frac{P_t}{C_v}, \quad \text{where} \quad C_v = \frac{A}{A + \sqrt{v}}}$$

For ordinary cut gears: $A = \mathbf{50.4}$
For carefully cut/hobbed gears: $A = \mathbf{52.8}$

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example — KTU Board Pattern

**Problem:** Design a spur gear pair to transmit **7.5 kW** at **1450 rpm** (pinion) to **725 rpm** (gear). Service factor = 1.5. Material: **40C8 steel** with $\sigma_b = 105 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $\sigma_{es} = 550 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $E = 2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$. Use $\phi = 20°$.

### Solution Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Number of Teeth Ratio**

$$i = \frac{N_p}{N_g} = \frac{1450}{725} = 2.0$$

**Step 2 — Assume Module Using Thumb Rule**

For agricultural drives, assume $m = 4$ mm initially. Let $T_p = 20$ teeth.

$$T_g = i \times T_p = 2.0 \times 20 = 40 \text{ teeth}$$

**Step 3 — Pitch Diameters**

$$D_p = m \times T_p = 4 \times 20 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_g = m \times T_g = 4 \times 40 = 160 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Pitch Line Velocity**

$$v = \frac{\pi \times D_p \times N_p}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times 80 \times 1450}{60 \times 1000}$$

$$v = \frac{364,424.8}{60,000} = 6.074 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Tangential Load**

$$P_t = \frac{1000 \times P}{v} = \frac{1000 \times 7.5}{6.074} = 1234.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Design Load with Service Factor**

$$P_d = P_t \times C_s = 1234.8 \times 1.5 = 1852.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7 — Lewis Form Factor (Pinion governs)**

$$y_p = 0.175 - \frac{0.841}{T_p} = 0.175 - \frac{0.841}{20} = 0.175 - 0.0421 = 0.1329$$

**Step 8 — Beam Strength (Assume $b = 10m = 40$ mm)**

$$S_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y_p$$

$$S_b = 105 \times 40 \times \pi \times 4 \times 0.1329$$

$$S_b = 105 \times 40 \times 1.6698 = 7013.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 9 — Wear Strength**

$$Q = \frac{2 T_g}{T_g + T_p} = \frac{2 \times 40}{40 + 20} = \frac{80}{60} = 1.333$$

$$K = \frac{\sigma_{es}^2 \sin\phi}{1.4} \left(\frac{1}{E_p} + \frac{1}{E_g}\right)$$

For same material: $K = \dfrac{(550)^2 \sin 20°}{1.4} \times \dfrac{2}{2.1 \times 10^5}$

$$K = \frac{302500 \times 0.342}{1.4} \times \frac{2}{210000} = 73825.9 \times 9.524 \times 10^{-6} = 0.7031 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$S_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K = 80 \times 40 \times 1.333 \times 0.7031 = 2999.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 10 — Governing Strength**

Since $S_w < S_b$, **wear governs**: $S_w = 2999.6 \text{ N} > P_d = 1852.2 \text{ N}$ ✅

The design is **SAFE** with a safety factor of $\dfrac{2999.6}{1852.2} = 1.62$.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Design Stage | If $S_b < S_w$ (Beam Governs) | If $S_w < S_b$ (Wear Governs) | If Both < $P_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Action | Increase **module** $m$ | Increase **face width** $b$ | **Increase both** $m$ and $b$ |
| Material Switch | Use higher $\sigma_b$ alloy steel | Use case-hardened steel (higher $\sigma_{es}$) | Upgrade material **and** geometry |
| Cost Implication | Moderate increase | Higher (wider gear blank) | Significant redesign required |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Spur Gear Terminology — Geometric Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Spur Gear Disc"] --> nodeB["Pitch Circle Diameter D"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Addendum Circle D plus 2m"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Dedendum Circle D minus 2.5m"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Base Circle D cos phi"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["Outside Diameter"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["Root Diameter"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["Module m equals D by T"]
    nodeB --> nodeI["Circular Pitch pc equals pi times m"]
    nodeB --> nodeJ["Pressure Angle phi equals 20 degrees"]
    nodeB --> nodeK["Face Width b equals 6m to 10m"]
    nodeA --> nodeL["Tooth Thickness equals pi m by 2"]
    nodeA --> nodeM["Tooth Space equals pi m by 2"]
```

## 4.2 Spur Gear Design Procedure — Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
```

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Define the following terms with reference to spur gear design: (a) Module, (b) Circular Pitch, (c) Lewis Form Factor.**

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Module ($m$):** It is the ratio of pitch circle diameter ($D$) to the number of teeth ($T$) on the gear. It is the fundamental unit of gear size and is standardized in **mm** for SI units. $m = D/T$.

**(b) Circular Pitch ($p_c$):** It is the arc distance measured along the pitch circle between corresponding points of two adjacent teeth. Mathematically, $p_c = \pi \cdot m = \pi D/T$.

**(c) Lewis Form Factor ($y$):** It is a dimensionless factor introduced by Wilfred Lewis to account for the **shape-dependent stress distribution** at the tooth root. For a **20° full-depth involute tooth**, $y = 0.175 - 0.841/T$. Lower tooth count → lower form factor → higher stress.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**State Buckingham's equation for wear strength and explain the significance of the ratio factor $Q$.**

#### Model Answer:

Buckingham's wear strength equation is:

$$S_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$$

where $S_w$ is the wear strength (N), $d_p$ is the pinion pitch diameter (mm), $b$ is the face width (mm), $Q$ is the **ratio factor** (dimensionless), and $K$ is the **load stress factor** (N/mm²).

**Significance of $Q$:** The ratio factor accounts for the **curvature geometry** of the meshing tooth flanks. It is given by:

$$Q = \frac{2 T_g}{T_g + T_p}$$

For an external gear pair, $Q$ ranges between **1.0 (equal teeth)** and **2.0 (large speed ratio)**. A higher $Q$ value indicates a more favorable curvature for distributing contact stress, allowing higher wear load capacity.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Design a pair of spur gears to transmit 11 kW from an electric motor running at 1440 rpm to a compressor running at 480 rpm. Take service factor = 1.5. Use 20° full-depth involute teeth. Material: 40C8 steel with $\sigma_b = 105$ N/mm², $\sigma_{es} = 550$ N/mm², and $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm². Assume velocity factor using Buckingham's equation with $A = 50.4$.**

**OR**

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the Lewis equation for beam strength. State its assumptions and limitations in spur gear design.

**(b)** A spur pinion has 18 teeth and meshes with a gear of 54 teeth. The module is 5 mm. The pinion runs at 1200 rpm and transmits 8 kW. Calculate:
1. Pitch line velocity
2. Tangential tooth load
3. Lewis beam strength if $\sigma_b = 90$ N/mm² and face width $b = 50$ mm
4. Dynamic load using $C_v = \dfrac{50.4}{50.4 + \sqrt{v}}$

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (Complete Step-by-Step)

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Power and Speed Calculations**

**Step 1: Speed Ratio**

$$i = \frac{N_p}{N_g} = \frac{1440}{480} = 3.0$$

**Step 2: Assume Teeth on Pinion**

For a pinion in agricultural drives, $T_p = 18$ teeth (standard minimum to avoid undercutting).

$$T_g = i \times T_p = 3.0 \times 18 = 54 \text{ teeth}$$

**Step 3: Initial Module Guess Using Thumb Rule**

For 11 kW at low-medium speeds, initial guess: $m = 4$ mm.

$$D_p = m \times T_p = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_g = m \times T_g = 4 \times 54 = 216 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Pitch Line Velocity**

$$v = \frac{\pi \times D_p \times N_p}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times 72 \times 1440}{60000} = \frac{325,725.1}{60,000}$$

$$v = 5.429 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Tangential Tooth Load**

$$P_t = \frac{1000 \times P}{v} = \frac{1000 \times 11}{5.429} = 2026.2 \text{ N}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Strength and Dynamic Checks**

**Step 6: Design Tangential Load (with Service Factor)**

$$P_d = C_s \times P_t = 1.5 \times 2026.2 = 3039.3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7: Velocity Factor (Buckingham)**

$$C_v = \frac{A}{A + \sqrt{v}} = \frac{50.4}{50.4 + \sqrt{5.429}} = \frac{50.4}{50.4 + 2.330} = \frac{50.4}{52.730}$$

$$C_v = 0.9558$$

**Step 8: Effective Tangential Load**

$$P_{eff} = \frac{P_t}{C_v} = \frac{2026.2}{0.9558} = 2120.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 9: Lewis Form Factor for Pinion (governs)**

$$y_p = 0.175 - \frac{0.841}{18} = 0.175 - 0.0467 = 0.1283$$

**Step 10: Beam Strength (Take $b = 10m = 40$ mm)**

$$S_b = \sigma_b \times b \times \pi \times m \times y_p$$

$$S_b = 105 \times 40 \times \pi \times 4 \times 0.1283 = 105 \times 40 \times 1.6121 = 6771.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 11: Wear Strength**

$$Q = \frac{2 \times 54}{54 + 18} = \frac{108}{72} = 1.5$$

For same material:

$$K = \frac{(550)^2 \times \sin 20°}{1.4} \times \frac{2}{2.1 \times 10^5}$$

$$K = \frac{302500 \times 0.342}{1.4} \times \frac{2}{210000} = 73825.9 \times 9.524 \times 10^{-6} = 0.7031 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$S_w = d_p \times b \times Q \times K = 72 \times 40 \times 1.5 \times 0.7031 = 3037.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 12: Final Verification**

Governing strength: $S_w = 3037.4 \text{ N}$ (wear, since lower than $S_b$)

$$S_w = 3037.4 \text{ N} > P_{eff} = 2120.0 \text{ N} \quad \checkmark$$

Safety Factor: $\dfrac{3037.4}{2120.0} = 1.43$ → **ADEQUATE**

**Design is SAFE.** ✅

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (Complete Step-by-Step)

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Lewis Equation Theory**

The **Lewis equation** treats a gear tooth as a cantilever beam fixed at its root, with the tangential load applied at the tooth tip. The bending moment at the critical root section is balanced against the allowable bending stress.

$$\boxed{S_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The load is concentrated at the **tooth tip** (worst case).
2. The tooth profile is approximated as a **parabolic curve**.
3. Only **one pair of teeth** carries the full load.
4. Stress concentration effects are lumped into the **Lewis form factor** $y$.

**Limitations:**
1. Does **not** account for dynamic loads or vibrations.
2. Assumes **uniform material** with no surface hardening effects.
3. Does not consider **wear, scuffing, or pitting**.
4. Accuracy degrades for **low tooth counts** ($T < 17$).

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Solution**

Given: $T_p = 18$, $T_g = 54$, $m = 5$ mm, $N_p = 1200$ rpm, $P = 8$ kW, $\sigma_b = 90$ N/mm², $b = 50$ mm.

**(i) Pitch Line Velocity**

$$v = \frac{\pi \times D_p \times N_p}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times (5 \times 18) \times 1200}{60000} = \frac{\pi \times 90 \times 1200}{60000}$$

$$v = \frac{339,292}{60,000} = 5.655 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Tangential Tooth Load**

$$P_t = \frac{1000 \times 8}{5.655} = 1414.6 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Lewis Beam Strength**

$$y_p = 0.175 - \frac{0.841}{18} = 0.1283$$

$$S_b = 90 \times 50 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.1283 = 90 \times 50 \times 2.015 = 9067.5 \text{ N}$$

**(iv) Dynamic Load**

$$C_v = \frac{50.4}{50.4 + \sqrt{5.655}} = \frac{50.4}{50.4 + 2.378} = \frac{50.4}{52.778} = 0.9550$$

$$P_d = \frac{P_t}{C_v} = \frac{1414.6}{0.9550} = 1481.3 \text{ N}$$

**Comparison:** $S_b = 9067.5 \text{ N} \gg P_d = 1481.3 \text{ N}$ → Design is **SAFE** with high safety margin.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Service Factor ($C_s$):** Always multiply $P_t$ by $C_s = 1.5$ (agricultural) before comparing with $S_b$ or $S_w$.
> 2. **Unit Confusion:** Velocity $v$ must be in **m/s** for the Buckingham $C_v$ formula. A common error is using cm/s.
> 3. **Governing Strength Selection:** Students often pick $S_b$ as the design basis even when $S_w < S_b$. The **minimum** of $S_b$ and $S_w$ is the governing value.
> 4. **Wrong Form Factor Equation:** Using the 14.5° formula instead of 20° (or vice versa) — **match the formula with the pressure angle given in the problem**.
> 5. **Pinion vs Gear Check:** Always check the **pinion** for beam strength because it has fewer teeth and hence lower $y$.
> 6. **Assuming $b = m$ instead of $b = 6m$ to $10m$:** This produces an undersized, unsafe gear.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Quick-Recall Bullet Checklist

- **Module** ($m$) is the **fundamental size parameter** of a spur gear; $D = mT$.
- **Pitch Circle** is the imaginary circle on which two gears roll without slipping.
- **Pressure Angle** standard is **20°** (modern); **14.5°** is the older British standard.
- **Lewis Form Factor** $y = 0.175 - 0.841/T$ for **20°** full-depth teeth.
- **Beam Strength** $S_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ — checks **bending failure**.
- **Wear Strength** $S_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$ — checks **pitting failure**.
- **Governing Strength** = $\min(S_b, S_w)$ — must exceed $P_d = P_t / C_v$.
- **Velocity Factor** $C_v = A / (A + \sqrt{v})$, with $A = 50.4$ (cut) or $52.8$ (hobbed).
- **Face Width** $b$ is typically **6m to 10m** for industrial/agricultural gears.
- **Minimum Pinion Teeth** to avoid undercutting: **17 teeth** at 20° pressure angle.
- **Standard Module Series (IS 4460):** 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm.
- **AGMA Recommendations:** For agricultural drives, prefer **module ≥ 3 mm** to avoid manufacturing difficulties and noise.
- **Material Trends:** **40C8** (low-carbon) for general drives; **20MnCr5 case-hardened** for high-load tractor gearboxes.
- **Always compare governing strength with dynamic load** $P_d$, **not** static $P_t$.
- **Efficiency of spur gears** ≈ **98–99% per mesh** — one of the highest among mechanical drives.
- **Center Distance** $a = m(T_1 + T_2)/2$ — important for shaft alignment in agricultural machinery.

### 🌾 Agricultural Application Memory Aid

| Machine | Typical Spur Gear Module | Typical Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| Tractor PTO gearbox | 3 – 6 mm | 1:3 to 1:4 |
| Combine harvester thresher | 4 – 8 mm | 1:2 to 1:5 |
| Power tiller rotary drive | 2.5 – 5 mm | 1:10 to 1:20 |
| Sugarcane crusher mill | 6 – 10 mm | 1:5 to 1:8 |
| Chaff cutter | 2 – 4 mm | 1:2 to 1:3 |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Screw Motion Mechanisms Like Screw Jack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT604 - DESIGN OF AGRICULTURAL IMPLEMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Screw Motion Mechanisms Like Screw Jack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Screw Motion Mechanism

A **screw motion mechanism** is a kinematic pair that converts rotational motion into linear motion (or vice versa) through the helical engagement of threaded surfaces. The fundamental governing geometric parameter is the **helix angle ($\alpha$)**, defined as the angle a thread makes with a plane perpendicular to the axis of the cylinder on which it is formed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A screw mechanism is a simple machine that transmits power or motion between a screw and a nut, both of which have mating helical threads. The most common agricultural application is the **Screw Jack**, used for raising/lowering heavy implements, ploughs, tractor-mounted equipment, and trailer bodies.

### 1.2 Classification of Screw Motion Mechanisms

Screw motion mechanisms used in agricultural and general mechanical engineering are broadly classified based on the thread profile and functional purpose:

| Classification Basis | Type | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Thread Profile | **Square Thread** | Power transmission, jacks (maximum efficiency) |
| Thread Profile | **V-Thread / ACME Thread** | General fastening, adjustable agricultural tools |
| Motion Type | **Translation Screw** | Leadscrews, screw jacks |
| Motion Type | **Rotation Screw** | Drill bits, augers (tractor-mounted posthole diggers) |
| Application | **Screw Jack** | Lifting ploughs, levelling implements, raising harvesters |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Simple English for First-Time Learners):**
> Imagine trying to lift a heavy tractor trolley by hand — impossible. Now imagine inserting a long metal rod with a spiral groove (a "screw") inside a fixed block with a matching groove (a "nut"). When you turn the rod using a small handle, the rod moves upward slowly but with tremendous force, easily lifting the trolley. **That is exactly how a Screw Jack works!** A small effort applied on the handle produces a large lifting force because of the mechanical advantage created by the inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder (the helix).

### 1.4 Physical Significance in Agricultural Engineering

In the context of agricultural machinery, the screw jack finds direct application in:

- **Levelling and height adjustment** of tractor-drawn ploughs, disc harrows, and cultivators.
- **Lifting trailer bodies** for unloading grains, fertilizers, and harvested produce.
- **Adjusting cutting height** of combine harvesters and reapers.
- **Height adjustment** of mounted rotary tillers and seed drills.
- **Tensioning mechanisms** in balers and mowers.

The fundamental engineering parameters (which will appear in KTU problems) are:

- **Load (P)** — measured in Newtons (N) or kiloNewtons (kN).
- **Nominal / Core screw diameter ($d_c$)** — the inner diameter of the thread.
- **Outer / Major screw diameter ($d_o$)** — the outer diameter.
- **Pitch ($p$)** — the axial distance between adjacent thread crests (typically **1 to 12 mm** for jacks).
- **Helix angle ($\alpha$)** — typically **1.5° to 5°** for power screws.
- **Friction angle ($\phi$)** — derived from the coefficient of friction $\mu$.
- **Coefficient of friction ($\mu$)** — typically **0.10 to 0.20** for steel-on-bronze lubricated threads.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometry of the Screw Thread

When a right-angled triangle is wrapped around a cylinder of mean diameter $d$, the hypotenuse traces out the **helix**. The relationship between the helix angle and the screw geometry is:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{L}{\pi d}$$

Where:
- $L$ = Lead of the screw (= pitch for single-start threads)
- $d$ = Mean diameter of the screw = $\frac{d_o + d_c}{2}$
- $\alpha$ = Helix angle

### 2.2 Forces Acting on the Screw Thread

When an axial load **P** is applied on a square-threaded screw, the normal reaction **N** acts perpendicular to the thread surface (which is inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal). The friction force **F = μN** acts tangentially opposing motion.

Resolution of forces on the inclined thread plane:

- **Tangential force on thread** = $P \tan\alpha$ (component along helix)
- **Normal force on thread** = $P \sec\alpha$
- **Friction force** = $\mu P \sec\alpha$

### 2.3 Torque Required to Raise the Load ($T_r$)

The total tangential force resisting rotation when raising the load is:

$$\text{Resisting tangential force} = P \tan\alpha + \mu P \sec\alpha$$

The torque required to overcome this at the mean radius ($d/2$):

$$T_r = \frac{P d}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

Where $\tan\phi = \mu$ (friction angle).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\tan(\alpha + \phi)$?** Because the combined effect of the helix angle and the friction angle adds vectorially when raising the load (motion is "uphill" against both the incline and friction).

### 2.4 Torque Required to Lower the Load ($T_l$)

When lowering the load, gravity assists motion but friction still opposes it. The net tangential force becomes:

$$T_l = \frac{P d}{2} \tan(\alpha - \phi)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $\alpha < \phi$, then $\tan(\alpha - \phi)$ is **negative**, meaning the load **cannot lower by itself** — this is called the **self-locking condition**, which is essential for a screw jack used for lifting ploughs and tractor implements (otherwise the load would crash down).

### 2.5 Efficiency of Screw Jack ($\eta$)

The efficiency is defined as the ratio of work output (ideal work to lift load) to work input (actual work done):

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Ideal effort} \times d/2}{\text{Actual torque}} = \frac{P \tan\alpha \times d/2}{T_r}$$

Simplifying:

$$\eta = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

### 2.6 Maximum Efficiency Condition

Differentiating $\eta$ with respect to $\alpha$ and setting $\frac{d\eta}{d\alpha} = 0$:

$$\alpha_{max\,eff} = 45° - \frac{\phi}{2}$$

The corresponding **maximum efficiency** is:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$$

### 2.7 Self-Locking Condition

A screw is **self-locking** when it does not require any external torque to hold the load, i.e., the load cannot back-drive the screw:

$$\alpha < \phi$$

Equivalently, the **lead angle must be less than the friction angle**.

### 2.8 Modification for V-Threads (ACME Threads)

For a V-thread with thread angle $\beta$ (typically **29°** for ACME, **60°** for standard V-thread), the effective friction coefficient increases due to the wedging action:

$$\mu' = \frac{\mu}{\cos(\beta/2)}$$

All torque and efficiency equations are modified by replacing $\mu$ with $\mu'$.

### 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Helix angle | $\tan\alpha = \frac{L}{\pi d}$ | $\alpha$ in radians or degrees |
| 2 | Friction angle | $\tan\phi = \mu$ | $\mu$ is dimensionless |
| 3 | Torque to raise (square thread) | $T_r = \frac{Pd}{2}\tan(\alpha+\phi)$ | $T_r$ in N·m |
| 4 | Torque to lower (square thread) | $T_l = \frac{Pd}{2}\tan(\alpha-\phi)$ | Sign indicates self-locking |
| 5 | Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha+\phi)}$ | Expressed as decimal or % |
| 6 | Max efficiency angle | $\alpha = 45° - \phi/2$ | Design optimization |
| 7 | Max efficiency value | $\eta_{max} = \frac{1-\sin\phi}{1+\sin\phi}$ | For square threads |
| 8 | Self-locking condition | $\alpha < \phi$ | Mandatory for lifting jacks |
| 9 | V-thread equivalent friction | $\mu' = \mu \div \cos(\beta/2)$ | $\beta$ is thread angle |
| 10 | Effort at handle (length $l$) | $F = T_r \div l$ | $F$ in Newtons |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always carry $\tan(\alpha + \phi)$ and $\tan(\alpha - \phi)$ to **at least 4 decimal places** for 14-mark problems to avoid losing marks in arithmetic accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Torque to Raise Load ($T_r$)

Consider a square-threaded screw jack supporting an axial load **P**. Unwind the helix by cutting along a vertical line and flattening it into a straight inclined plane of length $\pi d$ (one full turn) and height equal to the lead **L**.

**Step 1 — Free body diagram on unwound thread:**

Let the inclined surface have angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal. The block (representing the screw) rests on this incline under load **P** acting vertically downward.

**Step 2 — Resolve forces:**

The normal to the inclined surface makes angle $\alpha$ with the vertical. Resolving **P** along and perpendicular to the incline:

$$\text{Component along incline (down the slope)} = P \sin\alpha$$

$$\text{Component perpendicular to incline (pressing on surface)} = P \cos\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Apply friction:**

Since the block tends to move up the incline when raising the load, kinetic friction $\mu N$ acts **down the slope**:

$$P \sin\alpha + \mu P \cos\alpha = F_t$$

Where $F_t$ is the tangential force required at the mean radius to push the block up.

**Step 4 — Convert to torque:**

The tangential force acts at the mean radius $d/2$:

$$T_r = F_t \times \frac{d}{2} = \frac{P d}{2}(\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha)$$

**Step 5 — Combine into a single trigonometric term:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_r &= \frac{P d}{2}\cos\alpha(\tan\alpha + \mu) \\
T_r &= \frac{P d}{2}\cos\alpha\left(\frac{\tan\alpha + \tan\phi}{1 - \tan\alpha \tan\phi}\right)\times(1 - \tan\alpha\tan\phi) \\
T_r &= \frac{P d}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi)
\end{aligned}$$

> Hence the **governing equation**: $T_r = \dfrac{Pd}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi)$ ✓

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation of Torque to Lower Load ($T_l$)

When lowering, the block tends to slide **down** the incline, so friction now acts **up** the slope:

$$F_t = P \sin\alpha - \mu P \cos\alpha$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_l &= \frac{P d}{2}(\sin\alpha - \mu \cos\alpha) \\
T_l &= \frac{P d}{2}\cos\alpha(\tan\alpha - \tan\phi) \\
T_l &= \frac{P d}{2}\tan(\alpha - \phi)
\end{aligned}$$

> Hence the **governing equation**: $T_l = \dfrac{Pd}{2}\tan(\alpha - \phi)$ ✓

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation of Efficiency

**Definition:** Efficiency is the ratio of useful work output (lifting the load without friction) to the actual work input (with friction).

**Ideal work output** (no friction, $\phi = 0$) for one complete revolution (lift = L):

$$W_{ideal} = P \times L$$

**Actual work input** for one complete revolution (torque applied at mean radius):

$$W_{actual} = T_r \times 2\pi \times \frac{1}{1} \;\;\text{wait — correct is}\;W_{actual} = 2\pi T_r$$

But conceptually, ideal torque (no friction) = $\dfrac{Pd}{2}\tan\alpha$. So:

$$\eta = \frac{T_{ideal}}{T_{actual}} = \frac{(Pd/2)\tan\alpha}{(Pd/2)\tan(\alpha+\phi)} = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha+\phi)}$$

> Hence the **governing equation**: $\eta = \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha+\phi)}$ ✓

### 3.4 Maximum Efficiency Derivation

Given:

$$\eta = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha+\phi)}$$

For maximum efficiency, differentiate with respect to $\alpha$:

$$\frac{d\eta}{d\alpha} = 0$$

Let $f(\alpha) = \tan\alpha$ and $g(\alpha) = \tan(\alpha+\phi)$. Then $\eta = f/g$.

$$\frac{d\eta}{d\alpha} = \frac{f'g - fg'}{g^2} = 0 \implies f'g = fg'$$

$$\sec^2\alpha \cdot \tan(\alpha+\phi) = \tan\alpha \cdot \sec^2(\alpha+\phi)$$

Dividing both sides by $\sec^2\alpha \cdot \sec^2(\alpha+\phi)$:

$$\frac{\tan(\alpha+\phi)}{\sec^2(\alpha+\phi)} = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\sec^2\alpha}$$

$$\sin(\alpha+\phi)\cos(\alpha+\phi) = \sin\alpha\cos\alpha$$

$$\frac{\sin 2(\alpha+\phi)}{2} = \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}$$

$$\sin 2(\alpha+\phi) = \sin 2\alpha$$

This implies:

$$2(\alpha+\phi) = 180° - 2\alpha \implies 4\alpha = 180° - 2\phi$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_{max\,eff} = 45° - \frac{\phi}{2}}$$

Substituting back:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\tan(45° - \phi/2)}{\tan(45° + \phi/2)} = \frac{1 - \tan(\phi/2)}{1 + \tan(\phi/2)}$$

Using the identity $\tan(45° - x) = \frac{1-\tan x}{1+\tan x}$ and after simplification:

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}}$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A screw jack is used to lift a tractor-mounted plough assembly weighing **30 kN**. The screw has a **mean diameter of 50 mm** and a **pitch of 12 mm**. The coefficient of friction between the screw and nut is **0.12**. A lever arm of **400 mm** is used. Determine:
> (a) The helix angle and friction angle.
> (b) The torque required to raise the load.
> (c) The torque required to lower the load and check for self-locking.
> (d) The efficiency of the screw jack.
> (e) The effort required at the handle.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
$P = 30{,}000$ N, $d = 50$ mm = $0.050$ m, $L = p = 12$ mm = $0.012$ m, $\mu = 0.12$, $l = 400$ mm = $0.400$ m.

**(a) Helix angle and friction angle:**

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{L}{\pi d} = \frac{12}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{12}{157.0796} = 0.07639$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.07639) = 4.370°$$

$$\tan\phi = \mu = 0.12 \implies \phi = \tan^{-1}(0.12) = 6.843°$$

**(b) Torque to raise load:**

$$T_r = \frac{Pd}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

$$\alpha + \phi = 4.370° + 6.843° = 11.213°$$

$$\tan(11.213°) = 0.1984$$

$$T_r = \frac{30{,}000 \times 0.050}{2} \times 0.1984 = 750 \times 0.1984 = 148.80 \text{ N·m}$$

**(c) Torque to lower load and self-locking check:**

$$\alpha - \phi = 4.370° - 6.843° = -2.473°$$

$$\tan(-2.473°) = -0.04321$$

$$T_l = 750 \times (-0.04321) = -32.41 \text{ N·m}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that the load does not tend to come down by itself; in fact, a torque of magnitude **32.41 N·m** must be applied **in the lowering direction** to initiate downward motion. This confirms the screw jack is **self-locking** because $\alpha < \phi$ ($4.370° < 6.843°$) ✓.

**(d) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} = \frac{0.07639}{0.1984} = 0.3850 = 38.50\%$$

**(e) Effort at handle:**

$$F = \frac{T_r}{l} = \frac{148.80}{0.400} = 372.0 \text{ N}$$

> **Conclusion:** A modest handle force of 372 N can lift a 30 kN plough — demonstrating the massive **mechanical advantage of 30,000 / 372 ≈ 80.6**.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Helix Angle $\alpha$ | Friction Angle $\phi$ | Self-Locking? | Efficiency Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha < \phi$ | Any | **Yes** (Safe for jack) | Low efficiency |
| $\alpha = \phi$ | Equal | Limiting case | $\eta < 50\%$ |
| $\alpha > \phi$ | Less | **No** (Load drops) | Can exceed 50% |
| $\alpha = 45° - \phi/2$ | Any | Depends on $\phi$ | **Maximum efficiency** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
