<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a screw jack. State any two agricultural applications.**

**Model Answer:**
A screw jack is a simple lifting device that uses a screw-and-nut mechanism to raise heavy loads by applying a small effort at the end of a long lever arm. It works on the principle of converting rotational motion into linear motion through helical threads.

**Two agricultural applications:**
1. **Lifting tractor-mounted ploughs and cultivators** during transport and for depth adjustment.
2. **Raising the body of trailer trolleys** for unloading grains, fertilizers, and harvested crops.

*(For 3 marks: Definition = 1, Applications = 1, Working principle = 1)*

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the condition for self-locking of a screw jack and explain its significance in agricultural machinery.**

**Model Answer:**
A screw jack is said to be **self-locking** when the helix angle $\alpha$ is **less than the friction angle $\phi$**, i.e., $\alpha < \phi$.

Mathematically, this implies $\tan(\alpha - \phi) < 0$, which means the load cannot back-drive the screw and tends to remain stationary even without applied torque.

**Significance in agricultural machinery:**
In tractor-mounted ploughs, harvesters, and levelling equipment, the lifted load must **not descend suddenly** when the operator releases the handle. Self-locking ensures safety, prevents accidents, and eliminates the need for an external brake. *(3 marks)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an internal choice in Part B. Two fully-solved alternatives are provided below.

---

#### **QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)**

A **screw jack** is used in a workshop to lift a tractor engine weighing **25 kN**. The screw has a **mean diameter of 60 mm** and a **pitch of 10 mm**. The coefficient of friction for the square thread is **0.15**. A handle of length **450 mm** is used.

**(a) Determine the torque required to raise the load and the efficiency of the screw jack.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Determine the torque required to lower the load. Is the jack self-locking? What is the effort at the handle for raising?** *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Given:**
$P = 25{,}000$ N, $d = 0.060$ m, $L = p = 0.010$ m, $\mu = 0.15$, $l = 0.450$ m.

**Part (a) — Torque to raise and efficiency:**

Helix angle:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{L}{\pi d} = \frac{10}{\pi \times 60} = 0.05305$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.05305) = 3.038°$$

Friction angle:

$$\tan\phi = 0.15 \implies \phi = \tan^{-1}(0.15) = 8.531°$$

Sum of angles:

$$\alpha + \phi = 3.038° + 8.531° = 11.569°$$

$$\tan(11.569°) = 0.2047$$

Torque to raise:

$$T_r = \frac{Pd}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi) = \frac{25{,}000 \times 0.060}{2} \times 0.2047$$

$$T_r = 750 \times 0.2047 = 153.5 \text{ N·m}$$

Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha+\phi)} = \frac{0.05305}{0.2047} = 0.2591 = 25.91\%$$

**Part (b) — Torque to lower, self-locking check, effort at handle:**

$$\alpha - \phi = 3.038° - 8.531° = -5.493°$$

$$\tan(-5.493°) = -0.09611$$

$$T_l = \frac{Pd}{2}\tan(\alpha - \phi) = 750 \times (-0.09611) = -72.08 \text{ N·m}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that a torque of **72.08 N·m** must be applied in the lowering direction to make the load descend.

**Self-locking check:** Since $\alpha = 3.038° < \phi = 8.531°$, the screw jack **IS self-locking** ✓ — safe for agricultural lifting operations.

**Effort at handle for raising:**

$$F = \frac{T_r}{l} = \frac{153.5}{0.450} = 341.1 \text{ N}$$

**Final Answer Summary (for 14 marks):**
| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Torque to raise ($T_r$) | 153.5 N·m |
| Torque to lower ($T_l$) | −72.08 N·m |
| Efficiency ($\eta$) | 25.91% |
| Self-locking? | **Yes** ($\alpha < \phi$) |
| Effort at handle ($F$) | 341.1 N |

---

#### **QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (14 Marks)**

A **square-threaded screw jack** used to lift a combine harvester has the following specifications: **Load = 50 kN**, **Mean diameter = 50 mm**, **Pitch = 8 mm**, **Coefficient of friction = 0.12**, **Handle length = 350 mm**.

**(a) Calculate the torque required to raise the load and the efficiency. Also verify if the jack is self-locking.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) If a V-thread (ACME, thread angle $\beta = 29°$) is used instead with the same friction coefficient, find the new torque to raise the load and compare efficiencies.** *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Given:**
$P = 50{,}000$ N, $d = 0.050$ m, $L = p = 0.008$ m, $\mu = 0.12$, $l = 0.350$ m.

**Part (a) — Square-thread analysis:**

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{L}{\pi d} = \frac{8}{\pi \times 50} = 0.05093$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.05093) = 2.916°$$

$$\tan\phi = 0.12 \implies \phi = \tan^{-1}(0.12) = 6.843°$$

$$\alpha + \phi = 9.759°$$

$$\tan(9.759°) = 0.17185$$

$$T_r = \frac{50{,}000 \times 0.050}{2} \times 0.17185 = 1250 \times 0.17185 = 214.81 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\eta = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha+\phi)} = \frac{0.05093}{0.17185} = 0.2964 = 29.64\%$$

**Self-locking:** $\alpha = 2.916° < \phi = 6.843°$ ✓ → **Self-locking confirmed**.

**Part (b) — V-thread (ACME) modification:**

Equivalent friction coefficient for ACME thread ($\beta = 29°$):

$$\mu' = \frac{\mu}{\cos(\beta/2)} = \frac{0.12}{\cos(14.5°)} = \frac{0.12}{0.9681} = 0.1239$$

New friction angle:

$$\phi' = \tan^{-1}(0.1239) = 7.067°$$

Helix angle remains the same: $\alpha = 2.916°$.

$$\alpha + \phi' = 2.916° + 7.067° = 9.983°$$

$$\tan(9.983°) = 0.17615$$

New torque to raise (V-thread):

$$T_r' = \frac{50{,}000 \times 0.050}{2} \times 0.17615 = 1250 \times 0.17615 = 220.19 \text{ N·m}$$

New efficiency:

$$\eta' = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan(\alpha+\phi')} = \frac{0.05093}{0.17615} = 0.2891 = 28.91\%$$

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Square Thread | ACME (V) Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Torque to raise | 214.81 N·m | 220.19 N·m |
| Efficiency | 29.64% | 28.91% |
| Δ Torque | — | +5.38 N·m (≈ +2.5%) |
| Δ Efficiency | — | −0.73% |

**Conclusion:** ACME threads are slightly less efficient than square threads due to the wedging action at the thread flanks, but they are easier to manufacture and provide better centering — making them preferred for practical agricultural jacks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Pitch and diameter must be in **meters** when computing torque in N·m. Many students lose 1–2 marks by mixing mm and m.
> 2. **Sign confusion in $T_l$:** A negative value of $T_l$ does **not** mean physically negative torque — it means the screw is self-locking. Always state this interpretation clearly.
> 3. **Skipping self-locking verification:** KTU explicitly tests this. Even if not asked, always state whether $\alpha < \phi$.
> 4. **Not using $\mu'$ for V-threads:** When the problem specifies a V-thread or ACME thread, using plain $\mu$ in the formula loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Wrong angle conversion:** Angles must be in **degrees** when using calculators in DEG mode, OR converted to **radians** in RAD mode. Mismatch leads to wildly wrong answers.
> 6. **Not reporting efficiency as a percentage:** Report $\eta = 0.296$ as **29.64%** unless otherwise stated.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **screw jack** converts rotation into linear lift via helical thread engagement.
- ✅ **Helix angle**: $\tan\alpha = L / (\pi d)$ — always compute first.
- ✅ **Friction angle**: $\tan\phi = \mu$ — derive from the given coefficient of friction.
- ✅ **Torque to raise**: $T_r = (Pd/2)\tan(\alpha + \phi)$ — single most-tested formula.
- ✅ **Torque to lower**: $T_l = (Pd/2)\tan(\alpha - \phi)$ — sign reveals self-locking.
- ✅ **Efficiency**: $\eta = \tan\alpha / \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ — must be $< 1$.
- ✅ **Maximum efficiency** when $\alpha = 45° - \phi/2$.
- ✅ **Self-locking condition**: $\alpha < \phi$ — mandatory for agricultural lifting jacks.
- ✅ **V-thread (ACME)** equivalent friction: $\mu' = \mu / \cos(\beta/2)$.
- ✅ **Effort at handle**: $F = T_r / l$ where $l$ is lever arm length.
- ✅ **Mechanical advantage** of a screw jack = $P / F$ — often in the range 40–100.
- ✅ Units discipline: Force in N, length in **m**, torque in **N·m**, angles in **degrees**.
- ✅ Always interpret $T_l < 0$ as **self-locking**, not as an impossible scenario.
- ✅ For agricultural ploughs, harvesters, and trailers — **square threads preferred** for max efficiency; **ACME threads preferred** for ease of manufacture and durability.
- ✅ Watch out for KTU's standard problem pattern: "Given P, d, L/p, $\mu$, $l$ → find $T_r$, $T_l$, $\eta$, self-locking status, and $F$."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lead Screw
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT604 - DESIGN OF AGRICULTURAL IMPLEMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lead Screw

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lead Screw (Power Screw):** A lead screw is a mechanical linear actuator that converts rotational motion into precise linear motion, and vice versa, through a threaded shaft engaging with a mating nut. In agricultural engineering, it is classified under *simple machine elements* and is specifically termed a **power screw** when substantial forces must be transmitted (lifting, clamping, or positioning heavy loads).

A lead screw transmits power by virtue of the sliding contact between the helical threads of the screw and the nut. The term **"lead" (L)** denotes the axial distance advanced by the screw in one complete revolution; the term **"pitch" (p)** denotes the axial distance between adjacent thread crests.

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-start vs. Multi-start Threads:**
> For a single-start thread: $L = p$
> For a multi-start thread (n starts): $L = n \times p$

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ramps Inside the Thread"

Imagine you are rolling a heavy cylindrical barrel (the **load W**) up a spiral staircase (the **helical thread**) that wraps around a vertical pole. Each step of the staircase is tilted at a small angle, called the **helix angle (α)**. The smaller this angle, the harder it is to push the barrel up — but the barrel will not roll back on its own (this is called **self-locking**). The larger the helix angle, the easier it is to lift — but the load may descend by itself.

A lead screw works exactly on this principle. The thread is a **ramp wrapped around a cylinder**. When you rotate the screw:
- A **small helix angle** → high mechanical advantage, but friction dominates → low efficiency but self-locking.
- A **large helix angle** → low mechanical advantage, low friction effect → high efficiency, but no self-locking.

This is exactly why **screw jacks** (used for lifting tractors or levelling heavy ploughs in the field) use a lead screw: they hold the load firmly without slipping back.

> [!TIP]
> **Real agricultural example:** A tractor-mounted **hydraulic-type mechanical screw jack** for raising an attached cultivator or rotavator uses a lead screw with low helix angle (~3° to 5°) for safe, self-locking operation in field conditions.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Lead Screws Used in Agricultural Machinery

| Type | Description | Agricultural Use |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| **Screw Jack** | Lifts heavy loads vertically | Levelling ploughs, raising tines, lifting tractor attachments |
| **Clamping Screw** | Holds workpieces tightly | Clamping of implement frames, seed-cum-fertilizer drill frames |
| **Adjusting Screw** | Fine positioning control | Depth control of tynes, sprayer boom height adjustment |
| **Actuating Screw** | Converts motion in mechanisms | Seed metering shutters, fertilizer hopper gates |

---

### 1.4 Standard Thread Profiles in Lead Screws

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** Always draw the thread profile and label the included angle for each type.

| Thread Type | Included Angle | Application |
|-------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Square Thread** | 0° (flat flanks) | Highest efficiency; used in screw jacks, lathes |
| **ACME / Trapezoidal Thread** | **29°** | Most common in machine tools; replaces square due to easier machining |
| **Buttress Thread** | **45° on one side, 7° on other** | Heavy unidirectional loads (e.g., screw presses for oil extraction) |
| **V-Thread (ISO Metric)** | **60°** | General purpose; poor efficiency, used where low cost matters |

---

### 1.5 Key Geometric Terminology (with symbols)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Nominal / Major diameter | $d_o$ | Outer diameter of the thread |
| Core / Minor diameter | $d_c$ | Inner (root) diameter of the thread |
| Pitch diameter / Mean diameter | $d_m$ | $\frac{d_o + d_c}{2}$ — the diameter at which the thread thickness equals the groove width |
| Pitch | $p$ | Axial distance between adjacent thread crests |
| Lead | $L$ | Axial advance per revolution |
| Helix angle | $\alpha$ | $\tan(\alpha) = \frac{L}{\pi \, d_m}$ |
| Thread depth / Height | $h$ | For square: $h = \frac{p}{2}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mechanics of a Lead Screw — Force Model

A lead screw can be analysed as an **inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder**. When the screw rotates and lifts a load $W$:

1. The axial load $W$ acts on the thread surface at the mean radius $r_m = d_m/2$.
2. A tangential force $P$ must be applied at radius $r_m$ to overcome the axial resistance.
3. Friction force $\mu N$ acts along the thread surface, opposing motion.
4. Normal reaction $N$ acts perpendicular to the thread flank.

The two key operating conditions are:
- **Raising the load** → friction opposes upward sliding of the load.
- **Lowering the load** → friction reverses direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why square threads are most efficient:**
> The square thread has no radial component of normal force, so the entire thrust force is borne axially. This makes it the theoretical ideal — though ACME threads are preferred industrially due to ease of cutting and self-centering.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical**. Memorize the symbols: $W$ = load, $d_m$ = mean diameter, $L$ = lead, $\alpha$ = helix angle, $\phi$ = friction angle ($\tan\phi = \mu$).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Application |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Helix angle | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{L}{\pi \, d_m}$ | Geometry |
| 2 | Torque to raise load | $T_1 = \dfrac{W \, d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | Screw jack lifting |
| 3 | Torque to lower load | $T_2 = \dfrac{W \, d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha - \phi)$ | Lowering under control |
| 4 | Efficiency of screw | $\eta = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$ | Performance check |
| 5 | Self-locking condition | $\alpha \le \phi$ | Safety against back-driving |
| 6 | Maximum efficiency condition | $\alpha = 45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}$ | Optimal design |
| 7 | Effort at mean radius (raising) | $P_1 = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | At the handle |
| 8 | Bearing pressure (square thread) | $p_b = \dfrac{W}{\pi \, d_m \, h \, n}$ | Wear / crushing check |
| 9 | Overturning moment on nut | $M_o = \dfrac{W \, d_m}{2} \sin \alpha$ | Used for ACME only |
| 10 | Safe load against buckling | $W_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_{eff}^{\,2}}$ | Long slender screws |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Board-Exam Pitfall:**
> 1. Many students confuse **lead (L)** with **pitch (p)**. For single-start threads they are equal, but for multi-start threads $L = n \times p$.
> 2. The **friction angle $\phi$** must be in **radians** (or consistently in degrees) inside $\tan(\alpha + \phi)$. Mixing units is the most common valuation error.
> 3. The formula $T_1 = \dfrac{W d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ is for **square threads**. For ACME threads, the formula must include the **overturning term** $W \tan \alpha \cdot \tan \beta$ correction.

---

### 2.3 Design Considerations for Agricultural Lead Screws

A lead screw used in agricultural machinery must be designed for:

| Design Criterion | Why It Matters |
|------------------|----------------|
| **Bearing / Wear Pressure ($p_b \le p_{b,allow}$)** | Prevents thread stripping under repeated field loading |
| **Crushing / Shear Stress** | Threads must not fail by shear at the root |
| **Buckling Load** | Long screws (e.g., drawbar adjusters) act as columns |
| **Efficiency** | Determines operator effort and power consumption |
| **Self-locking** | Essential for jacks, otherwise dangerous in field use |
| **Material Selection** | Must resist corrosion from mud, water, and fertilizers |

> [!TIP]
> **Materials commonly used:**
> - **Screw:** Medium-carbon steel (e.g., EN8, AISI 1045) — hardened and ground.
> - **Nut:** Bronze (leaded gunmetal, phosphor bronze) — for low wear and good conformability.
> - **For soil-engaging screws:** Stainless steel 304/316 or epoxy-coated alloy steels.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Agricultural Engineering

Lead screws find application in:

- **Screw Jacks** for raising/lowering tractor-mounted ploughs, cultivators, and rotavators.
- **Seed Drill Depth Control** wheels — fine adjustment of sowing depth.
- **Sprayer Boom Leveling** — automatic or manual height correction.
- **Cotton Picker Spindle Banks** — height adjustment for crop clearance.
- **Sugarcane Harvester Base Cutters** — precision cutting height.
- **Hydraulic Presses for Oil Expulsion** — high-pressure buttress threads.
- **Tractor Trailer Couplings** — screw-type automatic hitches in some designs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Torque Required to Raise a Load (Square Threaded Power Screw)

**Step 1 — Unwrap the thread as an inclined plane.**
Imagine cutting the cylindrical thread along an axial line and unwrapping it flat. The helix becomes a straight ramp inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal.

Let:
- $W$ = axial load (weight to be lifted)
- $P$ = tangential effort applied at mean radius $r_m = d_m/2$
- $N$ = normal reaction on the thread flank
- $\mu N$ = friction force, with $\mu = \tan \phi$
- $\alpha$ = helix angle (slope of the ramp)

**Step 2 — Resolve forces along the inclined plane (direction of motion).**

$$P \cos \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu N$$

**Step 3 — Resolve forces perpendicular to the inclined plane.**

$$N = W \cos \alpha + P \sin \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (3) into (2).**

$$P \cos \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu(W \cos \alpha + P \sin \alpha)$$

**Step 5 — Group the $P$ terms on one side.**

$$P \cos \alpha - \mu P \sin \alpha = W \sin \alpha + \mu W \cos \alpha$$

$$P(\cos \alpha - \mu \sin \alpha) = W(\sin \alpha + \mu \cos \alpha)$$

**Step 6 — Divide numerator and denominator by $\cos \alpha$.**

$$P = W \cdot \frac{\tan \alpha + \mu}{1 - \mu \tan \alpha}$$

**Step 7 — Recognize the compound-angle identity.**

Using $\mu = \tan \phi$:

$$P = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

**Step 8 — Convert tangential force to torque.**

The effort $P$ acts at radius $r_m = d_m/2$, so the required torque is:

$$\boxed{\,T_1 = P \cdot \frac{d_m}{2} = \frac{W \, d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi)\,}$$

This is the **fundamental torque equation** for raising a load using a square-threaded power screw.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Torque Required to Lower a Load

When the load is being lowered, friction now acts **up the inclined plane** (it opposes the downward motion). Following the same force balance with reversed friction direction:

$$P \cos \alpha + \mu N = W \sin \alpha$$
$$N = W \cos \alpha + P \sin \alpha$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$P = W \tan(\alpha - \phi)$$

Hence the torque to lower the load is:

$$\boxed{\,T_2 = \frac{W \, d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha - \phi)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Note:** For $T_2$ to be **positive** (i.e., effort needed to lower), we require $\alpha > \phi$. If $\alpha < \phi$, the screw is **self-locking** and the load will not descend even if $P = 0$. This is the **self-locking criterion**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Efficiency of a Power Screw

**Step 1 — Define work output.**
In one full revolution of the screw, the load $W$ is lifted by exactly the lead $L$ (single-start thread). Therefore:

$$W_{\text{out}} = W \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Define work input.**
The effort $P$ acts at radius $r_m = d_m/2$ over a circular path of length $2\pi \, r_m$:

$$W_{\text{in}} = P \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{d_m}{2} = P \cdot \pi \, d_m$$

**Step 3 — Form the efficiency ratio.**

$$\eta = \frac{W_{\text{out}}}{W_{\text{in}}} = \frac{W \cdot L}{P \cdot \pi \, d_m}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $P = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$.**

$$\eta = \frac{W \cdot L}{W \tan(\alpha + \phi) \cdot \pi \, d_m} = \frac{L}{\pi \, d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the helix-angle identity $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{L}{\pi \, d_m}$.**

$$\boxed{\,\eta = \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}\,}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

We wish to find the value of $\alpha$ that maximizes $\eta(\alpha) = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate $\eta$ with respect to $\alpha$ and set to zero.**

Let $f(\alpha) = \tan \alpha$ and $g(\alpha) = \tan(\alpha + \phi)$. Then:

$$\frac{d\eta}{d\alpha} = \frac{g \cdot f' - f \cdot g'}{g^2} = 0$$

This implies:

$$\tan(\alpha + \phi) \cdot \sec^2 \alpha - \tan \alpha \cdot \sec^2(\alpha + \phi) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange.**

$$\tan(\alpha + \phi) \sec^2 \alpha = \tan \alpha \sec^2(\alpha + \phi)$$

Rewriting in terms of sines and cosines:

$$\frac{\sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos(\alpha + \phi)} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos^2 \alpha} = \frac{\sin \alpha}{\cos \alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos^2(\alpha + \phi)}$$

$$\sin(\alpha + \phi) \cos(\alpha + \phi) = \sin \alpha \cos \alpha$$

**Step 3 — Apply double-angle identity $2 \sin \theta \cos \theta = \sin 2\theta$.**

$$\sin\bigl(2(\alpha + \phi)\bigr) = \sin(2\alpha)$$

**Step 4 — Solve the trigonometric equation.**

The general solution is either $2(\alpha + \phi) = 2\alpha$ (trivial, $\phi = 0$) or:

$$2(\alpha + \phi) = 180° - 2\alpha$$

$$4\alpha + 2\phi = 180°$$

$$\boxed{\,\alpha_{\text{max}} = 45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum efficiency at this helix angle.**

Substituting back:

$$\eta_{\text{max}} = \frac{\tan(45° - \phi/2)}{\tan(45° + \phi/2)} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Bearing Pressure on a Square Thread

For a square thread with thread depth $h$, mean diameter $d_m$, and $n$ threads in contact:

**Bearing surface area** (projected cylindrical contact):

$$A_b = \pi \, d_m \cdot h \cdot n$$

**Bearing pressure:**

$$\boxed{\,p_b = \frac{W}{\pi \, d_m \, h \, n}\,}$$

For a single-threaded nut, $n = 1$. The allowable bearing pressure $p_{b,allow}$ is typically **5 to 10 N/mm²** for steel-on-bronze pairs (slow speeds, lubricated).

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A square-threaded screw jack lifts a load $W = 30 \text{ kN}$. Nominal diameter $d_o = 50$ mm, pitch $p = 8$ mm, coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.10$. Find: (i) helix angle $\alpha$, (ii) torque $T_1$ to raise the load, (iii) efficiency $\eta$, (iv) torque $T_2$ to lower the load. Check if the screw is self-locking.

**Given:** $W = 30\,000$ N, $d_o = 50$ mm, $p = 8$ mm (single-start $\Rightarrow L = 8$ mm), $\mu = 0.10$.

**Step (i) — Helix angle.**

Mean diameter: $d_m = d_o - p/2 = 50 - 4 = 46$ mm.

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{L}{\pi \, d_m} = \frac{8}{\pi \times 46} = \frac{8}{144.51} = 0.05536$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.05536) = 3.17°$$

**Step (ii) — Torque to raise.**

Friction angle: $\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.10) = 5.71°$.

$$\alpha + \phi = 3.17° + 5.71° = 8.88°$$

$$T_1 = \frac{W \, d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi) = \frac{30\,000 \times 46}{2} \times \tan(8.88°)$$

$$T_1 = 690\,000 \times 0.1562 = 107\,800 \text{ N·mm} \approx 107.8 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step (iii) — Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} = \frac{0.05536}{0.1562} = 0.354 = 35.4\%$$

**Step (iv) — Torque to lower.**

$$\alpha - \phi = 3.17° - 5.71° = -2.54°$$

Since $\alpha < \phi$, the quantity $\tan(\alpha - \phi)$ is **negative**, meaning $T_2 < 0$.

$$T_2 = \frac{30\,000 \times 46}{2} \times \tan(-2.54°) = 690\,000 \times (-0.0443) = -30\,600 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Negative sign → load cannot be lowered by a positive torque → the screw is SELF-LOCKING.**

This is exactly the safe behaviour required in a **field screw jack** used with a tractor.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Lead Screw System in an Agricultural Implement

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Operator Handle or Motor Torque T1] --> B[Lead Screw Shaft with Helical Thread]
    B --> C[Thread Engagement with Mating Nut]
    C --> D[Linear Motion Output Axial Force W]
    D --> E[Implement Component Moved]
    E1[Plough Depth Wheel] --> E
    E2[Sprayer Boom Height] --> E
    E3[Seed Drill Depth Lever] --> E
    E4[Cultivator Tine Height] --> E
    F[Friction at Thread Surfaces μN] --> C
    G[Self Locking Check alpha le phi] --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **A → B:** Input torque applied at the screw head (hand wheel or motor).
- **B → C:** Helical thread converts rotation into linear motion via the nut.
- **C → D:** Axial force generated, lifting or pressing the implement component.
- **E → (E1..E4):** Specific agricultural applications.
- **F & G:** Real-world constraints — friction loss and self-locking verification.

---

### 4.2 Force Resolution on the Unwrapped Helix (Inclined-Plane Model)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Axial Load W acting vertically] --> B[Decompose along thread plane]
    C[Tangential Effort P along motion] --> B
    B --> D[Normal Reaction N perpendicular to thread]
    B --> E[Friction Force μN opposing motion]
    D --> F[Resultant Force on thread flank]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Net Driving Torque T1]
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Matrix — Type of Lead Screw for Agricultural Use

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Choose Lead Screw Type] --> Q1{Load Direction Unidirectional?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> But[Buttress Thread - Sugarcane Press]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Self Locking Required?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Heavy Load lifting?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Sq[Square Thread - Tractor Jack]
    Q3 -- No --> Ac[ACME Thread - Adjustable Wheel]
    Q2 -- No --> Hi[High Helix Ball Screw - Precision Seeder]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Logic Summary:**
> - **Square thread** → maximum efficiency, self-locking, jacks.
> - **ACME thread** → balanced performance, easy machining, depth adjusters.
> - **Buttress thread** → heavy one-way loads, oil expellers.
> - **Ball screw** → high precision, low friction, automatic seeders.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Define a lead screw. Distinguish clearly between "lead" and "pitch" with suitable sketches.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A lead screw is a threaded shaft used to convert rotational motion into linear motion (or vice versa) for transmitting power, lifting loads, or providing precise positioning in agricultural machinery such as screw jacks, seed drills, and sprayers.

- **Lead (L):** The axial distance travelled by the screw (or nut) in **one complete revolution**.
- **Pitch (p):** The axial distance between **two adjacent thread crests**.

For a **single-start** thread: $L = p$
For a **multi-start** thread (with $n$ starts): $L = n \cdot p$

*(Sketch: Show two parallel threads with markings of pitch and lead — 1 mark, definition & formula — 2 marks.)*

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**State and explain the self-locking condition for a power screw. Why is this property important in agricultural jacks?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A lead screw is said to be **self-locking** if it does not rotate (and hence the load does not descend) even when the applied torque is removed. The condition is:

$$\alpha \le \phi \quad \text{where} \quad \tan \phi = \mu$$

Here $\alpha$ is the helix angle and $\phi$ is the friction angle.

**Importance in agricultural jacks:** Screw jacks used to lift tractors, ploughs, and rotavators must **hold the load indefinitely** without slipping back, even under vibrations from the engine or uneven field terrain. Self-locking ensures operator safety and prevents accidental lowering of heavy implements.

*(Condition — 1 mark, explanation — 1 mark, agricultural relevance — 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the torque required to raise a load $W$ using a square-threaded power screw. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** for the full derivation.

**Key Result:**

$$\boxed{\,T_1 = \frac{W \, d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi)\,}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Uniform thread geometry; square profile with no radial force component.
2. Uniform pressure distribution on the thread.
3. Friction coefficient $\mu$ is constant at the thread surface.
4. The load is purely axial and concentric with the screw axis.
5. Negligible inertia effects (quasi-static analysis).

**Mark Split (as per KTU valuation key):**
- Free-body diagram of unwrapped inclined plane: **2 marks**
- Force equilibrium equations: **2 marks**
- Algebraic manipulation to compound-angle form: **2 marks**
- Final torque expression: **1 mark**

---

**(b)** A square-threaded screw jack has the following specifications: **(7 Marks)**

Nominal diameter $d_o = 60$ mm, pitch $p = 10$ mm, coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.12$, load $W = 50$ kN.

Calculate:
(i) The mean diameter
(ii) The helix angle
(iii) The torque required to raise the load
(iv) The torque required to lower the load
(v) The efficiency of the screw
(vi) Whether the screw is self-locking.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $d_o = 60$ mm, $p = 10$ mm (single start → $L = 10$ mm), $\mu = 0.12$, $W = 50\,000$ N.

**(i) Mean diameter:**

$$d_m = d_o - \frac{p}{2} = 60 - 5 = 55 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Helix angle:**

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{L}{\pi \, d_m} = \frac{10}{\pi \times 55} = \frac{10}{172.79} = 0.05788$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.05788) = 3.31°$$

**(iii) Torque to raise:**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.12) = 6.84°$$
$$\alpha + \phi = 3.31° + 6.84° = 10.15°$$
$$T_1 = \frac{W \, d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi) = \frac{50\,000 \times 55}{2} \times \tan(10.15°)$$
$$T_1 = 1\,375\,000 \times 0.1790 = 246\,125 \text{ N·mm} \approx 246.1 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iv) Torque to lower:**

$$\alpha - \phi = 3.31° - 6.84° = -3.53°$$
$$T_2 = 1\,375\,000 \times \tan(-3.53°) = 1\,375\,000 \times (-0.0617)$$
$$T_2 = -84\,838 \text{ N·mm} \approx -84.8 \text{ N·m}$$

**(v) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} = \frac{0.05788}{0.1790} = 0.3233 = 32.33\%$$

**(vi) Self-locking check:**

Since $\alpha = 3.31° < \phi = 6.84°$, the condition $\alpha < \phi$ is satisfied.

**Therefore, the screw is SELF-LOCKING.**

**Mark Split:**
- Each sub-part (i)–(vi): **1 mark** + **0.5 mark** for units/conditions → totals to 7 marks.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the condition for maximum efficiency of a power screw. Also state the self-locking condition and explain its significance in agricultural machinery. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Refer to **Section 3.3** and **Section 3.4** for the full derivation.

**Key Results:**

- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$
- **Condition for maximum efficiency:** $\alpha = 45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}$
- **Maximum efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{max}} = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$
- **Self-locking condition:** $\alpha \le \phi$

**Significance in Agricultural Machinery:**
1. **Screw jacks** for tractor/implement lifting require **self-locking** to hold loads safely.
2. **Seed drill depth adjusters** operate near the maximum-efficiency helix angle for smooth hand operation.
3. **Sprayer boom height adjusters** in field conditions need **quick manual positioning**, hence moderate-to-high helix angle.
4. **Oil-expeller press screws** (buttress threads) operate in **highly efficient regimes** since high forces are transmitted continuously.
5. The choice of helix angle balances **efficiency**, **mechanical advantage**, and **self-locking** — a fundamental design trade-off.

**Mark Split:**
- Derivation of efficiency formula: **2 marks**
- Derivation of $\alpha = 45° - \phi/2$: **3 marks**
- Self-locking condition + significance: **2 marks**

---

**(b)** With neat sketches, explain the following thread profiles used in power screws and mention their specific agricultural applications: **(7 Marks)**

(i) Square thread
(ii) ACME thread
(iii) Buttress thread
(iv) V-thread

**Model Answer (Tabular Form):**

| Thread | Included Angle | Key Feature | Agricultural Application |
|--------|---------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Square** | 0° (flat) | Highest efficiency; no radial thrust | Tractor jacks, cultivator depth wheel |
| **ACME / Trapezoidal** | 29° | Easier to cut; self-centering | Seed drill depth adjustment, sprayer height |
| **Buttress** | 45° + 7° asymmetric | Handles heavy unidirectional thrust | Sugarcane crushers, oil expeller screws |
| **V-Thread (ISO)** | 60° | Low cost, general purpose | Light clamps, hopper gates |

**Mark Split:**
- Sketches of four thread profiles: **2 marks**
- Description of features: **2 marks**
- Agricultural applications: **3 marks** (at least one specific application per thread)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Mixing units of angle** (radians vs degrees) inside $\tan(\alpha + \phi)$ — **deduct 0.5 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $\mu$ to friction angle $\phi$** before substituting — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 3. **Using pitch instead of lead** (or vice versa) in a multi-start thread problem — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 4. **Not stating the self-locking condition explicitly** when raising/lowering question — **deduct 0.5 mark**.
> 5. **Showing the inclined-plane diagram incompletely** (missing $\mu N$ direction or $N$ location) — **deduct 0.5 mark**.
> 6. **No sketch of thread profile** in the descriptive question — **deduct 1 mark** (KTU strictly penalises absent sketches).
> 7. **Ignoring the column-buckling check** in long-screw design problems — **deduct 1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Lead Screw (Module 3, PBAGT604)**

- [x] **Lead Screw** = threaded shaft converting rotation ↔ linear motion.
- [x] **Lead (L)** = axial advance per revolution; **Pitch (p)** = distance between adjacent crests.
- [x] For **single-start** thread: $L = p$. For **multi-start**: $L = n \cdot p$.
- [x] **Helix angle:** $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{L}{\pi \, d_m}$.
- [x] **Torque to raise:** $T_1 = \dfrac{W d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi)$.
- [x] **Torque to lower:** $T_2 = \dfrac{W d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha - \phi)$.
- [x] **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$.
- [x] **Self-locking condition:** $\alpha \le \phi$.
- [x] **Maximum efficiency condition:** $\alpha = 45° - \phi/2$.
- [x] **Maximum efficiency value:** $\eta_{\text{max}} = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$.
- [x] **Bearing pressure (square thread):** $p_b = \dfrac{W}{\pi d_m h n}$.
- [x] **Buckling check:** $W_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_{eff}^{\,2}}$ (for long slender screws).
- [x] **Square thread** → best for screw jacks; **ACME** → general machinery; **Buttress** → oil presses; **V-thread** → low-cost clamps.
- [x] **Material pairing:** Hardened steel screw + bronze nut.
- [x] **Agricultural applications:** Tractor jacks, depth wheels, sprayer adjusters, seed drills, oil expellers.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Mnemonic for KTU Viva:**
> **"Lead Squared, Angle Plussed, Friction Minus, Efficiency Tanned"**
> → $T_1 = \tfrac{W d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ — $T_2 = \tfrac{W d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha - \phi)$ — $\eta = \tfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$

---

topics.title: Selection of Anti-friction Bearings - Ball Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT604 - DESIGN OF AGRICULTURAL IMPLEMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection of Anti-friction Bearings - Ball Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Anti-friction Bearings?

An **anti-friction bearing** is a bearing which carries the load with the help of small balls or rollers placed between two concentric races. Since the load is transferred through rolling contact rather than sliding contact, the friction between the moving parts is greatly reduced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Anti-friction bearings are rolling element bearings in which the load is transmitted from one race to another through rolling elements (balls or rollers) which have a much lower coefficient of friction than sliding bearings."

### 1.2 Classification of Anti-friction Bearings

Anti-friction bearings are broadly classified into two main categories:

| Type | Rolling Element | Load Carrying Capacity | Friction | Cost |
|------|----------------|----------------------|----------|------|
| **Ball Bearings** | Spherical balls | Lower | Lower | Lower |
| **Roller Bearings** | Cylindrical/Tapered rollers | Higher | Slightly higher | Higher |

> [!NOTE]
> Ball bearings are the most commonly used type of anti-friction bearing in agricultural machinery due to their **low cost**, **simple construction**, and ability to carry both **radial and axial (thrust) loads**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a ball bearing as a "skateboard on marbles." Imagine trying to push a heavy box across the floor — it sticks and drags. Now place a layer of marbles (or steel balls) under the box. Suddenly, it glides effortlessly. That's exactly what a ball bearing does: it replaces sliding friction with rolling friction, which can be **up to 1000 times smaller**.

In agricultural machinery like **tractors, threshers, and pumps**, ball bearings reduce wear, increase speed, and improve operational efficiency.

### 1.4 Construction of a Deep Groove Ball Bearing

A standard deep groove ball bearing consists of:
1. **Outer Race** – Fixed ring mounted in the housing
2. **Inner Race** – Rotating ring mounted on the shaft
3. **Balls** – Hardened steel rolling elements (typically **10 mm to 50 mm** diameter)
4. **Cage/Retainer** – Keeps the balls uniformly spaced and prevents contact between them
5. **Shields/Seals** – Prevent contamination by dust, mud, and moisture (critical in agriculture)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **agricultural applications**, bearings are exposed to dust, water, straw, and chaff. Hence, bearings with **2RS (rubber seals on both sides)** or **2Z (metal shields on both sides)** are preferred.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Selection Criteria for Ball Bearings

The selection of a ball bearing for any application is based on the following key parameters:

1. **Magnitude of load** (Radial and Axial)
2. **Speed of operation** (in rpm)
3. **Expected life** (in hours or million revolutions)
4. **Type of loading** (Steady, Fluctuating, or Shock)
5. **Operating environment** (Temperature, contamination)
6. **Mounting requirements** (Shaft and housing tolerances)

### 2.2 Fundamental Concepts

#### A. Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C)
The **basic dynamic load rating** is defined as the constant radial load which a bearing can theoretically endure for a rating life of **1 million revolutions** without developing fatigue cracks.

#### B. Basic Static Load Rating (C₀)
The **basic static load rating** is the static load that produces a permanent deformation of **0.0001 times the ball diameter** at the most heavily loaded contact point.

#### C. Equivalent Dynamic Bearing Load (P)
Since most bearings experience a combination of radial and axial loads, the actual load is converted into an **equivalent dynamic load**:

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

where:
- $P$ = Equivalent dynamic load (N)
- $F_r$ = Radial load on the bearing (N)
- $F_a$ = Axial (thrust) load on the bearing (N)
- $X$ = Radial load factor
- $Y$ = Axial load factor

#### D. Rating Life Equation (AFBMA / ISO Standard)

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

where:
- $L_{10}$ = Rating life in **million revolutions** (90% reliability)
- $C$ = Basic dynamic load rating (N)
- $P$ = Equivalent dynamic bearing load (N)
- $p$ = Exponent of the life equation:
  - $p = 3$ for **ball bearings**
  - $p = \frac{10}{3}$ for **roller bearings**

#### E. Life in Working Hours

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

For ball bearings ($p = 3$):

$$L_{10h} = \frac{16666}{n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3$$

where:
- $L_{10h}$ = Life in hours
- $n$ = Rotational speed (rpm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The exponent **p = 3** for ball bearings means that **doubling the load reduces life to 1/8th**. This is why overload protection is critical in agricultural machinery.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Application |
|---------|----------|-------------|
| Equivalent Dynamic Load | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | Combined load conversion |
| Equivalent Static Load | $P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a$ | Static load check |
| Dynamic Life (Revolutions) | $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ | Million revolutions |
| Dynamic Life (Hours) | $L_{10h} = (16666/n) \cdot (C/P)^3$ | Ball bearings |
| Dynamic Life (Hours) | $L_{10h} = (50000/n) \cdot (C/P)^{10/3}$ | Roller bearings |
| Required Dynamic Load | $C = P \cdot (L_{10h} \cdot n / 16666)^{1/3}$ | Design calculation |
| Load Ratio | $f_a = F_a / C_0$ | Determines X, Y, e |
| Modified Life | $L_{na} = a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot L_{10}$ | For special conditions |
| Axial Force from Angular Contact | $F_a = 0.68 \cdot F_r$ (typical) | Angular contact bearings |
| Rotational Frequency Relation | $f_r = n / 60$ (Hz) | Vibration analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In agricultural equipment like **rotavators, power tillers, combine harvesters, and PTO shafts**, ball bearing selection using these equations ensures **long service life**, **reduced downtime**, and **lower maintenance costs** — critical factors during peak farming seasons (Kharif and Rabi).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Life Equation

The fundamental relationship between load and fatigue life of rolling element bearings is derived from the **Lundberg-Palmgren theory** of rolling contact fatigue.

**Starting point:** The fatigue life of a stressed volume of material under Hertzian contact stress.

**Step 1:** Hertzian contact stress $\sigma$ at the ball-race interface is related to the load $Q$ on a single ball by:

$$\sigma \propto Q^{1/3}$$

**Step 2:** The fatigue life $L$ (in revolutions) of the material is inversely proportional to the cube of the stress:

$$L \propto \sigma^{-3}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the stress-load relationship:

$$L \propto (Q^{1/3})^{-3} = Q^{-1}$$

Therefore:

$$L \propto \frac{1}{Q}$$

**Step 4:** For a complete bearing, the total load $P$ is distributed among multiple rolling elements. The relationship simplifies to:

$$\frac{C}{P} = \left(\frac{L_{10}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging:

$$L_{10} = 10^6 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 6:** Converting to working hours at speed $n$ (rpm):

$$L_{10h} = \frac{L_{10} \cdot 10^6}{60 \cdot n}$$

**Step 7:** Combining for ball bearings ($p = 3$):

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6 \cdot (C/P)^3}{60 \cdot n} = \frac{16666}{n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3$$

### 3.2 Complete Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
A ball bearing carries a radial load of **4000 N** and an axial load of **2000 N**. The shaft rotates at **1440 rpm**. The bearing is required to give a service life of **8000 hours**. Determine the basic dynamic load rating of the bearing.

**Given Data:**
- Radial load, $F_r = 4000$ N
- Axial load, $F_a = 2000$ N
- Speed, $n = 1440$ rpm
- Life, $L_{10h} = 8000$ hours
- Bearing type: Single row deep groove ball bearing
- Assume rotation factor and standard operating conditions

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Estimate equivalent dynamic load (assume $X \approx 0.56$ and $Y \approx 1.6$ for initial check):

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

$$P = (0.56)(4000) + (1.6)(2000)$$

$$P = 2240 + 3200 = 5440 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Use the life equation to find required $C$:

$$L_{10h} = \frac{16666}{n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to solve for $C$:

$$\left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3 = \frac{L_{10h} \cdot n}{16666}$$

$$\left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3 = \frac{8000 \times 1440}{16666}$$

$$\left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3 = \frac{11,520,000}{16,666} = 691.2$$

**Step 4:** Take the cube root:

$$\frac{C}{P} = (691.2)^{1/3} = 8.84$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $C$:

$$C = 8.84 \times P = 8.84 \times 5440$$

$$\boxed{C = 48,090 \text{ N} \approx 48.1 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 6:** Select from manufacturer catalog — Choose a bearing with $C \geq 48,100$ N (typically a **6209** or equivalent SKF/NTN bearing).

**Step 7:** Verification with manufacturer's data — if $C = 50,000$ N, then actual life:

$$L_{10h} = \frac{16666}{1440} \cdot \left(\frac{50000}{5440}\right)^3$$

$$L_{10h} = 11.57 \times (9.19)^3 = 11.57 \times 776.6$$

$$\boxed{L_{10h} \approx 8987 \text{ hours} > 8000 \text{ hours} \checkmark}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Factors Affecting Bearing Selection

| Factor | Effect on Selection | Agricultural Implication |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Radial Load** | Increases $P$, reduces life | Thresher drum, tiller rotor |
| **Axial Load** | Requires angular contact bearing | Inclined shaft, helical gears |
| **Speed (n)** | Higher $n$ → lower life | PTO shaft, fan impeller |
| **Temperature** | Reduces C above 100°C | Engine bearings, exhaust fans |
| **Contamination** | Need 2RS seals | Tractors, harvesters |
| **Shock Load** | Use factor 1.2–1.8 | Soil engagement tools |
| **Vibration** | Use factor 1.5–2.0 | Rotavator, crusher |
| **Misalignment** | Use self-aligning bearing | Long shaft with deflection |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Ball Bearing Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Requirements] --> B[Identify Loads]
    A --> C[Identify Speed]
    A --> D[Identify Life Requirement]
    
    B --> B1[Radial Load Fr]
    B --> B2[Axial Load Fa]
    B --> B3[Shock Factor]
    
    B1 --> E[Calculate Equivalent Load P]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    
    C --> F[Determine Rotational Speed n]
    D --> G[Determine L10h]
    
    E --> H[Apply Life Equation]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    
    H --> I[Calculate Required C]
    
    I --> J{Select from Catalog}
    
    J --> K1[Deep Groove Ball Bearing 6-series]
    J --> K2[Angular Contact Bearing 7-series]
    J --> K3[Self-aligning Bearing 1-series]

    K1 --> L[Verify P-C0 Ratio]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    
    L --> M{Acceptable?}
    
    M -->|Yes| N[Final Selection Complete]
    M -->|No| O[Re-select Higher C]
    O --> J
```

### 4.2 Ball Bearing Load Distribution Schematic

```mermaid

        OR[Outer Race]
        IR[Inner Race]

    end
    
    RAD[Radial Load Fr] --> IR

```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix for Ball Bearing Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Selection] --> Q1{Load Type?}
    
    Q1 -->|Pure Radial| QR[Deep Groove 6-series]
    Q1 -->|Radial + Axial| QA[Angular Contact 7-series]
    Q1 -->|Heavy Radial| QT[Tapered Roller 3-series]
    Q1 -->|Misalignment| QS[Self-aligning 1-series]
    Q1 -->|Thrust Only| QTH[Thrust Ball 5-series]
    
    QR --> Q2{Operating Environment?}
    QA --> Q2
    QT --> Q2
    QS --> Q2
    QTH --> Q2
    
    Q2 -->|Dusty / Wet| Seal[Use 2RS Sealed]
    Q2 -->|Clean / High Speed| Shield[Use 2Z Shielded]
    Q2 -->|High Temp| Spec[Special Steel/Ceramic]
    
    Seal --> End[Final Bearing]
    Shield --> End
    Spec --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define: (a) Basic dynamic load rating, (b) Basic static load rating of a ball bearing.*

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C):** It is the constant radial load (purely radial) which a bearing can theoretically endure for a rating life of **1 million revolutions** without developing fatigue cracks. It is measured in Newtons (N).

(b) **Basic Static Load Rating (C₀):** It is the static load which produces a permanent deformation of **0.0001 times the ball diameter** at the most heavily loaded contact point. It represents the maximum load a bearing can carry when stationary without damage.

---

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the exponent 'p' equal to 3 for ball bearings and 10/3 for roller bearings?*

**Model Answer:**

The exponent 'p' depends on the geometry of the contact between the rolling element and the raceway. For **ball bearings**, the contact is **point contact** (Hertzian elliptical contact), and theoretical analysis based on the Lundberg-Palmgren fatigue theory gives $p = 3$. For **roller bearings**, the contact is **line contact** (cylindrical), which gives a higher load-carrying capacity and $p = 10/3 \approx 3.33$. Hence, roller bearings have a longer life for the same load and speed.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

(a) *Explain the procedure for selection of a ball bearing for a given application. List the factors affecting bearing life. (7 marks)*

(b) *A ball bearing operates on the following duty cycle: (i) Radial load 5000 N at 720 rpm for 30% of time, (ii) Radial load 3000 N at 1440 rpm for 70% of time. Calculate the equivalent constant load for rating life calculation. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Bearing Selection Procedure:**

1. **Identify Application Requirements:** Determine shaft diameter, type of mounting, operating environment.
2. **Determine Loads:** Calculate radial ($F_r$) and axial ($F_a$) loads using shaft analysis.
3. **Determine Speed:** Find rotational speed $n$ in rpm.
4. **Estimate Required Life:** Based on application — typical values:
   - Pumps: 2000–5000 hours
   - Tractors: 5000–10000 hours
   - Continuous duty: >20000 hours
5. **Calculate Equivalent Load:** $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$
6. **Calculate Required C:** Use $C = P \cdot (L_{10h} \cdot n / 16666)^{1/3}$
7. **Select from Catalog:** Choose bearing with $C_{catalog} \geq C_{required}$.
8. **Verify:** Check static load, temperature factor, contamination protection.

**Factors Affecting Bearing Life:**
- Magnitude of load (radial and axial)
- Speed of rotation
- Operating temperature
- Lubrication condition
- Contamination level
- Mounting accuracy and alignment
- Shock and vibration loads
- Material and heat treatment

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Calculate the effective life-weighted speed:
$$n_{eq} = \frac{\sum(n_i \cdot t_i)}{\sum t_i} = \frac{(720 \times 30) + (1440 \times 70)}{30 + 70} = \frac{21600 + 100800}{100} = 1224 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate equivalent dynamic load using load-life cubed relationship:
$$P_{eq} = \left[\frac{\sum(P_i^p \cdot n_i \cdot t_i)}{\sum(n_i \cdot t_i)}\right]^{1/p}$$

With $p = 3$ for ball bearings:
$$P_{eq}^3 = \frac{(5000)^3 \cdot 720 \cdot 30 + (3000)^3 \cdot 1440 \cdot 70}{720 \cdot 30 + 1440 \cdot 70}$$

$$P_{eq}^3 = \frac{125 \times 10^9 \cdot 21600 + 27 \times 10^9 \cdot 100800}{122400}$$

$$P_{eq}^3 = \frac{2.7 \times 10^{15} + 2.72 \times 10^{15}}{122400} = \frac{5.42 \times 10^{15}}{122400} = 4.428 \times 10^{10}$$

$$P_{eq} = (4.428 \times 10^{10})^{1/3} \approx 3535 \text{ N}$$

**Answer:** $P_{eq} \approx 3535$ N, $n_{eq} = 1224$ rpm

---

**Question B (Option 2): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

(a) *With neat sketches, describe the construction and working of a deep groove ball bearing. List its advantages and limitations. (7 marks)*

(b) *A 6208 deep groove ball bearing carries a radial load of 3500 N at a shaft speed of 1750 rpm. The catalog rating for the bearing is: C = 32,500 N and C₀ = 17,300 N. Determine: (i) The life of the bearing in hours, (ii) The probability of survival for 20,000 hours of operation. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction:** A deep groove ball bearing consists of:
- **Inner race** (mounted on shaft)
- **Outer race** (mounted in housing)
- **Balls** (rolling elements, 8–12 in number)
- **Cage/Retainer** (steel or brass, holds balls in position)
- **Seals/Shields** (2RS rubber seals or 2Z metal shields)

**Working:** The shaft rotates with the inner race. Balls roll between inner and outer races, transmitting the load from one race to the other through Hertzian contact. The cage ensures uniform spacing and prevents ball-to-ball contact.

**Advantages:**
- Low friction coefficient (0.001–0.003)
- Simple construction, low cost
- Suitable for high speeds
- Can carry radial and moderate axial loads
- Low maintenance, long service life
- Easy to mount and dismount

**Limitations:**
- Sensitive to misalignment (>0.001 rad)
- Cannot carry heavy axial loads
- Sensitive to contamination
- Limited shock load capacity
- Noise at high speeds
- Standard designs limited to small sizes

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $F_r = 3500$ N, $n = 1750$ rpm
- $C = 32,500$ N, $C_0 = 17,300$ N

**(i) Life in hours:**

For a single radial load, $P = F_r = 3500$ N (assuming $X = 1$ for pure radial load).

$$L_{10h} = \frac{16666}{n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3$$

$$L_{10h} = \frac{16666}{1750} \cdot \left(\frac{32500}{3500}\right)^3$$

$$L_{10h} = 9.524 \cdot (9.286)^3$$

$$L_{10h} = 9.524 \cdot 800.2 = 7621 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Probability of survival for 20,000 hours:**

Weibull distribution gives: $L_n = a_1 \cdot L_{10}$

For 20,000 hours: $L_n / L_{10} = 20000 / 7621 = 2.624$

From Weibull tables for ball bearings: failure probability at this ratio is approximately **52%**.

Therefore, probability of survival $S = 100\% - 52\% = 48\%$.

**Answer:** Life = 7621 hours, Probability of survival ≈ 48% (NOT acceptable — bearing should be re-selected).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (resulting in 2-3 mark loss per question):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert life units:** Using million revolutions directly in hours without dividing by 60n.
> 2. **Wrong exponent:** Using $p = 3.33$ for ball bearings (this is for roller bearings). Always check bearing type.
> 3. **Incorrect equivalent load calculation:** Using $P = F_r + F_a$ directly without the factors X and Y from the load ratio.
> 4. **Not considering shock/vibration factors:** Real agricultural loads are 1.5–2.0× nominal — multiplying at the start avoids repeated recalculation.
> 5. **Unit confusion:** N vs kN — C is typically reported in kN in catalogs but used as N in formulas.
> 6. **Forgetting temperature derating:** Above 100°C, $C$ must be reduced by factor $f_T$.
> 7. **Not verifying C₀:** Always check that $P_0 \leq C_0$ to prevent permanent deformation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ball Bearing Selection:**

✅ **Core Equations to Memorize:**
- $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ (Equivalent load)
- $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ (Life in million revolutions)
- $L_{10h} = (16666/n)(C/P)^3$ (Life in hours, ball bearings)
- $C_{req} = P \cdot (L_{10h} \cdot n/16666)^{1/3}$ (Required dynamic load)

✅ **Key Constants:**
- $p = 3$ for ball bearings (point contact)
- $p = 10/3$ for roller bearings (line contact)
- **1 million revolutions** is the basis of dynamic load rating
- 0.0001 × ball diameter = permanent deformation limit (static)

✅ **Selection Procedure (5 steps):**
1. Find $F_r$, $F_a$, $n$
2. Calculate $P$ using X, Y from load ratio $f_a = F_a/C_0$
3. Use life equation to find required $C$
4. Select catalog bearing with $C_{cat} \geq C_{req}$
5. Verify static load and derating factors

✅ **Standard Design Service Lives (Industry Standard):**
- Agricultural machinery: **3000–6000 hours**
- Tractors and combines: **5000–10000 hours**
- Pumps, fans, gearboxes: **10000–20000 hours**
- Continuous duty machines: **>25000 hours**

✅ **Preferred Bearing Types in Agriculture:**
- **6200-series** (Deep groove): General purpose
- **6300-series** (Heavy duty): Threshers, crushers
- **2RS sealed type:** Outdoor/dusty applications
- **Stainless steel:** Corrosive environment (fertilizer)

✅ **Common Bearing Designations:**
- **6**: Single row deep groove ball bearing
- **2**: Spherical outer race (self-aligning)
- **3**: Angular contact / tapered
- **5**: Thrust ball
- **0**: Double row
- **7**: Angular contact

✅ **Practical Tip for KTU Exams:**
Always state **assumptions clearly**, **show units in every step**, and **use manufacturer data for X, Y, e** rather than assuming values without justification. This is what differentiates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harrows and Cultivators, Design of Mould Board Plough
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT604 - DESIGN OF AGRICULTURAL IMPLEMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Harrows and Cultivators, Design of Mould Board Plough

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Primary Tillage vs Secondary Tillage Implements

In modern mechanised agriculture, soil-engaging implements are classified based on the depth, intensity, and purpose of soil manipulation. Understanding this hierarchy is **fundamental** to Module 3 because the **Mould Board Plough (MBP)** belongs to primary tillage, while **Harrows and Cultivators** belong to secondary/secondary-primary tillage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Tillage**: Deep, aggressive soil inversion, cutting and pulverising, performed after harvest to open the soil profile (depth > 150 mm). Example: Mould Board Plough, Disc Plough.
> **Secondary Tillage**: Shallow conditioning of already loosened soil to refine the seedbed, mix residues, and destroy weeds (depth 50–150 mm). Examples: Harrows, Cultivators, Rotary Tillers.

## 1.2 Definition of a Mould Board Plough (MBP)

A **Mould Board Plough** is a tractor-drawn (or animal-drawn) primary tillage implement consisting of one or more bottoms (bodies) fitted to a common frame, where each bottom comprises a **share**, a **mould board**, a **landside**, and a **frog**, designed to **cut, lift, invert, and pulverise** the soil furrow slice in a single continuous pass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> *"Mould Board Plough — types, parts, design parameters, forces acting on the plough, and draft computation using empirical and analytical approaches."*

### 1.2.1 Real-World Analogy — The "Soil Pancake Flipper"

Imagine a chef flipping a thick pancake on a griddle. The spatula slides under the pancake (the **share** cutting horizontally), lifts it (the **mould board** raising and curving the slice), and flips it completely over (the curved geometry of the board inverts the furrow slice). The edge of the spatula that never leaves the pan is the **landside**, which absorbs the side-thrust. That single spatula is the **plough bottom** — and stacking 3 such spatulas on a frame gives you a **3-bottom plough**.

## 1.3 Definition of a Harrow

A **harrow** is a secondary tillage implement used to break clods, pulverise the soil, mix fertiliser/manure into the topsoil, cover seeds, and level the field surface. Common types are the **Disc Harrow**, **Tooth/Spike Tooth Harrow**, **Spring Tine Harrow**, and **Chain Harrow**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Disc Harrow**: A series of concave steel discs mounted on a common gang shaft, set at an adjustable **gang angle** to the direction of travel, which cuts, lifts, and mixes the soil.

## 1.4 Definition of a Cultivator

A **cultivator** is an implement (rigid tine, spring tine, or duck-foot) used for **inter-row cultivation**, weed control, and shallow secondary tillage (75–150 mm depth) without inverting the soil. It is the workhorse of row-crop farming.

### 1.4.1 Real-World Analogy — The "Soil Comb"

If the mould board plough is the chef's spatula (lift, flip, turn), a **cultivator** is like a **wide-tooth comb** being dragged through hair — it scratches, loosens, and lifts the top layer of soil without flipping it. A **disc harrow** is a **pizza cutter** rolled across dough — it slices the clods into smaller pieces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinguishing Markers for KTU Viva:**
> - **MBP** → inverts furrow slice by **~180°**
> - **Disc Plough** → inverts slice by **~45–90°** (partial inversion)
> - **Cultivator** → **0°** inversion, only loosening and weed cutting
> - **Harrow** → **0°** inversion, mainly clod pulverisation and levelling

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (Bold for emphasis) |
|---|---|
| Furrow depth, $d$ | **150–300 mm** for general purpose MBP |
| Furrow width, $w$ | **250–400 mm** per bottom |
| Ploughing speed | **4–7 km/h** |
| Specific soil resistance | **20–80 kPa** (typical **40 kPa** for medium loam) |
| Coefficient of soil-metal friction, $\mu$ | **0.3–0.6** |
| Disc diameter (Disc Harrow) | **460–660 mm** (commonly **560 mm**) |
| Gang angle, $\alpha$ | **0°–25°** (commonly **17°–22°**) |
| Disc concavity | **75–100 mm** |
| Tine spacing (Cultivator) | **200–300 mm** |
| Sweep width (duck-foot) | **150–300 mm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mould Board Plough — Anatomy & Functional Logic

Each plough bottom is a **soil-engaging mechanism** performing four sequential actions:

1. **Cutting** — the **share** (a sharp, replaceable steel blade) horizontally severs the furrow slice from the unploughed land.
2. **Lifting** — the **mould board** (a curved steel plate) gradually raises the cut slice.
3. **Inverting** — the curved surface rotates the slice through ~180°.
4. **Pulverising** — as the slice bends and shears, it fractures along natural planes of weakness.

### 2.1.1 Three Surface Types of Mould Board (KTU Must-Know)

| Type | Description | Inversion | Pulverisation | Soil Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cylindrical** (e.g., Ames, Victory) | Single radius curvature | Poor | Good | Sandy, loamy |
| **Helicoidal / General Purpose** (e.g., Kverneland, S.B.O.) | Spiral curvature | Good | Moderate | All soils |
| **Stump Jump / Breaker** | Short, abrupt, heavily curved | Best for breaking | Excellent | Heavy, sticky |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern (Repeated 2019, 2021, 2023):** *"Compare the three types of mould board surfaces with neat sketches and discuss their suitability for different soils."*

### 2.1.2 Force Analysis on a Mould Board Bottom

Three primary forces act on the plough bottom during operation:

- $R_x$ — horizontal force **resisting forward motion** (sum of share + mould board + landside resistance components).
- $R_y$ — vertical force (**penetration or upward force**).
- $R_z$ — side force (perpendicular to direction of travel, absorbed by the **landside**).

The resultant $R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2 + R_z^2}$ must be balanced by the **tractive effort of the tractor**, with $R_z$ balanced by the landside reaction to keep the plough tracking straight.

## 2.2 The Disc Harrow — Theoretical Framework

A disc harrow gang has discs that act as **rolling wedges** cutting into the soil. Two geometric parameters dominate the design:

- **Gang angle ($\alpha$)** — angle between the gang axis and the direction of travel.
  - $\alpha = 0°$: no soil throw, only penetration.
  - $\alpha = 21°$ (max for conventional disc harrow): maximum pulverisation.
- **Disc angle / Tilt angle ($\beta$)** — angle the disc makes with the vertical.
  - Controls penetration: typical $\beta = 15°\text{–}25°$.

The disc acts as a **rolling inclined plane**, and the soil-metal interaction follows the classical **inclination plane friction model** used in earth-moving equipment design.

## 2.3 The Cultivator — Theoretical Framework

Cultivator tines are essentially **soil-engaging wedges** (2D or 3D). The **standard earthmoving equation** applies:

$$
D = F \left( \gamma \cdot w \cdot d \cdot \sin\theta + C_a \cdot w \cdot d \right)
$$

Where $D$ is the draft, $F$ is a soil-texture factor, $\gamma$ is specific weight, $w$ is width of cut, $d$ is depth, $\theta$ is tool-soil friction angle, $C_a$ is soil-metal adhesion (a cohesion-like term).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` in tables to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Furrow cross-section area | $A = w \cdot d$ | $w$ = width, $d$ = depth of cut; $\text{m}^2$ |
| 2 | Furrow volume per unit length | $V = w \cdot d \cdot 1$ | $\text{m}^3 / \text{m}$ |
| 3 | Draft (specific resistance) | $D = k \cdot w \cdot d \cdot n$ | $k$ = specific resistance (kPa), $n$ = no. of bottoms |
| 4 | Total draft — ASAE model | $D = A \cdot S_r \cdot (1 + 0.1 V) \cdot C_t$ | $S_r$ = specific resistance, $V$ = speed (km/h), $C_t$ = texture factor |
| 5 | ASAE Standard D497.7 | $D = F_i \cdot \left[ A + B \cdot V + C \cdot V^2 \right] \cdot W \cdot T$ | $F_i$ = dimensionless, $W$ = width, $T$ = depth |
| 6 | Soil-metal friction angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$ | $\mu$ = coefficient of friction |
| 7 | Side-draft angle of MBP | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{R_z}{R_x}$ | $\delta$ = side-draft angle |
| 8 | Penetration force (Mould Board) | $R_y = R \cdot \sin(\gamma)$ | $\gamma$ = upward inclination of resultant |
| 9 | Disc harrow pull | $D_{DH} = n_d \cdot F_d \cdot d_d$ | $n_d$ = no. of discs, $F_d$ = force/disc, $d_d$ = disc diameter |
| 10 | Disc harrow effective width | $W_e = W_n \cdot \cos\alpha$ | $W_n$ = nominal width, $\alpha$ = gang angle |
| 11 | Tine dynamic similarity (Re) | $Re = \dfrac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$ | $L$ = characteristic length (m), $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity |
| 12 | Cultivator draft per tine | $D_t = k_t \cdot w_t \cdot d_t$ | $k_t$ = specific resistance for tine |
| 13 | Field capacity | $C = \dfrac{W \cdot V}{10}$ | ha/h, with $W$ in m, $V$ in km/h |
| 14 | Field efficiency | $\eta_f = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + T_a}$ | $T_t$ = theoretical time, $T_a$ = actual time |

### 2.4.1 Engineering Utility — Why These Formulas Matter

In **tractors and farm machinery design**, these formulas allow engineers to size the **PTO power requirement**, select the **optimum ploughing speed**, and determine the **optimum implement width** for a given tractor. The **ASAE D497.7** model is the global benchmark and is reproduced verbatim in KTU question papers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Draft of a Mould Board Plough Using Specific Resistance

**Given:**
- Furrow width $w = 0.30$ m
- Furrow depth $d = 0.20$ m
- Specific resistance of soil $k = 50$ kPa
- Number of bottoms $n = 3$
- Ploughing speed $V = 5$ km/h

**Find:** Total draft force $D$

**Step 1 — Establish the governing equation.**

The fundamental draft equation for a MBP is:

$$
D = k \cdot A \cdot n = k \cdot (w \cdot d) \cdot n
$$

Where $k$ is the specific resistance, $A$ is the furrow cross-sectional area, and $n$ is the number of bottoms. This is a direct application of the **uniform pressure model** — the soil resists cutting with a constant specific resistance per unit area.

**Step 2 — Compute the cross-sectional area of a single furrow.**

$$
A = w \cdot d = 0.30 \text{ m} \times 0.20 \text{ m} = 0.06 \text{ m}^2
$$

This is the cross-sectional area of soil that one plough bottom must shear, lift, invert, and pulverise per unit length of travel.

**Step 3 — Convert specific resistance to SI units.**

$$
k = 50 \text{ kPa} = 50{,}000 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

Pressure must be in N/m² to maintain dimensional consistency with area in m² when computing force.

**Step 4 — Compute draft per bottom.**

$$
D_{\text{per bottom}} = k \cdot A = 50{,}000 \text{ N/m}^2 \times 0.06 \text{ m}^2 = 3{,}000 \text{ N} = 3.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by number of bottoms.**

$$
D = 3{,}000 \text{ N} \times 3 = 9{,}000 \text{ N} = 9.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Speed correction (ASAE enhancement).**

At higher speeds, draft increases. The empirical speed factor is $(1 + 0.1 V)$ where $V$ is in km/h:

$$
D_{\text{corrected}} = D \cdot (1 + 0.1 \cdot V) = 9{,}000 \times (1 + 0.5) = 13{,}500 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute required PTO power.**

$$
P = \dfrac{D_{\text{corrected}} \cdot V}{3.6 \times 1000} \text{ kW}
$$

(where $V$ in km/h is converted to m/s by dividing by 3.6):

$$
P = \dfrac{13{,}500 \text{ N} \times (5/3.6) \text{ m/s}}{1000} = \dfrac{13{,}500 \times 1.389}{1000} = 18.75 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Calculation Pattern:** Always show units, always convert to SI, and never skip the speed-correction step — examiners award 2 marks for it consistently.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Side-Draft Angle of a Mould Board Plough

**Given:** Horizontal force $R_x = 8.0$ kN, Side force $R_z = 2.4$ kN.

**Find:** Side-draft angle $\delta$.

**Step 1 — Free body concept.**

When the plough is not perfectly aligned with the tractor's centre line (or the bottom has a finite hitch offset), a component $R_z$ acts perpendicular to the direction of motion. This component tries to swing the plough off-line. The **hitch point** is positioned to create an equal and opposite pull, keeping the plough on course.

**Step 2 — Apply trigonometry.**

The side-draft angle is defined as:

$$
\tan\delta = \dfrac{R_z}{R_x}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$
\tan\delta = \dfrac{2.4}{8.0} = 0.30
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\delta$.**

$$
\delta = \tan^{-1}(0.30) = 16.7^{\circ}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

A side-draft angle of $16.7°$ is significant. It means the operator must steer the tractor slightly to compensate, causing operator fatigue. Mitigation: use a **longer hitch** (increases $R_x$ component at the hitch) or use a **trailing / mounted plough with proper lateral adjustment**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cultivator Tine Draft (Standard Earth-Moving Equation)

**Given:** A rigid cultivator tine, depth $d = 0.10$ m, width $w = 0.05$ m, soil specific weight $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³, soil-tool friction angle $\theta = 25°$, soil-metal adhesion $C_a = 5$ kPa, soil-texture factor $F = 1.2$, soil-metal friction angle $\delta = 0.6 \phi$ with $\phi = 30°$.

**Find:** Draft per tine $D_t$.

**Step 1 — Write the standard equation.**

$$
D_t = F \left( \gamma \cdot w \cdot d^2 \cdot \sin\theta + C_a \cdot w \cdot d \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sin\theta$.**

$$
\sin(25°) = 0.4226
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute all values.**

$$
D_t = 1.2 \times \left[ 17{,}000 \times 0.05 \times (0.10)^2 \times 0.4226 + 5{,}000 \times 0.05 \times 0.10 \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Compute first term (gravity/density component).**

$$
17{,}000 \times 0.05 \times 0.01 \times 0.4226 = 17{,}000 \times 2.113 \times 10^{-4} = 3.59 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute second term (cohesion/adhesion component).**

$$
5{,}000 \times 0.05 \times 0.10 = 25 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Sum and apply $F$.**

$$
D_t = 1.2 \times (3.59 + 25) = 1.2 \times 28.59 = 34.31 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation.**

A single tine draws only ~34 N. For a 13-tine cultivator (typical):

$$
D_{\text{total}} = 13 \times 34.31 = 446 \text{ N} \approx 0.45 \text{ kN}
$$

This is why cultivators are also called **low-draft implements** — they consume 5–10 times less power than a 3-bottom MBP.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU)

| Parameter | Mould Board Plough | Disc Harrow | Cultivator (Rigid Tine) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Function | Primary tillage, inversion | Secondary, clod breaking | Secondary, weeding, aeration |
| Soil inversion angle | $\sim 180°$ | $\sim 45°\text{–}90°$ | $0°$ (no inversion) |
| Typical depth | 150–300 mm | 75–150 mm | 50–150 mm |
| Specific resistance $k$ | 40–80 kPa | 25–50 kPa | 20–40 kPa |
| Operating speed | 4–7 km/h | 6–10 km/h | 5–9 km/h |
| Typical draft/bottom or per unit width | 3.0 kN/bottom | 1.5 kN/m width | 0.3 kN/tine |
| Critical design parameter | Mould board curvature, suction, landside reaction | Gang angle, disc concavity, tilt angle | Tine geometry, rake angle, sweep width |
| Power requirement (3 m width) | 30–50 kW | 15–25 kW | 10–20 kW |
