# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** With a neat sketch, list the main parts of a mould board plough bottom and state the function of each.

**Model Answer:**

A plough bottom consists of the following parts:

1. **Share (a)** — A sharp, replaceable steel blade that severs the furrow slice horizontally from the unploughed land. It is the primary soil-cutting element.
2. **Mould Board (b)** — A curved steel plate that receives the cut slice, lifts it, inverts it through ~180°, and pulverises it by controlled bending.
3. **Landside (c)** — A flat plate fitted on the rear of the frog, sliding against the furrow wall, absorbing the side-thrust ($R_z$) and preventing the plough from swinging off-line.
4. **Frog (d)** — The cast/welded structural housing to which the share, mould board, and landside are bolted; it transfers all forces to the plough beam.

*(Award: 2 marks for listing 4 parts with one-line functions, 1 mark for sketch.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between a disc harrow and a cultivator. State one specific application of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Disc Harrow | Cultivator |
|---|---|---|
| **Soil Action** | Rolling discs cut, lift and pulverise clods; partial inversion | Tines/sweeps loosen and stir soil; no inversion |
| **Working Depth** | 75–150 mm (shallow) | 50–150 mm (inter-row) |
| **Soil Condition** | Heavy, dry, trashy fields | Row-crop fields for weed control |
| **Application** | Secondary tillage after ploughing to break clods and mix fertiliser | Inter-row weeding in standing row crops like maize, sugarcane, cotton |

*(Award: 1 mark each for 2 differences, 1 mark for applications.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**A (a)** (7 Marks) Explain the three types of mould board surfaces with suitable sketches. Discuss their suitability for different soil conditions.

**A (b)** (7 Marks) A 3-bottom mould board plough is operated at a depth of 0.22 m and width of 0.30 m per bottom in a soil of specific resistance 55 kPa. Compute the (i) draft per bottom, (ii) total draft, and (iii) drawbar power at a forward speed of 6 km/h. Use the empirical speed factor $(1 + 0.1V)$ where $V$ is in km/h.

---

#### Model Solution to A (a)

The three principal types of mould board surfaces are:

1. **Cylindrical Mould Board** — The surface is generated by a circular arc. The radius of curvature is large and nearly constant from the share to the wing of the mould board. The slice is lifted and twisted progressively but **not fully inverted**; it rests against the previously turned furrow.
   - *Suited for*: **Sandy, loamy, friable soils** where excessive pulverisation would be undesirable.

2. **Helicoidal / General-Purpose Mould Board** — The surface is generated by a variable-radius helix. The curvature is gentle near the share and steepens towards the wing. The slice is **lifted, twisted, and inverted almost completely**.
   - *Suited for*: **All soil types**; widely used in general farming. Examples: Kverneland, S.B.O., Bottom-15.

3. **Stump Jump / Breaker Mould Board** — Short, abruptly curved, with a small top surface. The slice is **broken (pulverised) violently** rather than smoothly inverted.
   - *Suited for*: **Heavy, sticky, wet, or virgin soils**; produces excellent pulverisation at the cost of higher draft.

**Comparison table for 3 additional marks:**

| Feature | Cylindrical | Helicoidal | Stump Jump |
|---|---|---|---|
| Curvature | Constant radius | Variable, spiral | Abrupt, short |
| Inversion | Partial | Complete | Broken, not inverted |
| Pulverisation | Low | Moderate | High |
| Draft | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| Soil best suited | Sandy/loam | General | Heavy/sticky |

*(Award: 2 marks each for the three types with sketch/description, 1 mark for the comparison/suitability.)*

---

#### Model Solution to A (b)

**Given:**
- Number of bottoms $n = 3$
- Furrow depth $d = 0.22$ m
- Furrow width per bottom $w = 0.30$ m
- Specific resistance $k = 55$ kPa $= 55{,}000$ N/m²
- Forward speed $V = 6$ km/h

**Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional area per furrow.**

$$
A = w \cdot d = 0.30 \times 0.22 = 0.066 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute draft per bottom (without speed correction).**

$$
D_{\text{per bottom}} = k \cdot A = 55{,}000 \times 0.066 = 3{,}630 \text{ N} = 3.63 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Total draft for 3 bottoms (without speed correction).**

$$
D_{\text{total}} = 3{,}630 \times 3 = 10{,}890 \text{ N} = 10.89 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply empirical speed correction.**

$$
D_{\text{corrected}} = D_{\text{total}} \cdot (1 + 0.1 \cdot V) = 10{,}890 \times (1 + 0.6) = 10{,}890 \times 1.6 = 17{,}424 \text{ N} \approx 17.42 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert speed to SI.**

$$
V = 6 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{6}{3.6} = 1.667 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute drawbar power.**

$$
P = D_{\text{corrected}} \times V = 17{,}424 \times 1.667 = 29{,}046 \text{ W} \approx 29.05 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Draft per bottom = **3.63 kN**
- (ii) Total draft (corrected) = **17.42 kN**
- (iii) Drawbar power = **29.05 kW**

*(Award: 2 marks for steps 1–2, 2 marks for step 3, 2 marks for step 4, 1 mark for step 5–6.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**B (a)** (7 Marks) Describe with a sketch the working principle of a disc harrow. Explain the role of **gang angle** and **tilt angle** in disc harrow operation.

**B (b)** (7 Marks) A 13-tine rigid cultivator is used at 0.10 m depth, each tine cutting 0.05 m width. Soil specific weight = 17 kN/m³, soil-tool friction angle $\theta = 25°$, adhesion $C_a = 5$ kPa, soil-texture factor $F = 1.2$. Compute the total draft of the cultivator.

---

#### Model Solution to B (a)

A **disc harrow** consists of a series of **concave steel discs** (diameter 460–660 mm) mounted on a common **gang shaft**. The gang is bolted to the harrow frame, and the gang axis makes an angle $\alpha$ (gang angle) with the direction of travel, while the disc plane makes an angle $\beta$ (tilt/disc angle) with the vertical.

**Working Principle:**
As the implement is pulled forward, the discs **rotate** due to soil reaction, and each disc acts as a **rolling wedge** that cuts, lifts, pulverises, and partially mixes the soil. **Scrapers** are provided behind each disc to remove sticky soil and prevent clogging.

**Role of Gang Angle ($\alpha$):**
- $\alpha = 0°$: discs only penetrate; no soil throw.
- $\alpha \approx 17°\text{–}22°$: optimum — good penetration, soil mixing, and pulverisation.
- $\alpha > 25°$: excessive side-thrust and wheel slip; impractical.

**Role of Tilt Angle ($\beta$):**
- $\beta = 0°$: no penetration (disc rolls on surface).
- $\beta \approx 15°\text{–}25°$: optimum — disc edge bites into the soil.
- $\beta > 30°$: excessive penetration, deep cutting, high draft.

**Relationship:** Both angles must be tuned together. Increasing $\alpha$ increases pulverisation; increasing $\beta$ increases penetration.

*(Award: 2 marks for sketch, 2 marks for working principle, 1.5 marks each for gang and tilt angle discussion.)*

---

#### Model Solution to B (b)

**Given:**
- Number of tines $n = 13$
- Depth $d = 0.10$ m
- Width per tine $w = 0.05$ m
- $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³ $= 17{,}000$ N/m³
- $\theta = 25°$, $\sin\theta = 0.4226$
- $C_a = 5$ kPa $= 5{,}000$ N/m²
- $F = 1.2$

**Step 1 — Standard earth-moving equation per tine.**

$$
D_t = F \left( \gamma \cdot w \cdot d^2 \cdot \sin\theta + C_a \cdot w \cdot d \right)
$$

**Step 2 — First term.**

$$
\gamma \cdot w \cdot d^2 \cdot \sin\theta = 17{,}000 \times 0.05 \times 0.01 \times 0.4226 = 3.59 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Second term.**

$$
C_a \cdot w \cdot d = 5{,}000 \times 0.05 \times 0.10 = 25 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum and apply $F$.**

$$
D_t = 1.2 \times (3.59 + 25) = 1.2 \times 28.59 = 34.31 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Total draft.**

$$
D_{\text{total}} = n \cdot D_t = 13 \times 34.31 = 446 \text{ N} \approx 0.45 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final Answer:** Total cultivator draft = **0.446 kN (≈ 446 N)**.

*(Award: 2 marks for equation, 2 marks for term-by-term substitution, 1 mark for multiplication by $F$, 1 mark for total draft, 1 mark for correct unit.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversions** — forgetting to convert kPa → N/m², or km/h → m/s. (Loss: 1–2 marks per question)
> 2. **Skipping the speed correction** $(1 + 0.1V)$ in MBP draft problems. (Loss: 2 marks consistently)
> 3. **Confusing gang angle and tilt angle** — write the *same* role for both and lose 3 marks. Gang angle → soil throw/pulverisation; Tilt angle → penetration.
> 4. **Wrong number of tines multiplier** — using the number of sweeps instead of tines, or vice versa.
> 5. **Omitting sketches** in 7-mark questions — you lose at least 2 marks even with a perfect answer.
> 6. **Power conversion** — write drawbar power in **W** instead of **kW** and forget the /1000. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 7. **Misidentifying soil-metal friction angle vs soil internal friction angle** in the standard earth-moving equation. (Loss: 2 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Pin This to Your Study Wall:**

- **MBP Functions**: Cut → Lift → Invert → Pulverise (CLIP).
- **Four parts of a plough bottom**: Share, Mould Board, Landside, Frog.
- **Three mould board surfaces**: Cylindrical (sandy), Helicoidal (general), Stump Jump (heavy).
- **Three forces on plough bottom**: $R_x$ (resist), $R_y$ (vertical), $R_z$ (side, balanced by landside).
- **Side-draft angle**: $\tan\delta = R_z / R_x$.
- **Draft formula (MBP)**: $D = k \cdot w \cdot d \cdot n$ (basic); $D_{\text{corrected}} = D \cdot (1 + 0.1V)$ with $V$ in km/h.
- **Drawbar power**: $P = D \cdot V$ in SI units → divide by 1000 for kW.
- **Disc Harrow key angles**: Gang angle $\alpha$ (0°–25°, controls soil throw), Tilt angle $\beta$ (15°–25°, controls penetration).
- **Disc harrow effective width**: $W_e = W_n \cdot \cos\alpha$.
- **Cultivator draft** (standard earth-moving): $D_t = F \left( \gamma w d^2 \sin\theta + C_a w d \right)$.
- **Cultivator draft is much lower** than MBP (~5–10×) because of smaller depth and absence of inversion.
- **Tine spacing on cultivator**: 200–300 mm typical; bar-to-bar distance ~500 mm.
- **Soil inversion sequence**: Disc Plough (~45–90°) < MBP (~180°); Cultivator/Harrow (0°).
- **Standard disc diameter**: 460, 510, 560, 610, 660 mm (common = 560 mm).
- **Ploughing speed range**: 4–7 km/h for MBP; 6–10 km/h for disc harrow.
- **Specific resistance $k$**: 20–80 kPa (sandy low, clay high; typical loam = 40–50 kPa).
- **Land side function**: Absorbs $R_z$ (side thrust) — *never* the share or mould board.
- **Scrapers** in disc harrow → prevent soil adhesion to disc concave surface.
- **Frog** in MBP → structural backbone mounting share, board, landside; takes all forces.
- **Field capacity formula**: $C = W \cdot V / 10$ ha/h.
- **Field efficiency**: ratio of theoretical to actual time, typically 70–85%.
- **Sweep/Duck-foot** is for shallow full-width weed cutting (no furrow formed).
- **Shovel/Point** is a narrow, deep-penetrating tool for in-row loosening.
- **Spring tine** auto-deflects around stones, ensuring safety in rocky soils.
- **Rigid tine** is cheaper, used in stone-free, light soils.
- **Rake angle** of tine (15–25°) is critical: too low → poor penetration; too high → high draft.
- **Reversible shovel/point** doubles life — flip the worn side down.
- **Three-point hitch** is the standard tractor-implement interface for mounted MBP and cultivators.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Forces Acting on Plough Bottom by the Cut Soil Slices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBAGT604 - DESIGN OF AGRICULTURAL IMPLEMENTS & MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Analysis of Forces Acting on Plough Bottom by the Cut Soil Slices ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Force Analysis on Plough Bottom (KTU 2024 Definition)** > The **analysis of forces acting on a plough bottom by the cut soil slices** is the static and dynamic equilibrium study of all external and internal force vectors — including soil reaction, friction, cohesion, inertia, and gravity — that act upon a soil slice as it is sheared, lifted, inverted, and translated across the working surface of a moldboard plough. This analysis establishes the magnitude and direction of the **Draft Force ($D$)**, **Vertical Suction Force ($P_v$)**, and **Side Force ($P_s$)**, which collectively govern the design, material selection, and energy requirement of tillage implements. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, this topic is categorized under **Module 3 – Soil-Machine Interaction & Draft Analysis**, and is directly mapped to **Course Outcome CO3** (*Analyze the force system acting on agricultural implements to compute draft and energy requirements*). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sled on a Snow Slope" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Real-World Analogy** > Imagine pushing a heavy wooden sled up a curved, polished ice slope. The ice pushes back on the sled (a *normal reaction*), the rough surface drags the sled backward (a *friction force*), and you must apply a horizontal pull (*draft force*) plus push down or lift slightly (*vertical force*) to keep the sled following the curved path. Add a layer of sticky snow that adheres to the surface (a *cohesion force*), and the sled's own tendency to keep sliding forward even when you stop pushing (*inertia*). A soil slice sliding over a plow bottom behaves **identically** — the only difference is that the "ice" is the polished steel moldboard and the "snow" is moist, cohesive agricultural soil. ### 1.3 Physical Parameters and Standard Metrics The following standard metrics are used in the KTU-2024 board evaluation scheme: | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Range | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Coefficient of friction (soil–steel) | $\mu$ | **0.30 – 0.60** | dimensionless | | Friction angle | $\phi$ | **15° – 35°** | degrees | | Soil bulk density | $\rho_b$ | **1200 – 1800** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | $\text{m/s}^2$ | | Soil-metal adhesion coefficient | $C_a$ | **1.0 – 5.0** | kPa | | Specific draft resistance | $K$ | **20 – 80** | kPa | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always state units explicitly. A 2-mark deduction is routinely imposed for missing units in numerical answers involving draft force ($D$ in N or kN). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 System Identification — Forces on a Soil Slice When the plow share shears a thin slice of soil from the furrow bottom, that slice is transported up, across, and inverted over the **moldboard**. During transit, **six (6) primary force components** act on the slice. The KTU board examiner expects every student to enumerate and resolve these forces in 3-D. **The Six Primary Forces:** 1. **Weight of the soil slice ($W$)** — acts vertically downward through the slice's center of gravity. 2. **Soil Reaction Force ($R$)** — acts normal (perpendicular) to the local moldboard surface at the point of contact. 3. **Friction Force ($F$)** — acts tangentially along the moldboard surface, opposing the slice's relative motion. 4. **Cohesion / Adhesion Force ($C_a$)** — acts tangentially, representing soil-to-metal bonding. 5. **Inertia Force ($F_i$)** — pseudo-force due to acceleration of the slice as it follows the curved moldboard. 6. **Applied External Force (Reaction at share)** — the reaction from the share point, often resolved into draft, vertical, and side components. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow - **Step 1 — Identify the soil slice geometry:** Cross-section is typically rectangular, defined by **depth** ($a$) and **width** ($b$). - **Step 2 — Determine the velocity vector** of the slice relative to the plow at each point along the moldboard trajectory. - **Step 3 — Resolve all forces** into the **three principal axes**: longitudinal ($x$), vertical ($y$), lateral ($z$). - **Step 4 — Apply equilibrium equations** $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum F_z = 0$. - **Step 5 — Compute the resultant draft force** by combining the longitudinal components. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this analysis matters (Engineering Utility):** > - It determines the **horsepower requirement** of the tractor. > - It governs the **structural design** of the plow beam (to prevent bending failure). > - It influences the **material selection** for moldboard (high-grade steel vs. cast iron). > - It is the foundation for **ASABE Standard S313.3** draft prediction models used globally. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table consolidates every equation you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE: | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W = \rho_b \cdot a \cdot b \cdot L \cdot g$ | Weight of soil slice | N | | 2 | $F = \mu \cdot R_n$ | Friction force on moldboard | N | | 3 | $R = \sqrt{R_n^2 + F^2}$ | Resultant surface force | N | | 4 | $D = R \cos(\alpha + \phi) + W \sin\alpha$ | Draft (horizontal pull) | N | | 5 | $P_v = R \sin(\alpha + \phi) - W \cos\alpha$ | Vertical suction force | N | | 6 | $P_s = R \sin\beta$ | Side (lateral) force | N | | 7 | $F_i = m \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{r}$ | Centripetal/inertia force | N | | 8 | $C_a = c_a \cdot A_{contact}$ | Soil-metal adhesion | N | | 9 | $D_{specific} = K \cdot a \cdot b$ | Empirical draft equation | N | | 10 | $R_n = \dfrac{W \cos\alpha - F_i \sin\alpha}{\sin(\alpha + \phi)}$ | Normal reaction | N | **Where:** - $\alpha$ = **angle of approach** of soil slice with respect to horizontal - $\phi$ = **angle of soil–metal friction** ($\tan\phi = \mu$) - $\beta$ = **angle of moldboard twist** - $L$ = length of soil slice along direction of travel - $r$ = radius of curvature of moldboard at point of contact > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention (KTU Board Rule):** Forces acting in the +$x$ (forward) direction are **positive**. Vertical downward is **negative $y$**. Lateral force pointing toward the unploughed land is **positive $z$**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Draft Force Equation We consider an elementary soil slice of mass $m$ moving up the moldboard. Let the local moldboard surface make an angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal. The slice experiences four forces in the plane of motion: weight $W$, normal reaction $R_n$, friction $F = \mu R_n$, and adhesion $C_a$. We ignore inertia initially. **Step 1 — Resolve the Weight into Components** The weight $W$ acts vertically downward. Its components along and perpendicular to the moldboard surface are: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\parallel} &= W \sin\alpha \quad \text{(along the surface, pulling the slice down the moldboard)} \\ W_{\perp} &= W \cos\alpha \quad \text{(pressing the slice into the moldboard)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Write Perpendicular Equilibrium (Normal Direction)** The slice does not accelerate into or away from the surface, so: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum F_n &= 0 \\ R_n &= W \cos\alpha \end{aligned} $$ This states that the normal reaction exactly balances the perpendicular component of weight. **Step 3 — Write Tangential Equilibrium (Along the Surface)** The slice moves **up** the moldboard. Friction and adhesion act **down** the moldboard (opposing motion). The applied draft force $D$ projected along the surface provides the driving component. Resolving the draft force $D$ along the surface (acting at angle $\alpha$ to horizontal): $$ \begin{aligned} D_{\parallel} &= D \cos\alpha \\ \sum F_t &= 0 \\ D \cos\alpha &= W \sin\alpha + F + C_a \\ D \cos\alpha &= W \sin\alpha + \mu R_n + c_a A_c \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute $R_n$ from Step 2** $$ \begin{aligned} D \cos\alpha &= W \sin\alpha + \mu W \cos\alpha + c_a A_c \\ D \cos\alpha &= W (\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha) + c_a A_c \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Express $\mu$ as $\tan\phi$ to Obtain the Standard Form** $$ \begin{aligned} D \cos\alpha &= W \sin\alpha + W \tan\phi \cos\alpha + c_a A_c \\ D \cos\alpha &= W \sin\alpha + W \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos\phi} \cos\alpha + c_a A_c \\ D \cos\alpha &= \dfrac{W (\sin\alpha \cos\phi + \cos\alpha \sin\phi)}{\cos\phi} + c_a A_c \\ D \cos\alpha &= \dfrac{W \sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos\phi} + c_a A_c \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Solve for $D$** $$ \begin{aligned} D &= \dfrac{W \sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi} + \dfrac{c_a A_c}{\cos\alpha} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Standard KTU Form (with adhesion neglected for simplicity):** $$ \boxed{\,D \;=\; W \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** As the angle of approach $\alpha$ increases (steeper moldboard), the draft rises sharply. As the soil becomes more abrasive (higher $\phi$), draft also increases. This is why well-designed moldboards use $\alpha$ in the range **25° – 45°**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation Including Inertia Force When the slice follows a curved path of radius $r$ at velocity $v$, a centripetal acceleration $\dfrac{v^2}{r}$ is produced, requiring a horizontal centripetal force: $$ \begin{aligned} F_i &= m \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{r} = \dfrac{W}{g} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{r} \end{aligned} $$ Modifying the normal equilibrium from Step 2: $$ \begin{aligned} R_n - W \cos\alpha + F_i \sin\alpha &= 0 \\ R_n &= W \cos\alpha - F_i \sin\alpha \end{aligned} $$ The modified draft expression becomes: $$ \begin{aligned} D &= \dfrac{\sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi}\left[W \cos\alpha - F_i \sin\alpha\right] + \dfrac{c_a A_c}{\cos\alpha} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Exam Shortcut (KTU):** If the problem states "neglect inertia and adhesion," the expression collapses directly to $D = R \cos(\alpha + \phi) + W\sin\alpha$ as listed in the formula sheet. --- ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style) > **Given:** A moldboard plough has depth $a = 0.15$ m, width $b = 0.30$ m, slice length $L = 0.50$ m. Soil bulk density $\rho_b = 1500\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.40$, $\alpha = 30°$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. Adhesion is negligible. > > **Find:** (a) Weight of soil slice (b) Draft force (c) Vertical force on plow **Solution (a) — Weight:** $$ \begin{aligned} W &= \rho_b \cdot a \cdot b \cdot L \cdot g \\ &= 1500 \times 0.15 \times 0.30 \times 0.50 \times 9.81 \\ &= 331.1\ \text{N} \end{aligned} $$ **Solution (b) — Draft Force:** First compute $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu) = \tan^{-1}(0.40) = 21.8°$ Then: $$ \begin{aligned} D &= W \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi} \\ &= 331.1 \times \dfrac{\sin(30° + 21.8°)}{\cos 30° \cos 21.8°} \\ &= 331.1 \times \dfrac{\sin 51.8°}{0.8660 \times 0.9285} \\ &= 331.1 \times \dfrac{0.786}{0.804} \\ &= 323.7\ \text{N} \end{aligned} $$ **Solution (c) — Vertical Force:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_v &= R \sin(\alpha + \phi) - W \cos\alpha \\ \text{where } R &= \dfrac{W \cos\alpha}{\cos\phi} = \dfrac{331.1 \times 0.8660}{0.9285} = 308.7\ \text{N} \\ P_v &= 308.7 \times \sin 51.8° - 331.1 \times \cos 30° \\ &= 308.7 \times 0.786 - 331.1 \times 0.866 \\ &= 242.6 - 286.7 \\ &= -44.1\ \text{N} \end{aligned} $$ The **negative sign** indicates a downward vertical force of **44.1 N** — meaning the soil slice tends to push the plow **down into the ground**, which is the desired behavior for penetration. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Free Body Diagram — Soil Slice on Moldboard (Mermaid Block Schematic) Since physical hand-drawn diagrams cannot be rendered in plain text, the following Mermaid architecture block diagrams the **force vector flow** on a soil slice traversing a curved moldboard: ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Soil Slice
Mass m, Weight W] --> B[Contact Point
on Moldboard] B --> C{Normal Reaction Rn} B --> D{Friction F = mu times Rn} B --> E{Cohesion Ca} B --> F{Inertia Force Fi} C --> G[Resultant R
along R vector] D --> G E --> G F --> G G --> H[Resolve into Axes] H --> I[Horizontal Draft D] H --> J[Vertical Force Pv] H --> K[Side Force Ps] I --> L[Tractor Drawbar Pull] J --> M[Penetration or Lifting] K --> N[Lateral Stability] ``` ### 4.2 Force-Resolution Architecture (3-D Vector Decomposition) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Three_D_Resolution R0[Resultant Soil Reaction R] --> Rx[X Component: Draft D] R0 --> Ry[Y Component: Vertical Pv] R0 --> Rz[Z Component: Side Ps] end subgraph Contributing_Sources S1[Weight W] --> Rx S1 --> Ry S2[Friction F] --> Rx S2 --> Ry S3[Inertia Fi] --> Rx S3 --> Ry S4[Adhesion Ca] --> Rx S5[Moldboard Twist Angle beta] --> Rz end Rx --> Out1[Forward Pull on Tractor] Ry --> Out2[Upward or Downward Push] Rz --> Out3[Lateral Drift Force] ``` ### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Plow Bottom Force System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Input_Parameters P1[Depth a] P2[Width b] P3[Velocity v] P4[Soil Properties
mu, rho b, c a] P5[Moldboard Geometry
alpha, beta, r] end subgraph Force_Computation_Engine P1 & P2 & P3 & P4 --> C1[Compute Slice Weight W] P4 & P5 --> C2[Compute Friction F] P4 & P5 --> C3[Compute Adhesion Ca] P3 & P5 --> C4[Compute Inertia Fi] C1 & C2 & C3 & C4 --> C5[Apply Equilibrium Equations] end subgraph Output_Forces C5 --> O1[Draft Force D] C5 --> O2[Vertical Force Pv] C5 --> O3[Side Force Ps] O1 & O2 & O3 --> O4[Total Power Requirement kW] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Each box represents a physical quantity or computational step. Arrows denote data/force flow. This Mermaid-based architecture is the closest valid representation of the physical free-body diagram within a plain-text rendering. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define "Draft Force" in the context of a moldboard plough. List the three components of the total force exerted by the soil on the plough bottom.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Draft Force (D):** The horizontal component of the total soil reaction force acting on the plough bottom, measured parallel to the direction of travel, which the tractor must overcome through its drawbar pull. > > The three components of the total soil reaction on the plough bottom are: > 1. **Horizontal Draft Component ($D$)** — acts in the direction of travel. > 2. **Vertical Component ($P_v$)** — acts perpendicular to the ground plane (upward or downward). > 3. **Lateral / Side Component ($P_s$)** — acts sideways, perpendicular to the direction of travel. *(1 mark definition + 1.5 marks for listing + 0.5 for clarity)* --- **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the role of the angle of approach ($\alpha$) and the soil-metal friction angle ($\phi$) in determining the draft force on a plough bottom.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - **Angle of approach ($\alpha$):** It is the angle that the soil slice's trajectory makes with the horizontal as it begins to ride up the moldboard. A larger $\alpha$ increases the draft because the soil must be lifted to a greater height and the surface area of contact increases. > - **Friction angle ($\phi$):** Defined by $\tan\phi = \mu$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction between soil and the steel moldboard. It determines the magnitude of the friction force opposing the slice's motion. > - Combined effect: The draft force is proportional to $\sin(\alpha + \phi)$; hence, **both** parameters must be minimized (within design limits) to reduce draft. *(1 mark each parameter + 1 mark for combined effect)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice) > **Module 3 Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Both questions carry equal marks (7 + 7). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Force Analysis with Inertia* **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** **(a)** With the help of a free-body diagram, derive the expression for the **draft force** acting on a soil slice moving over a moldboard plough bottom. Include the effect of soil–metal friction and weight. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A mouldboard plough operates at a depth of **18 cm** and width of **30 cm**. The soil slice has length **45 cm** along the direction of travel. If the bulk density of the soil is **1700 kg/m³**, the angle of approach is **35°**, the coefficient of soil–metal friction is **0.45**, and the forward speed is **4.5 km/h**, calculate: **(7 marks)** 1. The weight of the soil slice 2. The friction angle 3. The draft force 4. The vertical force component --- **Model Solution to Q-A:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. The examiner expects the following step sequence: 1. (1 mark) Statement of assumptions and free-body diagram verbal description. 2. (2 marks) Resolution of weight $W$ into components $W\sin\alpha$ and $W\cos\alpha$. 3. (1 mark) Perpendicular equilibrium yielding $R_n = W\cos\alpha$. 4. (2 marks) Tangential equilibrium yielding $D\cos\alpha = W\sin\alpha + \mu R_n$. 5. (1 mark) Final boxed answer: $$D = W \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** *Step 1: Weight of the soil slice (1.5 marks)* $$ \begin{aligned} V &= a \times b \times L = 0.18 \times 0.30 \times 0.45 = 0.0243\ \text{m}^3 \\ m &= \rho_b \cdot V = 1700 \times 0.0243 = 41.31\ \text{kg} \\ W &= m \cdot g = 41.31 \times 9.81 = 405.2\ \text{N} \end{aligned} $$ *Step 2: Friction angle (1 mark)* $$ \begin{aligned} \phi &= \tan^{-1}(\mu) = \tan^{-1}(0.45) = 24.23° \end{aligned} $$ *Step 3: Draft force (2.5 marks)* $$ \begin{aligned} D &= 405.2 \times \dfrac{\sin(35° + 24.23°)}{\cos 35° \cdot \cos 24.23°} \\ &= 405.2 \times \dfrac{\sin 59.23°}{0.8192 \times 0.9117} \\ &= 405.2 \times \dfrac{0.859}{0.7469} \\ &= 466.1\ \text{N} \end{aligned} $$ *Step 4: Vertical force (2 marks)* $$ \begin{aligned} R_n &= \dfrac{W \cos\alpha}{\cos\phi} = \dfrac{405.2 \times 0.8192}{0.9117} = 364.1\ \text{N} \\ P_v &= R_n \tan(\alpha + \phi) - W \cos\alpha \quad \text{(equivalent form)} \\ &= 364.1 \times \tan 59.23° - 405.2 \times \cos 35° \\ &= 364.1 \times 1.677 - 405.2 \times 0.8192 \\ &= 610.7 - 331.9 \\ &= 278.8\ \text{N (upward — the slice tends to lift the plow)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A & B):** > - **Most common 2-mark loss:** Forgetting to convert speed (if given in km/h) to m/s before computing inertia. $v = 4.5 \text{ km/h} = 1.25 \text{ m/s}$. > - **Second most common:** Using $\phi$ (friction angle) in degrees inside a calculator set to radians. The final numerical answer will be off by an order of magnitude. > - **Third most common:** Mixing up $R_n$ (normal to surface) and $R$ (resultant). The draft uses $R_n$ inside the formula; do **not** square and add friction prematurely. > - **Critical:** Always show units in every step. Missing unit on **draft** = **−1 mark guaranteed**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice — Empirical Draft Equation* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Explain the **ASABE-specific-draft equation** $D = K \cdot a \cdot b$ and discuss how it differs from the **analytical force-balance approach**. List the limitations of the empirical method. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A tractor-pulled mouldboard plough has the following specifications: width of cut $b = 1.2$ m, depth $a = 0.20$ m, soil specific resistance $K = 45$ kPa, and field efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. If the forward speed is **6 km/h**, calculate: **(7 marks)** 1. The draft force per plough bottom (assuming 3 bottoms) 2. The total drawbar power required 3. The total power at the PTO if tractive efficiency is 0.55 --- **Model Solution to Q-B:** **(a) Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):** 1. (2 marks) The ASABE equation $D = K \cdot a \cdot b$ treats draft as proportional to the **cross-sectional area** of the cut slice, with $K$ as an empirical coefficient incorporating soil type, moisture, speed, and geometry. 2. (2 marks) Differences: Empirical (no force vectors, uses field-measured $K$) vs Analytical (resolves each force, requires $\mu$, $\alpha$, $\phi$, $W$). 3. (1 mark) Limitations: (i) $K$ varies with speed, (ii) doesn't give vertical/side components, (iii) doesn't account for moldboard curvature, (iv) soil-type specific. 4. (2 marks) Real-world usage: Tractor sizing, implement matching, fuel estimation. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** *Step 1: Draft per bottom (1.5 marks)* $$ \begin{aligned} A &= a \times b_{\text{bottom}} = 0.20 \times (1.2/3) = 0.20 \times 0.40 = 0.08\ \text{m}^2 \\ D_{\text{per bottom}} &= K \times A = 45{,}000 \times 0.08 = 3600\ \text{N} = 3.6\ \text{kN} \end{aligned} $$ *Step 2: Total drawbar power (2.5 marks)* $$ \begin{aligned} D_{\text{total}} &= 3 \times 3600 = 10{,}800\ \text{N} \\ v &= 6\ \text{km/h} = 1.667\ \text{m/s} \\ P_{\text{drawbar}} &= D_{\text{total}} \times v = 10{,}800 \times 1.667 = 18{,}003\ \text{W} \approx 18.0\ \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ *Step 3: PTO power (3 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{PTO}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{drawbar}}}{\eta_{\text{tractive}}} = \dfrac{18.0}{0.55} = 32.7\ \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 3)** - ☐ A soil slice is acted upon by **6 primary forces**: $W$, $R_n$, $F$, $C_a$, $F_i$, and the share reaction. - ☐ The **three orthogonal components** of total soil reaction on the plow are: **Draft $D$**, **Vertical $P_v$**, and **Side $P_s$**. - ☐ The governing draft equation (analytical) is: $D = W \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi}$. - ☐ The empirical form is: $D = K \cdot a \cdot b$. - ☐ The friction angle is always computed first: $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$. - ☐ Negative $P_v$ ⇒ **downward** force ⇒ **self-penetration** (desirable). - ☐ Positive $P_v$ ⇒ **upward** force ⇒ plow tends to **rise out of soil** (undesirable). - ☐ Speed must always be in **m/s** for power calculations: $v\ (\text{m/s}) = v\ (\text{km/h}) \times \dfrac{5}{18}$. - ☐ Power = $D \times v$ (drawbar) or $\dfrac{D \times v}{\eta}$ (PTO). - ☐ Coefficient of soil–metal friction $\mu$ for steel on moist soil ≈ **0.30 – 0.60**. - ☐ Side force $P_s$ arises from the moldboard's **twist angle $\beta$**; balanced by landside/hind wheel. - ☐ When inertia is **neglected** (most KTU exam problems), the equation simplifies — read the problem statement carefully. - ☐ Always state **assumptions** explicitly: smooth surface, constant velocity, negligible adhesion (unless given). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Examiner's Mantra:** > *"Define the FBD. Resolve the forces. Apply equilibrium. Compute. State units. Box the final answer."* > Following this five-step mantra will earn you full or near-full marks in any question from this topic. ---

topics.title: Disc Implement Types and Design of Disc for Different Disc Tools

Spacing of Disc in Multi-disc Tools, Force Analysis of Vertical and Inclined Disc Tools, Specific Work of Rotary Tools and Machine and Dynamic Forces, Reliability Criteria in Design and Its Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT604 - DESIGN OF AGRICULTURAL IMPLEMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spacing of Disc in Multi-disc Tools, Force Analysis of Vertical and Inclined Disc Tools, Specific Work of Rotary Tools and Machine and Dynamic Forces, Reliability Criteria in Design and Its Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Spacing of Disc in Multi-disc Tools

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
In multi-disc implements such as **disc ploughs** and **disc harrows**, two or more concave discs are mounted on a single common **gang axle (gang bolt)** at a fixed longitudinal distance called the **disc spacing (S)**. The spacing is the centre-to-centre distance measured along the gang axle between two consecutive discs. It is a critical geometric design parameter because it governs (i) the **width of cut per gang**, (ii) the degree of **soil pulverization**, (iii) the magnitude of **draft**, and (iv) the tendency of the implement to **clog with trash, weeds and crop residues**.

The standard KTU-recommended spacing rules are:
- **Disc plough:** $S = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \times \text{depth of cut}$ (typically **200–250 mm** for normal ploughing depth of 150 mm)
- **Disc harrow (tandem/off-set):** $S = 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5 \times \text{disc diameter}$ (typically **170–220 mm**)
- **One-way disc plough:** spacing equal on each side of the **scrapper position**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The optimum spacing of discs on a gang axle is chosen so that the **soil slice cut by one disc is fully inverted and pulverised** before the next disc engages the same furrow slice. Insufficient spacing causes soil back-flow and clogging; excessive spacing leaves uncut strips (ridging) between furrows.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a row of knives cutting slices off a loaf of bread as it slides forward on a conveyor. If the knives are placed too close together, the slices overlap and the bread gets crushed instead of being cleanly cut. If they are placed too far apart, strips of bread are left uncut between slices. **Disc spacing on a gang axle behaves in exactly the same way** — it must be just right so that each disc cuts a clean, separate furrow slice that the previous disc has already turned over. This is the "right amount of overlap for clean slicing" rule.

### Governing Design Equations (Standard Empirical Rules)

$$
S_{plough} = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0)\,d
$$

$$
S_{harrow} = (0.18 \text{ to } 0.25)\,D
$$

where  
$S$ = disc spacing (m)  
$d$ = depth of cut (m)  
$D$ = nominal disc diameter (m)

The **volume of soil handled per disc per unit time** is:
$$
V_d = S \cdot d \cdot v
$$
where $v$ = forward speed (m s⁻¹).

---

## 1.2 Force Analysis of Vertical and Inclined Disc Tools

### Formal Definition
A **disc plough disc** is a concave circular steel plate set at three angular settings on the gang axle:
- **Disc angle (α):** angle between the disc plane and the direction of travel (typically **42°–47°** for ploughing).
- **Tilt angle (β):** angle between the disc plane and the vertical (typically **15°–25°**).
- **Scraper angle:** the angle the scraper blade makes with the disc face.

A **vertical disc** (β = 0°) cuts a clean vertical furrow wall but throws soil almost entirely sideways.  
An **inclined disc** (β > 0°) is "tilted backwards" so that it both cuts and lifts/turns the furrow slice, providing the desired soil inversion.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Hold a dinner plate vertically and push it forward through sand — it cuts a vertical wall and pushes sand sideways, but doesn't lift it. Now tilt the top of the plate backwards by about 20° and push again — the plate now scoops and throws the sand upward and sideways, just like a snow-plough or a tilted bulldozer blade. The **tilt angle converts a sideways-pushing action into a lifting-and-turning action**, which is the entire reason agricultural discs are tilted.

### Forces Acting on a Disc (Free Body)

The **resultant soil reaction force (R)** on the disc face has three orthogonal components measured in the machine coordinate frame:

- **Horizontal Draft force ($F_h$ or $D_x$):** resists forward motion → *must be overcome by the tractor drawbar pull*.
- **Vertical force ($F_v$ or $D_y$):** acts downward (soil reaction + self-weight) or upward ("soil suction") → *governs depth control*.
- **Side force ($F_s$ or $D_z$):** acts laterally on the disc bearings → *balanced between left and right gangs in tandem implements*.

> [!NOTE]
> **For a vertical disc (β = 0):** $F_v$ is small and acts downward; side force $F_s$ is large.  
> **For an inclined disc (β > 0):** $F_v$ becomes negative (upward, "suction") which *aids* penetration and reduces the need for ballast. Side force reduces, draft slightly increases.

---

## 1.3 Specific Work of Rotary Tools and Machine

### Formal Definition
**Specific work (e)** is the **work done per unit volume of soil disturbed** (or per unit cross-sectional area of cut) by a rotary cutting or soil-engaging tool. It is the rotary equivalent of "specific draft" in tillage mechanics and is the primary **specific resistance** parameter used to size the **Power Take-Off (PTO)** of a tractor driving a rotary tiller, rotavator or rotary harrow.

$$
e = \frac{W}{V} \quad \text{[J m}^{-3}\text{] or [N m}^{-2}\text{]}
$$

where  
$W$ = total work done by the rotor (J)  
$V$ = volume of soil cut (m³) = cross-sectional area × length of cut

Typical values for rotary tillage:
- **Dry soil:** $e = 40\,000 \text{ to } 80\,000 \text{ N m}^{-2}$
- **Moist loam:** $e = 25\,000 \text{ to } 50\,000 \text{ N m}^{-2}$
- **Sod/turf:** $e = 60\,000 \text{ to } 100\,000 \text{ N m}^{-2}$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of "specific work" as the **"fuel cost per kg of vegetables chopped"** by a kitchen food processor. A dull blade chopping hard carrots costs more "work per kg" than a sharp blade chopping soft cucumber. In tillage, a sharp L-shaped blade cutting moist loam costs far less energy per cubic metre than a dull blade chewing through dry hardpan. Specific work is that "energy-per-kg-of-soil-moved" metric.

### Theoretical Basis (Gill–Vanden Berg Model)
For a rotary blade moving with a peripheral speed $u$ through soil of cohesion $c$ and internal friction angle $\phi$:

$$
e \approx c \cdot N_c \;+\; \gamma \, d \, N_q \;+\; 0.5 \, \gamma \, d \, N_\gamma
$$

where $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ are **bearing capacity factors** (functions of $\phi$ and blade geometry), $\gamma$ = soil unit weight, $d$ = depth. The first term dominates in cohesive agricultural soils.

---

## 1.4 Dynamic Forces on Rotary Machines

### Formal Definition
**Dynamic forces** are time-varying loads generated on a rotary tillage rotor because (a) the blades rotate through a cyclic "cut-engage-disengage" cycle, and (b) the soil is not a perfectly homogeneous medium — roots, stones, hard clods and variable moisture cause fluctuating reaction forces. These forces have three principal components:
1. **Steady-state rotary torque reaction** at the rotor shaft.
2. **Cyclic torsional vibration** of the rotor, PTO shaft and tractor driveline.
3. **Vertical and lateral impact spikes** when a blade strikes a stone or root.

The instantaneous power requirement is therefore:

$$
P_{inst}(t) = \tau_{rotor}(t) \cdot \omega
$$

A practical design uses the **mean power plus a fluctuation factor (k)**:

$$
P_{design} = \bar{P} \cdot (1 + k)
$$

where $k$ typically ranges from **0.15 to 0.35** for soft soils and **0.30 to 0.50** for stony/hard soils.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in design:** Sizing the tractor PTO and gear-train using only the *mean* torque leads to frequent clutch slippage, PTO shear-bolt failure, and premature bearing fatigue. KTU design problems therefore require you to compute both $\bar{\tau}$ and the cyclic amplitude.

---

## 1.5 Reliability Criteria in Design and Its Application

### Formal Definition
**Reliability (R)** is the probability that a machine, component or system will perform its intended function **without failure** under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time.

For agricultural machinery the standard reliability function is the **exponential (constant-hazard) model**:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

where  
$\lambda$ = constant **failure rate** (failures per hour)  
$t$ = mission time (hours)  
**Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** = $1/\lambda$  
**Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** = $1/\lambda$ for non-repairable items.

For a system with $n$ independent components in **series**, the system reliability is the product:

$$
R_{system} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)
$$

For **parallel (redundant)** sub-systems:
$$
R_{system} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Emphasis (2024 Scheme):** Reliability is now treated as a design parameter, not just a post-sale quality metric. Students must know how to compute required reliability of components given an overall machine reliability target (a common numerical problem in ESE).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine buying a cheap electric iron. After 100 uses the thermostat fails; after 300 uses the cord breaks. Each component has its own "lifetime probability curve." The reliability of the *whole iron* is limited by its weakest link — just like a chain. If you want the iron to last 500 uses with 95 % probability, you cannot use a thermostat with 90 % reliability over that period — you must upgrade it. Reliability engineering is exactly this kind of **"weakest-link budgeting" applied to components**.

### Application Areas in Agricultural Machinery Design
1. **Selection of bearings** for disc-gang axles (target MTBF > 2000 h).
2. **PTO shaft sizing** using the dynamic torque spectrum (cyclic reliability).
3. **Spring tine fatigue life** under stone-impact loading.
4. **Hydraulic cylinder seal life** on tractor-implement hitches.
5. **Crop-thresher concave-bar fatigue** under continuous cyclic load.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theory of Disc Spacing Selection

1. **Identify implement type and primary function** — plough (soil inversion), harrow (pulverisation), or off-set (weed cutting).
2. **Select the recommended spacing factor (k)** from empirical tables:
   - Plough → $k = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0$
   - Harrow → $k = 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5$ (of depth)
3. **Compute spacing from depth of cut:** $S = k \cdot d$.
4. **Check clearance for trash flow:** Ensure $S \ge 1.5 d$ to avoid clogging in stubble/trashy fields.
5. **Verify gang-angle limit:** For a 5-disc gang of diameter $D$, total width $B = (n-1) S + 2R_{rim}$ should not exceed the tractor's effective drawbar width.
6. **Compute volume per disc:** $V_d = S \cdot d \cdot v$ (used in draft and specific-resistance calculations downstream).

### Why These Steps Matter
- **Step 2** anchors the design in empirical evidence (Kepner, Bainer & Barger; Gill & Vanden Berg).
- **Step 4** is the field-reality check — the most common cause of multi-disc plough failure in rice-straw fields is trash clogging, not bending.
- **Step 6** is the bridge to Module 3's later sub-topics on specific work.

---

## 2.2 Force Analysis — Detailed Component Breakdown

### Free-Body Force Components on a Disc

The soil reaction $R$ on the concave face of the disc can be decomposed as:

$$
\vec{R} = F_x\,\hat{i} + F_y\,\hat{j} + F_z\,\hat{k}
$$

| Component | Symbol | Direction | Cause |
|-----------|--------|-----------|-------|
| **Draft (Horizontal)** | $F_x = D$ | Opposes travel | Soil shear + bearing pressure |
| **Vertical** | $F_y = V_d$ | Down (or up if suction) | Disc self-weight + soil reaction |
| **Side** | $F_z = S_f$ | Lateral on gang axle | Disc-angle-induced side thrust |

### Effect of Tilt Angle (β) on Force Distribution

Empirical results (Gill & Vanden Berg) show that as the tilt angle increases from 0° to 25°:
- **Draft** $F_x$ increases slightly (~ 8–12 % per 5°).
- **Vertical force** $F_y$ decreases and becomes **negative** (upward suction) beyond β ≈ 15°.
- **Side force** $F_z$ decreases substantially.

### Key Analytical Equations (Vertical vs. Inclined)

For a **vertical disc** with disc-angle α, the resultant force in the plane of the disc is:
$$
R = \frac{F_x}{\cos\alpha}
$$
The component perpendicular to the cutting edge (which does the soil cutting work) is:
$$
F_n = F_x \sin\alpha + F_z \cos\alpha
$$

For an **inclined disc** (with tilt β added), the horizontal and vertical components of the soil reaction measured in the machine frame are:

$$
F_{x,incl} = R \cos\alpha \cos\beta
$$

$$
F_{y,incl} = R \sin\beta \quad \text{(downward component)}
$$

$$
F_{z,incl} = R \sin\alpha \cos\beta
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "compare vertical and inclined disc", state that an inclined disc **generates an upward suction force** that assists penetration — this is why all practical disc ploughs use β = 15°–25°, never β = 0°.

### Draft Equation for a Single Disc (Kepner–Bainer–Barger)

A widely used semi-empirical draft formula for a disc:

$$
D_{disc} = K_0 \cdot d \cdot W \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot \cos\beta
$$

where  
$K_0$ = soil resistance coefficient (kN m⁻²)  
$d$ = depth (m)  
$W$ = width of cut per disc = $S$ (m)  
$\alpha$ = disc angle  
$\beta$ = tilt angle

For a gang of $n$ discs, total draft (ignoring gang-angle correction):

$$
D_{gang} \approx n \cdot D_{disc} \cdot \eta_g
$$

where $\eta_g$ = gang efficiency (typically 0.85–0.95) — accounts for mutual soil loosening by adjacent discs.

---

## 2.3 Specific Work of Rotary Tools

### Derivation of Specific Work from First Principles

For a single blade element of width $b$ cutting a slice of thickness $\delta$ at peripheral speed $u$ while the tool advances at forward speed $v$, the cut per revolution is:

$$
L = \frac{2\pi u}{N \cdot z}
$$

where $N$ = rotor rpm, $z$ = number of blades.

Volume cut per revolution:
$$
V_{rev} = b \cdot d \cdot L \cdot z
$$

Power required (ignoring idling losses):
$$
P = T \cdot \omega = e \cdot V_{rev} \cdot N / 60
$$

Hence:
$$
\boxed{\,e = \frac{60 \, T \, \omega}{V_{rev} \, N} = \frac{2 \pi \, T}{V_{rev} \, z}\,}
$$

### Specific Work for Common Soil Conditions

| Soil condition | Specific work $e$ (kN m⁻²) | Cutting tool |
|----------------|------------------------------|--------------|
| Dry, hard clay | 70 – 100 | Slashing blade |
| Moist loam | 25 – 50 | L-blade |
| Sandy loam | 15 – 30 | C-blade |
| Wet paddy | 20 – 35 | Paddy blade |
| Stony / root-infested | 80 – 120 | Reinforced blade |

---

## 2.4 Dynamic Forces — Analytical Treatment

### Centrifugal Force on Each Blade

For a blade of mass $m$ at radius $r$ rotating at angular speed $\omega$:

$$
F_c = m \, \omega^2 \, r
$$

This force is balanced by the rotor-shaft bearing reaction and creates a steady radial load.

### Cyclic Cutting Force

The tangential cutting force on a blade has a fundamental frequency:

$$
f_{cutting} = \frac{N \cdot z}{60} \quad \text{[Hz]}
$$

This frequency must not coincide with any natural frequency of the rotor, frame or tractor-implement system, or **resonance failure** occurs.

### Power-Torque Dynamics

The instantaneous torque at any instant is:
$$
\tau(t) = \tau_{mean} + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \tau_k \sin(k \omega t + \phi_k)
$$

For design sizing, only the mean plus first two harmonics matter. The peak torque (used to select shear-pin or slip-clutch rating) is:
$$
\tau_{peak} = \tau_{mean} + \sqrt{\tau_1^{\,2} + \tau_2^{\,2}}
$$

---

## 2.5 Reliability in Design — Mathematical Treatment

### Exponential (Constant-Hazard) Model

For components operating in their **useful life** (after infant mortality, before wear-out), failure rate is constant and:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \qquad f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \qquad \text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

### Weibull Model (More General)

When failure rate varies with time (wear-out phase), the **Weibull distribution** is used:

$$
R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]
$$

where  
$\beta$ = shape parameter (β < 1 → infant mortality, β = 1 → random, β > 1 → wear-out)  
$\eta$ = scale parameter (characteristic life)

### Series-System Reliability

For $n$ components in **series** (system fails if any component fails):

$$
R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = \exp\!\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i t\right)
$$

### Reliability Allocation (Apportionment)

Given a target system reliability $R_s^*$ and equal weighting, each component's required reliability is:

$$
R_i^* = (R_s^*)^{1/n}
$$

For unequal weighting (AGREE method):

$$
R_i = 1 - \frac{W_i (1 - R_s^*)}{n}
$$

where $W_i$ = weighting factor of $i$-th component (sum of weights = $n$).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol meaning | Units |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | $S = k \cdot d$ | Spacing, depth of cut | m, m |
| 2 | $V_d = S \, d \, v$ | Soil volume per disc | m³ s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $D_{disc} = K_0 \, d \, W \, \sin\alpha \, \cos\beta$ | Single disc draft | N |
| 4 | $D_{gang} = n \, D_{disc} \, \eta_g$ | Total gang draft | N |
| 5 | $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ | Centrifugal force on blade | N |
| 6 | $e = W/V$ | Specific work of rotary tool | J m⁻³ or N m⁻² |
| 7 | $P_{design} = \bar{P}(1+k)$ | Design power with fluctuation | W |
| 8 | $\tau_{peak} = \tau_{mean} + \sqrt{\tau_1^2 + \tau_2^2}$ | Peak torque (cyclic) | N m |
| 9 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability (exponential) | — |
| 10 | $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ | Mean time between failures | h |
| 11 | $R_s = \prod R_i$ | Series-system reliability | — |
| 12 | $R_s = 1 - \prod(1-R_i)$ | Parallel-system reliability | — |
| 13 | $R_i^* = (R_s^*)^{1/n}$ | Equal-reliability allocation | — |
| 14 | $R(t) = \exp[-(t/\eta)^\beta]$ | Weibull reliability | — |
| 15 | $f_{cutting} = N z / 60$ | Cyclic load frequency | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard reference values to memorize for KTU:**  
> $K_0$ (soft soil) ≈ 30 kN m⁻²; (medium) ≈ 60; (hard/stony) ≈ 100.  
> Disc angle α = 42°–47°; Tilt angle β = 15°–25°.  
> Gang efficiency η_g = 0.85–0.95.
