Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flow Diagram — KTU Project Topic Selection Funnel This Mermaid diagram maps the **decision logic** an examiner expects a student to follow when justifying the topic in the Synopsis. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Identify Local Farm Problem] --> B{Is it in Module 4 Domain?} B -- No --> C[Reject - Not aligned to PBAGT604] B -- Yes --> D[List Existing Solutions] D --> E{Is there a clear Improvement Gap?} E -- No --> F[Reject - No Innovation] E -- Yes --> G[Define Design Objective + Metrics] G --> H{Feasibility Check: Cost, Time, Skills} H -- No --> I[Pick Alternative Topic] H -- Yes --> J[Write Synopsis with CO Mapping] J --> K[Guide Approval + Literature Review] K --> L[Detailed Design Calculation] L --> M[CAD Model + GA Drawing] M --> N[Fabrication or Simulation] N --> O[Performance Testing] O --> P[Final Report + Demo] P --> Q[KTU Evaluation: 100 marks] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic Agricultural Implement Project Because Module-4 projects are **mechanical-system** in nature (not purely electronic), this diagram maps the **functional flow of energy & material** through the prototype — exactly what examiners expect in the "System Architecture" slide. ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[Power Source
Motor or PTO] --> node2[Power Transmission
V-Belt or Gearbox] node2 --> node3[Prime Mover
Cylinder or Rotor] node3 --> node4[Working Zone
Threshing or Shelling] node4 --> node5[Separation Zone
Concave or Sieve] node5 --> node6[Cleaning Zone
Fan or Blower] node6 --> node7[Output Collection
Bagging or Chute] node7 --> node8[Performance Test
Capacity, Efficiency, Breakage] node8 --> node9[Data Analysis and Report] node4 -. feedback .-> node2 node6 -. feedback .-> node2 ``` **Explanation of Blocks (read top-left → bottom-right):** - `node1` (Power Source): 1–5 HP motor / tractor PTO. Always start with this. - `node2` (Power Transmission): V-belt + pulleys (cheapest) or worm gearbox (higher torque). - `node3` (Prime Mover): Cylinder, rotor, blade, auger — the heart of the implement. - `node4` (Working Zone): Where crop is acted upon (threshing, shelling, cutting). - `node5` (Separation Zone): Concave / sieve / aspirator. - `node6` (Cleaning Zone): Removes chaff, dust, light grains. - `node7` (Output Collection): Final bagging point. - `node8` (Performance Test): You must measure at least 3 metrics (capacity, efficiency, quality). - `node9` (Data Analysis): Statistical analysis — use MS Excel, plot graphs. > [!NOTE] > The **dotted feedback arrows** from `node4` and `node6` back to `node2` represent the **re-circulation of un-threshed grain / chaff** — a *must-include* feature in any modern thresher/cleaner design. Examiners award extra credit for this design refinement. ## 4.3 Schematic — Topic-to-CO Mapping (Mandatory for KTU Synopsis) ```mermaid flowchart TD T1[Topic 1 Thresher] --> CO1[CO1 Engineering Knowledge] T1 --> CO3[CO3 Design Solutions] T1 --> CO5[CO5 Modern Tool Usage] T2[Topic 6 Cleaner] --> CO2[CO2 Problem Analysis] T2 --> CO3 T2 --> CO5 T3[Topic 16 Solar Dryer] --> CO1 T3 --> CO4[CO4 Environment and Sustainability] T3 --> CO7[CO7 Project Management] T4[Topic 22 IoT Moisture Meter] --> CO1 T4 --> CO5 T4 --> CO6[CO6 The Engineer and Society] T5[Topic 25 Transplanter] --> CO1 T5 --> CO3 T5 --> CO5 T5 --> CO8[CO8 Life-long Learning] ``` > **Take-away:** A single topic can map to **3–5 Course Outcomes**. The stronger the CO coverage, the higher the KTU internal marks in *Project Evaluation Phase I (Synopsis)*. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** **"List any four essential criteria for selecting a valid B.Tech project topic in the domain of agricultural machinery design."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A valid project topic in agricultural machinery design must satisfy: 1. **Local Problem Relevance** — must address a real agricultural issue (labour shortage, post-harvest loss, drudgery). 2. **Engineering Design Component** — must involve design calculations, drawings, and selection of standard components. 3. **Fabrication or Simulation Feasibility** — must be buildable within ₹25,000 and 6 months. 4. **Testability** — must allow measurement of at least 3 performance parameters (e.g., capacity, efficiency, breakage %). *(Optional 5th point for 1 bonus mark: Cost–benefit / economic feasibility analysis.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **"What is the role of Module-4 (Harvesting & Post-Harvest) in defining the project scope for PBAGT604?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. Module-4 governs the **equipment family** from which the project topic is drawn (threshers, shellers, cleaners, dryers, harvesters). 2. It provides the **theoretical base** (cylinder dynamics, sieving, drying kinetics) needed to perform design calculations. 3. It ensures the project is **syllabus-aligned** for KTU's CO3 and CO5 attainment. 4. Topics *outside* Module-4 (e.g., tractor engine design, irrigation pumps) are **rejected** by the project review committee. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Standard KTU Pattern (PBAGT604):** *Either (a) or (b) for 14 marks. Split as 7 + 7 sub-parts.* --- ### **Q3 (a). [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Pattern]** **"As a project coordinator, suggest FIVE suitable design projects for final-year B.Tech students in PBAGT604, specifically aligned to Module-4. For each project, state (i) the local problem addressed, (ii) the key design calculation required, and (iii) the CO mapping. Justify your top recommendation."** #### **Model Solution (14 marks = 5 × 2 + 4 for justification):** **Project 1: Variable-Speed Axial-Flow Paddy Thresher (2 marks)** - Problem: Existing threshers have fixed speeds → 12–15 % grain breakage in moist paddy. - Key Design Calculation: Cylinder peripheral speed $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ and threshing power $P = \dfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^2 K$. - COs mapped: CO1, CO3, CO5. **Project 2: Multi-Crop Cleaner (2 marks)** - Problem: Farmers cannot afford separate cleaners for paddy, pulses, oilseeds. - Key Design Calculation: Sieve area $A = \dfrac{Q}{v \cdot \eta}$ and air-flow $Q_{air} = A \cdot v_{air}$. - COs mapped: CO2, CO3, CO5. **Project 3: Solar-Biomass Hybrid Dryer for Cardamom (2 marks)** - Problem: Kerala's cardamom drying is 90 % smoke-based → health hazard & quality loss. - Key Design Calculation: Heat & mass balance — $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ and drying rate equation. - COs mapped: CO1, CO4, CO7. **Project 4: Ergonomic Redesign of Hand-Held Sowing Tool (2 marks)** - Problem: Women farmers in Wayanad suffer back pain using traditional *Kurpa*. - Key Design Calculation: Lever ratio, torque on wrist based on anthropometric data. - COs mapped: CO3, CO5, CO6. **Project 5: IoT-Based Grain Moisture Meter (2 marks)** - Problem: Mismatched moisture → fungal spoilage of stored grains. - Key Design Calculation: Sensor calibration curve $V = f(MC)$; power budget for battery. - COs mapped: CO1, CO4, CO5, CO6. **Justification of Top Recommendation (4 marks):** **Project 1 (Variable-Speed Thresher)** is the top pick because: - It **directly addresses Kerala's paddy mechanisation** (Kerala produces 0.6 MT paddy / year; ~70 % of threshing is by combined harvesters which damage grain). - It combines **all 5 mandatory ingredients** (problem, design, material, test, cost-benefit). - It uses a **VFD (modern tool — CO5)** which is now affordable (₹ 2,500 for 1 HP). - It is **fabricable in any district KTU workshop** (sheet metal, V-belt, standard pulleys). - It maps to **5 Course Outcomes** (CO1, CO2, CO3, CO5, CO7) — highest mapping strength. --- ### **Q3 (b). [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Pattern, Alternative Choice]** **"For a final-year project on a paddy thresher with cylinder diameter 0.5 m, length 1.0 m, and speed 750 rpm, calculate (i) the peripheral velocity, (ii) the threshing power for a feed rate of 0.5 kg/s, (iii) the total motor power if drive efficiency is 85 %, and (iv) the recommended motor HP with a 25 % service factor. Take impact factor = 1.25."** #### **Model Solution (14 marks = 4 + 4 + 3 + 3):** **Given:** - $D = 0.5 \text{ m},\ L = 1.0 \text{ m},\ N = 750 \text{ rpm},\ \dot{m} = 0.5 \text{ kg/s},\ \eta = 0.85,\ K = 1.25.$ **(i) Peripheral velocity (4 marks):** $$ v = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} $$ $$ v = \frac{\pi \times 0.5 \times 750}{60} $$ $$ v = \frac{1178.10}{60} = 19.63 \text{ m/s} $$ > ✅ *Check: Within recommended 18–24 m/s for paddy.* **(ii) Threshing power (4 marks):** $$ P_{thresh} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \dot{m} \cdot v^2 \cdot K $$ $$ P_{thresh} = 0.5 \times 0.5 \times (19.63)^2 \times 1.25 $$ $$ P_{thresh} = 0.25 \times 385.34 \times 1.25 $$ $$ P_{thresh} = 120.42 \text{ W} \approx 0.120 \text{ kW} $$ **(iii) Motor power (3 marks):** Adding windage (≈ 15 %): $$ P_{shaft} = 1.15 \times 120.42 = 138.48 \text{ W} $$ $$ P_{motor} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta} = \frac{138.48}{0.85} = 162.92 \text{ W} $$ **(iv) Motor HP with service factor (3 marks):** $$ P_{selected} = 1.25 \times 162.92 = 203.65 \text{ W} $$ $$ P_{selected} \text{ in HP} = \frac{203.65}{746} = 0.273 \text{ HP} $$ > **Conclusion:** A **0.5 HP (0.37 kW) standard motor** should be selected (next standard size above 0.273 HP). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common ways students LOSE marks in project-topic questions:** > 1. **Confusing "topic" with "title":** A topic like *"thresher"* is too broad — examiners want a **specific, defined problem**, e.g., *"Design of a low-cost rubber-finger maize sheller for small-scale farmers in Palakkad."* > 2. **Skipping the cost-benefit / economic analysis:** A design without pay-back period is treated as an *academic exercise* and **loses 2–3 marks**. > 3. **Forgetting to map COs:** Every KTU project synopsis **must show a CO mapping table**. Missing it = **−2 marks**. > 4. **Selecting a topic with no fabrication:** *"Design of a harvester using FEA only"* is acceptable ONLY if the student has no fabrication budget; otherwise the synopsis is **rejected** in the first review. > 5. **Using wrong units:** $P = T N / 9550$ requires **N·m and rpm**; students often use N·mm and get 1000× error. > 6. **Copy-paste literature review:** Plagiarism check is mandatory in KTU 2024 — Turnitin score must be **< 20 %**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🎯 12-Point Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Suggestion on Project Topics" — Module 4, PBAGT604** - [ ] **1.** A valid project must address a **local farm problem** identified in Kerala / India. - [ ] **2.** It must contain a **design calculation** — never pure literature review. - [ ] **3.** It must include a **fabricated prototype OR a complete 3D CAD + FEA model**. - [ ] **4.** It must be **tested** — at least 3 performance parameters measured. - [ ] **5.** It must include a **cost–benefit / pay-back analysis** in the report. - [ ] **6.** Module-4 specifically favours topics in: **threshing, shelling, cleaning, drying, harvesting, storage**. - [ ] **7.** The **syllabus formula $P = T N / 9550$** is the most-tested equation for motor selection. - [ ] **8.** The **peripheral velocity equation $v = \pi D N / 60$** must be in every thresher / cutter project. - [ ] **9.** A topic should map to **at least 3 Course Outcomes** (ideally CO1, CO3, CO5). - [ ] **10.** Group size is **3–5 students**; each member must have an *individual* design sub-task. - [ ] **11.** Cost ceiling is **~₹ 25,000 per group** (reimbursable only for in-campus fabrications). - [ ] **12.** Final deliverables: **Hard-bound report + GA drawing + PPT + working demo + video (3 min).** > **Final Tip:** Choose a topic that is **fabricable, testable, locally relevant, and CO-rich**. This single sentence, if followed, guarantees a *Distinction* grade in the KTU project evaluation.
